concepts.name: Mathematical Thinking
concepts.description: The disciplined cognitive approach of reasoning precisely about abstract structures, identifying patterns, and constructing valid arguments from given information.

concepts.name: Abstraction
concepts.description: The process of extracting essential properties of mathematical objects while ignoring irrelevant details, enabling the same reasoning to apply across many contexts.

concepts.name: Pattern Recognition
concepts.description: The ability to identify recurring structures, relationships, or regularities in mathematical objects and use them to predict or generalize.

concepts.name: Mathematical Problem Solving
concepts.description: The structured application of strategies — working backwards, drawing diagrams, reducing to simpler cases, and systematic trial — to resolve novel mathematical questions.

concepts.name: Problem-Solving Strategies
concepts.description: A repertoire of techniques including drawing diagrams, working backwards, finding a simpler related problem, looking for symmetry, and exhaustive case analysis.

concepts.name: Mathematical Modeling
concepts.description: The process of translating a real-world situation into mathematical language, solving the mathematical problem, and interpreting the solution back in context.

concepts.name: Generalization
concepts.description: The act of formulating a statement that holds for a broad class of cases, typically discovered by examining specific instances and identifying invariant structure.

concepts.name: Mathematical Language
concepts.description: The precise symbolic and verbal conventions used to express mathematical ideas unambiguously, including notation, terminology, and the grammar of mathematical writing.

concepts.name: Mathematical Notation
concepts.description: The standardized system of symbols (=, +, ×, ∈, ∑, ∫, etc.) used to represent mathematical objects and operations compactly and unambiguously.

concepts.name: Variable
concepts.description: A symbol representing an unspecified or changing quantity, serving as a placeholder in expressions and equations and enabling the formulation of general mathematical statements.

concepts.name: Mathematical Symbols
concepts.description: The collection of standardized symbols used in mathematics including operational symbols, relational symbols, set symbols, logical connectives, and quantifiers.

concepts.name: Reading Mathematics
concepts.description: The skill of parsing and interpreting formal mathematical statements, definitions, theorems, and proofs accurately.
references: Solow 2014 How to Read and Do Proofs

concepts.name: Mathematical Logic
concepts.description: The branch of mathematics studying the principles of valid reasoning, the structure of mathematical statements, and the formal rules governing deduction.

concepts.name: Proposition
concepts.description: A declarative statement that is either true or false (but not both), forming the basic building block of propositional logic.

concepts.name: Logical Connectives
concepts.description: Operators (¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔) that combine propositions to form compound statements, each with a precise truth-functional meaning.

concepts.name: Truth Table
concepts.description: A tabular method for determining the truth value of a compound proposition for every possible combination of truth values of its component propositions.

concepts.name: Predicate Logic
concepts.description: An extension of propositional logic that introduces predicates, terms, and quantifiers to express statements about objects and their properties.
references: Enderton 2001 A Mathematical Introduction to Logic

concepts.name: Predicate
concepts.description: A statement containing one or more free variables that becomes a proposition when the variables are assigned specific values.

concepts.name: Quantifiers
concepts.description: Logical symbols ∀ (for all) and ∃ (there exists) that bind variables and assert properties about entire domains or the existence of particular elements.

concepts.name: Logical Equivalence
concepts.description: Two compound propositions are logically equivalent if they have identical truth values for every assignment of truth values to their component propositions.

concepts.name: Rules of Inference
concepts.description: Formally valid argument patterns (modus ponens, modus tollens, hypothetical syllogism, etc.) that allow drawing new conclusions from given premises.

concepts.name: Mathematical Proof
concepts.description: A finite sequence of logically valid steps that establishes the truth of a mathematical statement with certainty, starting from axioms or previously proven results.

concepts.name: Direct Proof
concepts.description: A proof that directly derives the conclusion from the hypothesis through a chain of logical deductions, without requiring assumptions of the negation.

concepts.name: Proof by Contradiction
concepts.description: A proof technique that assumes the negation of the statement to be proved and derives a contradiction, thereby establishing the original statement.

concepts.name: Proof by Contrapositive
concepts.description: A proof of P→Q by instead proving ¬Q→¬P, exploiting the logical equivalence of a conditional and its contrapositive.

concepts.name: Mathematical Induction
concepts.description: A proof technique for statements indexed by natural numbers: establish a base case, then show that truth for n implies truth for n+1.

concepts.name: Strong Induction
concepts.description: A variant of induction in which the inductive step may use the truth of the statement for all values up to n, not just n alone.

concepts.name: Well-Ordering Principle
concepts.description: The principle that every nonempty subset of the natural numbers has a least element, equivalent to mathematical induction over ℕ.

concepts.name: Proof by Cases
concepts.description: A proof that partitions all possible situations into finitely many cases and proves the statement separately for each case.

concepts.name: Existence Proof
concepts.description: A proof that at least one object satisfying a given property exists, either by explicitly constructing it or by showing non-existence of its negation leads to contradiction.

concepts.name: Uniqueness Proof
concepts.description: A proof that demonstrates exactly one object satisfies a given property, typically by assuming two such objects exist and showing they must be equal.

concepts.name: Writing Mathematics
concepts.description: The craft of expressing mathematical reasoning clearly, precisely, and in standard notation, balancing rigor with readability.

concepts.name: Axiom
concepts.description: A statement accepted as true without proof, forming the foundational assumptions of a mathematical theory from which all other results are deduced.

concepts.name: Theorem
concepts.description: A mathematical statement that has been rigorously proved to be true from axioms and previously established theorems.

concepts.name: Lemma
concepts.description: A proven statement used as a stepping stone in the proof of a larger theorem, valued for its utility in breaking complex proofs into manageable parts.

concepts.name: Corollary
concepts.description: A result that follows easily from a theorem, often requiring only a brief additional argument or a direct substitution.

concepts.name: Conjecture
concepts.description: A mathematical statement believed to be true on the basis of evidence or intuition but not yet proven or disproven.

concepts.name: Mathematical Definition
concepts.description: A precise, unambiguous specification of the meaning of a mathematical term, introducing new vocabulary by relating it to previously defined concepts.

concepts.name: Axiomatic System
concepts.description: A collection of axioms together with the rules of logic from which all theorems of a mathematical theory are derived.

concepts.name: Set Theory
concepts.description: The mathematical theory of collections of objects (sets), providing the foundational language and framework in which all of modern mathematics is expressed.

concepts.name: Set
concepts.description: A well-defined, unordered collection of distinct objects called elements, the primitive object of set theory.

concepts.name: Set Membership
concepts.description: The fundamental binary relation ∈ asserting that an object x is an element of a set A, written x ∈ A.

concepts.name: Empty Set
concepts.description: The unique set containing no elements, denoted ∅ or {}, a subset of every set.

concepts.name: Set-Builder Notation
concepts.description: A notation {x ∈ A : P(x)} defining a set by specifying a domain and a predicate that its elements must satisfy.

concepts.name: Subset
concepts.description: A set A is a subset of B (A ⊆ B) if every element of A is also an element of B.

concepts.name: Proper Subset
concepts.description: A set A is a proper subset of B if A ⊆ B and A ≠ B, i.e., B contains at least one element not in A.

concepts.name: Set Equality
concepts.description: Two sets A and B are equal (A = B) if and only if they have exactly the same elements: A ⊆ B and B ⊆ A.

concepts.name: Set Operations
concepts.description: The standard operations on sets — union (∪), intersection (∩), difference (\), and complement (Aᶜ) — forming a Boolean algebra.

concepts.name: Union
concepts.description: The union A ∪ B is the set of all elements belonging to A, to B, or to both.

concepts.name: Intersection
concepts.description: The intersection A ∩ B is the set of elements belonging to both A and B.

concepts.name: Set Difference
concepts.description: The set difference A \ B consists of all elements in A that are not in B.

concepts.name: Set Complement
concepts.description: The complement of A relative to a universal set U is the set of all elements of U not in A.

concepts.name: Venn Diagram
concepts.description: A visual representation of sets and their relationships using overlapping circles within a universal rectangle.

concepts.name: Power Set
concepts.description: The power set P(A) is the set of all subsets of A; if A has n elements, then P(A) has 2ⁿ elements.

concepts.name: Cartesian Product
concepts.description: The Cartesian product A × B is the set of all ordered pairs (a, b) where a ∈ A and b ∈ B.

concepts.name: Ordered Pair
concepts.description: An ordered pair (a, b) is a pair of elements where the order matters: (a, b) = (c, d) iff a = c and b = d.

concepts.name: Relation
concepts.description: A relation R from A to B is any subset of A × B; the relation holds between a and b iff (a, b) ∈ R.

concepts.name: Reflexive Relation
concepts.description: A relation R on A is reflexive if (a, a) ∈ R for every a ∈ A.

concepts.name: Symmetric Relation
concepts.description: A relation R on A is symmetric if (a, b) ∈ R implies (b, a) ∈ R for all a, b ∈ A.

concepts.name: Transitive Relation
concepts.description: A relation R on A is transitive if (a, b) ∈ R and (b, c) ∈ R implies (a, c) ∈ R.

concepts.name: Equivalence Relation
concepts.description: A relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, partitioning a set into disjoint equivalence classes.

concepts.name: Equivalence Class
concepts.description: The equivalence class [a] of an element a is the set of all elements related to a under the equivalence relation.

concepts.name: Partition
concepts.description: A partition of a set A is a collection of nonempty, pairwise disjoint subsets of A whose union equals A.

concepts.name: Partial Order
concepts.description: A reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive relation on a set, providing a generalized notion of ordering where not all pairs need be comparable.
references: Davey & Priestley 2002 Introduction to Lattices and Order

concepts.name: Total Order
concepts.description: A partial order in which every pair of elements is comparable: for all a, b, either a ⊴ b or b ⊴ a.

concepts.name: Hasse Diagram
concepts.description: A graphical representation of a finite partially ordered set where directed edges represent immediate predecessor relationships.

concepts.name: Function (Set-Theoretic)
concepts.description: A function f: A → B is a relation where every element of A is related to exactly one element of B, i.e., a right-unique and left-total relation.

concepts.name: Cardinality
concepts.description: The number of elements in a set; for infinite sets, the size is measured via bijections and gives rise to the theory of infinite cardinal numbers.

concepts.name: Finite Set
concepts.description: A set whose elements can be counted using a finite natural number; equivalently, one that is in bijection with {1, 2, …, n} for some n ∈ ℕ.

concepts.name: Countable Set
concepts.description: A set that is either finite or can be placed in bijection with the natural numbers ℕ; examples include ℤ and ℚ.

concepts.name: Uncountable Set
concepts.description: A set whose cardinality is strictly greater than that of ℕ; the real numbers ℝ are uncountable (Cantor's diagonal argument).

concepts.name: Axiomatic Set Theory
concepts.description: The standard axiomatic foundation of mathematics, ZFC, consisting of Zermelo-Fraenkel axioms plus the Axiom of Choice, resolving the paradoxes of naive set theory.

concepts.name: Ordinal Number
concepts.description: A generalization of natural numbers to describe the order type of well-ordered sets, extending counting into the transfinite.

concepts.name: Cardinal Arithmetic
concepts.description: The extension of arithmetic operations to infinite cardinals, including ℵ₀, ℵ₁, and the continuum hypothesis.

concepts.name: Inductive Reasoning
concepts.description: Reasoning from specific observations to general conclusions; in mathematics, this is conjectural and not logically certain (distinguished from mathematical induction, a proof technique).

concepts.name: Deductive Reasoning
concepts.description: Reasoning from general principles to specific conclusions in a logically valid manner; the mode of mathematical proof.

concepts.name: Natural Numbers
concepts.description: The set ℕ = {0, 1, 2, 3, …} (or {1, 2, 3, …}) with its canonical order and arithmetic operations, characterized axiomatically by the Peano axioms.

concepts.name: Integers
concepts.description: The set ℤ = {…, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, …} extending the natural numbers to include additive inverses, forming a ring under addition and multiplication.

concepts.name: Rational Numbers
concepts.description: The set ℚ of all fractions p/q where p ∈ ℤ and q ∈ ℤ\{0}, forming a field and a dense total order without the completeness of ℝ.

concepts.name: Irrational Numbers
concepts.description: Real numbers that cannot be expressed as p/q with p, q ∈ ℤ; their decimal expansions are non-repeating and non-terminating (e.g., √2, π, e).

concepts.name: Real Numbers
concepts.description: The complete ordered field ℝ encompassing all rational and irrational numbers, characterized by the least upper bound (completeness) property.

concepts.name: Complex Numbers
concepts.description: The field ℂ = {a + bi : a, b ∈ ℝ} where i² = −1, completing the real numbers algebraically (Fundamental Theorem of Algebra) and enabling rotation in the plane.

concepts.name: Counting
concepts.description: The process of establishing a bijection between a finite set and an initial segment of the natural numbers to determine its size.

concepts.name: Counting Sequence
concepts.description: The ordered sequence 1, 2, 3, … used to assign a unique natural number to each element of a finite set.

concepts.name: Subitizing
concepts.description: The perceptual ability to instantly recognize the cardinality of a small set (≤4) without counting each element.

concepts.name: Place Value
concepts.description: The positional system in which the value of a digit depends on its position within a numeral; in base 10, each position represents a power of 10.

concepts.name: Ones, Tens, Hundreds
concepts.description: The first three positional columns in base-10 notation, representing 10⁰, 10¹, and 10² respectively.

concepts.name: Expanded Form
concepts.description: Writing a number as the sum of the values of each of its digits multiplied by the corresponding power of 10 (e.g., 345 = 300 + 40 + 5).

concepts.name: Number Base
concepts.description: A positional numeral system using b distinct digit symbols; base 2 (binary), base 8 (octal), and base 16 (hexadecimal) are common in computing.

concepts.name: Addition
concepts.description: The binary operation combining two quantities into their total; the foundation of all arithmetic and the additive structure of every number system.

concepts.name: Carrying (Regrouping)
concepts.description: The algorithm step in multi-digit addition where a digit sum ≥ 10 results in carrying 1 to the next column.

concepts.name: Column Addition
concepts.description: The standard written algorithm for adding multi-digit numbers by aligning place values and adding column by column from right to left.

concepts.name: Mental Addition
concepts.description: Strategies for computing sums in one's head without writing, including making tens, decomposing numbers, and using number bonds.

concepts.name: Subtraction
concepts.description: The inverse operation to addition, computing the difference between two quantities; requires the concept of additive inverse for extension to negative numbers.

concepts.name: Borrowing (Regrouping in Subtraction)
concepts.description: The algorithm step in multi-digit subtraction where a digit is insufficient for the column subtraction, requiring borrowing from the next higher column.

concepts.name: Negative Numbers
concepts.description: Numbers less than zero, arising naturally as additive inverses; their arithmetic requires careful attention to sign rules.

concepts.name: Number Line
concepts.description: A geometric representation of numbers as points on a line, with a designated origin, unit length, and direction, enabling visualization of arithmetic and order.

concepts.name: Ordering Numbers
concepts.description: Determining the relative size of numbers using the relations <, >, ≤, ≥, based on their position on the number line.

concepts.name: Absolute Value
concepts.description: The absolute value |x| of a real number is its distance from zero on the number line: |x| = x if x ≥ 0, and |x| = −x if x < 0.

concepts.name: Integer Arithmetic
concepts.description: The four arithmetic operations applied to the set of integers, including the sign rules for multiplication and division of signed numbers.

concepts.name: Multiplication
concepts.description: A binary operation representing repeated addition; a × b is the sum of b copies of a, extended to all number systems via the distributive law.

concepts.name: Multiplication Table
concepts.description: The memorized table of products for integers 1 through 12, enabling rapid mental multiplication.

concepts.name: Long Multiplication
concepts.description: The standard written algorithm for multiplying multi-digit numbers by partial products aligned by place value.

concepts.name: Mental Multiplication
concepts.description: Strategies for multiplying numbers mentally, including doubling, halving, distributive decomposition, and patterns with powers of 10.

concepts.name: Division
concepts.description: The inverse operation to multiplication; a ÷ b finds how many times b fits into a, with a possible remainder for integers.

concepts.name: Long Division
concepts.description: The step-by-step algorithm for dividing multi-digit numbers, producing a quotient and remainder.

concepts.name: Remainder
concepts.description: The amount left over after integer division; if a = bq + r with 0 ≤ r < b, then r is the remainder of a ÷ b.

concepts.name: Divisor and Dividend
concepts.description: In a ÷ b = q, a is the dividend, b is the divisor, and q is the quotient; standard vocabulary for the components of a division expression.

concepts.name: Order of Operations
concepts.description: The conventional rules governing the sequence in which operations are performed in an expression: parentheses, exponents, multiplication/division, addition/subtraction.

concepts.name: Fractions
concepts.description: A representation of a rational number as a ratio p/q of two integers, where q ≠ 0, expressing a part-whole or part-part relationship.

concepts.name: Equivalent Fractions
concepts.description: Two fractions a/b and c/d are equivalent if ad = bc; multiplying numerator and denominator by the same nonzero number preserves the fraction's value.

concepts.name: Fraction Simplification
concepts.description: Writing a fraction in its simplest (lowest) form by dividing numerator and denominator by their greatest common divisor.

concepts.name: Addition and Subtraction of Fractions
concepts.description: Adding or subtracting fractions by finding a common denominator and combining numerators; requires knowledge of LCM.

concepts.name: Multiplication and Division of Fractions
concepts.description: Multiplying fractions by multiplying numerators and denominators separately; dividing fractions by multiplying by the reciprocal of the divisor.

concepts.name: Reciprocal
concepts.description: The reciprocal of a nonzero number x is 1/x; their product is 1, making the reciprocal the multiplicative inverse in the field of rationals.

concepts.name: Mixed Numbers
concepts.description: A number expressed as a whole part plus a proper fraction (e.g., 3½), convertible to and from improper fractions.

concepts.name: Improper Fractions
concepts.description: A fraction where the numerator is greater than or equal to the denominator (e.g., 7/3), representing a quantity ≥ 1.

concepts.name: Decimals
concepts.description: A notation for rational numbers using powers of 10 in positions to the right of a decimal point, extending place-value notation to fractions.

concepts.name: Decimal Operations
concepts.description: The four arithmetic operations applied to decimal numbers, requiring careful alignment of decimal points for addition/subtraction and place-value tracking for multiplication.

concepts.name: Terminating Decimals
concepts.description: A decimal whose digits end after finitely many places; corresponds exactly to fractions whose denominators have only 2 and 5 as prime factors.

concepts.name: Repeating Decimals
concepts.description: A decimal in which a block of digits repeats infinitely; every rational number has either a terminating or repeating decimal expansion.

concepts.name: Percentages
concepts.description: A way of expressing a number as a fraction of 100 (per centum); x% = x/100, widely used in everyday contexts such as interest rates and proportions.

concepts.name: Percentage Calculations
concepts.description: Computing percentages, finding what percent one number is of another, and finding the whole given a part and a percent.

concepts.name: Percentage Change
concepts.description: Expressing the change in a quantity as a percentage of its original value: (new − old) / old × 100%.

concepts.name: Ratio
concepts.description: A comparison of two quantities by division, expressing how many times one is contained in the other; written a:b or a/b.

concepts.name: Proportion
concepts.description: An equation stating that two ratios are equal (a/b = c/d); used to solve scale problems, map reading, and many applied problems.

concepts.name: Unit Rate
concepts.description: A ratio whose denominator is 1, expressing the amount of one quantity per single unit of another (e.g., km/h, price per kg).

concepts.name: Direct Variation
concepts.description: A relationship where y = kx for a constant k, meaning y is proportional to x and the graph passes through the origin.

concepts.name: Inverse Variation
concepts.description: A relationship where y = k/x for a constant k, meaning y decreases as x increases and their product is constant.

concepts.name: Rounding
concepts.description: Approximating a number to a specified number of significant figures or decimal places by replacing it with the nearest representable value.

concepts.name: Estimation
concepts.description: The process of finding an approximate value for a calculation using rounding or simplified numbers, emphasizing reasonableness of answers.

concepts.name: Significant Figures
concepts.description: The meaningful digits in a number that carry information about its precision, used in scientific measurement and numerical computation.

concepts.name: Exponentiation
concepts.description: The operation aⁿ representing a multiplied by itself n times; extended to rational and real exponents via roots and limits.

concepts.name: Exponent Rules
concepts.description: The algebraic laws governing exponentiation: aᵐ·aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ, (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ, (ab)ⁿ = aⁿbⁿ, a⁰ = 1, a⁻ⁿ = 1/aⁿ.

concepts.name: Perfect Squares
concepts.description: Integers of the form n² (1, 4, 9, 16, 25, …); recognizing them enables rapid mental square roots.

concepts.name: Perfect Cubes
concepts.description: Integers of the form n³ (1, 8, 27, 64, 125, …); arising in volume calculations and polynomial factorization.

concepts.name: Square Roots
concepts.description: The square root √x of a nonneg real x is the nonneg number y such that y² = x; extends to n-th roots for positive integers n.

concepts.name: Irrational Square Roots
concepts.description: Square roots of non-perfect-square integers (e.g., √2, √3) that are irrational; their proofs use the uniqueness of prime factorization.

concepts.name: Scientific Notation
concepts.description: Writing a number as a × 10ⁿ where 1 ≤ |a| < 10 and n is an integer, enabling compact representation of very large or very small quantities.

concepts.name: Mental Arithmetic
concepts.description: The ability to perform arithmetic computations quickly and accurately without written work, using strategies like decomposition, compensation, and memorized facts.

concepts.name: Divisibility
concepts.description: An integer b is divisible by a (written a | b) if there exists an integer k such that b = ak; the foundation of number theory.
references: Hardy & Wright 2008 An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers

concepts.name: Divisibility Rules
concepts.description: Shortcuts to determine divisibility by small numbers (2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 11) from the decimal representation of a number.

concepts.name: Prime Number
concepts.description: A natural number greater than 1 whose only positive divisors are 1 and itself; the building blocks of all positive integers.

concepts.name: Composite Number
concepts.description: A positive integer greater than 1 that is not prime; equivalently, one with at least one divisor other than 1 and itself.

concepts.name: Sieve of Eratosthenes
concepts.description: An ancient algorithm for finding all primes up to a limit by iteratively marking multiples of each prime as composite.

concepts.name: Prime Factorization
concepts.description: Writing a composite integer as a product of prime powers; unique up to order (the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic).

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
concepts.description: Every integer greater than 1 is either prime or can be expressed uniquely (up to order) as a product of prime numbers.
references: Niven et al 1991 An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers

concepts.name: Greatest Common Divisor
concepts.description: The largest positive integer dividing two given integers a and b; computable efficiently via the Euclidean algorithm.

concepts.name: Euclidean Algorithm
concepts.description: An efficient algorithm computing GCD(a, b) by repeatedly replacing (a, b) with (b, a mod b) until the remainder is zero.

concepts.name: Extended Euclidean Algorithm
concepts.description: Extension of the Euclidean algorithm that also computes integers x, y such that ax + by = GCD(a, b), enabling computation of modular inverses.

concepts.name: Bézout's Identity
concepts.description: For integers a and b, there exist integers x and y such that ax + by = GCD(a, b); a cornerstone of elementary number theory.

concepts.name: Least Common Multiple
concepts.description: The smallest positive integer divisible by both a and b; related to GCD by lcm(a, b) = ab / GCD(a, b).

concepts.name: Division Algorithm
concepts.description: For integers a and b > 0, there exist unique integers q (quotient) and r (remainder) with a = bq + r and 0 ≤ r < b.

concepts.name: Modular Arithmetic
concepts.description: Arithmetic on a finite set of residues {0, 1, …, n−1}, where arithmetic operations are defined modulo n; foundational to cryptography.
references: Ireland & Rosen 1990 A Classical Introduction to Modern Number Theory

concepts.name: Congruence
concepts.description: Two integers a and b are congruent modulo n (a ≡ b (mod n)) if n | (a − b); congruence is an equivalence relation partitioning ℤ into residue classes.

concepts.name: Residue Classes
concepts.description: The n residue classes {[0], [1], …, [n−1]} modulo n form a ring ℤ/nℤ; a field when n is prime.

concepts.name: Modular Inverse
concepts.description: An integer b such that ab ≡ 1 (mod n); exists iff GCD(a, n) = 1, computable via the extended Euclidean algorithm.

concepts.name: Chinese Remainder Theorem
concepts.description: If n₁, …, nₖ are pairwise coprime, the system x ≡ aᵢ (mod nᵢ) has a unique solution modulo n₁⋯nₖ; has applications in cryptography and fast arithmetic.

concepts.name: Fermat's Little Theorem
concepts.description: If p is prime and p ∤ a, then aᵖ⁻¹ ≡ 1 (mod p); fundamental to primality testing and RSA cryptography.

concepts.name: Euler's Theorem
concepts.description: If GCD(a, n) = 1, then a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n), where φ(n) is Euler's totient function; generalizes Fermat's Little Theorem.

concepts.name: Euler's Totient Function
concepts.description: φ(n) counts the number of integers in {1, …, n} coprime to n; computable as n∏(1 − 1/p) over primes p dividing n.

concepts.name: Primality Testing
concepts.description: Algorithms for determining whether a number is prime; ranging from trial division to probabilistic (Miller-Rabin) and deterministic (AKS) polynomial-time tests.

concepts.name: Linear Diophantine Equations
concepts.description: Equations ax + by = c with integer unknowns; solvable iff GCD(a, b) | c, and when solvable have infinitely many integer solutions.

concepts.name: Diophantine Equations
concepts.description: Polynomial equations with two or more unknowns for which integer (or rational) solutions are sought; Fermat's Last Theorem is the most famous example.
references: Silverman 2006 A Friendly Introduction to Number Theory

concepts.name: Pythagorean Triples
concepts.description: Integer solutions (a, b, c) to a² + b² = c²; the primitive triples are parametrized by m > n > 0 with GCD(m,n)=1: a = m²−n², b = 2mn, c = m²+n².

concepts.name: Pell's Equation
concepts.description: The Diophantine equation x² − Dy² = 1 (D not a perfect square); has infinitely many solutions generated from the fundamental solution via continued fractions.

concepts.name: RSA Cryptography (Number-Theoretic Basis)
concepts.description: The number-theoretic foundation of RSA encryption: choose large primes p, q; n = pq; public exponent e coprime to φ(n); private exponent d = e⁻¹ mod φ(n).

concepts.name: Induction in Number Theory
concepts.description: Application of mathematical induction to prove divisibility results, summation formulas, and inequalities about integers.

concepts.name: Distribution of Primes
concepts.description: The study of how primes are distributed among the integers; the Prime Number Theorem states π(x) ~ x/ln(x) as x → ∞.

concepts.name: Prime Number Theorem
concepts.description: The asymptotic law π(x) ~ x/log x describing how the count of primes up to x grows; proved via complex analysis of the Riemann zeta function.
references: Apostol 1976 Introduction to Analytic Number Theory

concepts.name: Riemann Hypothesis
concepts.description: The conjecture that all non-trivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function have real part 1/2; equivalent to the best possible error term in the Prime Number Theorem. Unsolved (Clay Millennium Problem).

concepts.name: Continued Fractions
concepts.description: Representations of real numbers as nested fractions a₀ + 1/(a₁ + 1/(a₂ + …)); rational numbers terminate; quadratic irrationals have periodic CFs.

concepts.name: Algebraic Number Theory
concepts.description: The study of algebraic integers and number fields, extending number theory to rings of integers in finite extensions of ℚ; includes class groups and ideal theory.

concepts.name: Algebraic Integers
concepts.description: Roots of monic polynomials with integer coefficients; they generalize the ordinary integers ℤ to rings of integers in number fields.

concepts.name: Number Fields
concepts.description: Finite extensions K/ℚ; their rings of integers 𝒪_K generalize ℤ, and factorization of ideals replaces unique factorization of elements.

concepts.name: Analytic Number Theory
concepts.description: The use of complex analysis and real analysis to study number-theoretic problems, particularly the distribution of primes via L-functions and Dirichlet series.

concepts.name: Algebraic Expression
concepts.description: A combination of numbers, variables, and arithmetic operations that represents a mathematical quantity; does not contain an equality or inequality sign.

concepts.name: Term
concepts.description: A single number, variable, or product of numbers and variables in an algebraic expression; separated from other terms by + or −.

concepts.name: Coefficient
concepts.description: The numerical factor multiplying the variable part of a term; in 3x², the coefficient is 3.

concepts.name: Like Terms
concepts.description: Terms with identical variable parts (same variables to the same powers); only like terms may be combined by adding or subtracting coefficients.

concepts.name: Algebraic Simplification
concepts.description: Rewriting an algebraic expression in a simpler equivalent form by combining like terms, applying exponent rules, and expanding brackets.

concepts.name: Equation
concepts.description: A statement asserting the equality of two algebraic expressions; solving an equation means finding all values of the variable(s) that make it true.

concepts.name: Solution Set
concepts.description: The set of all values of the variable(s) satisfying an equation or inequality; may be empty, finite, or infinite.

concepts.name: Linear Equation in One Variable
concepts.description: An equation of the form ax + b = c with a ≠ 0; has a unique solution x = (c − b)/a.

concepts.name: Linear Inequality in One Variable
concepts.description: An inequality of the form ax + b < c (or ≤, >, ≥); solution is an interval, and the direction flips when multiplying by a negative.

concepts.name: Absolute Value Equations and Inequalities
concepts.description: Equations and inequalities involving the absolute value; solved by splitting into cases based on the sign of the expression inside the absolute value.

concepts.name: Linear Equation in Two Variables
concepts.description: An equation of the form ax + by = c with infinitely many solutions forming a line in the coordinate plane.

concepts.name: Linear Inequality in Two Variables
concepts.description: An inequality ax + by < c (or ≤, >, ≥) whose solution is a half-plane; used in linear programming to define feasible regions.

concepts.name: Systems of Linear Equations
concepts.description: A collection of linear equations with the same variables; solutions are points (or none, or infinitely many) satisfying all equations simultaneously.

concepts.name: Substitution Method
concepts.description: Solving a system of equations by expressing one variable in terms of others from one equation and substituting into the remaining equations.

concepts.name: Elimination Method
concepts.description: Solving a system of equations by adding or subtracting multiples of equations to eliminate one variable at a time.

concepts.name: Systems of 3 Equations in 3 Variables
concepts.description: Extension of 2-variable systems; solved by elimination or row reduction, with solutions being a point, a line, a plane, or the empty set.

concepts.name: Polynomial
concepts.description: An expression of the form aₙxⁿ + … + a₁x + a₀ where n ∈ ℕ and aₙ ≠ 0; the degree is n and the set of all polynomials forms a ring.

concepts.name: Degree of a Polynomial
concepts.description: The highest power of the variable in a polynomial; determines the maximum number of roots and the long-run behavior.

concepts.name: Polynomial Operations
concepts.description: Adding, subtracting, and multiplying polynomials by combining like terms and applying the distributive property; products may use FOIL or the grid method.

concepts.name: Polynomial Division
concepts.description: Dividing polynomial p(x) by divisor d(x) to get quotient q(x) and remainder r(x) with p(x) = d(x)q(x) + r(x) and deg r < deg d.

concepts.name: Remainder Theorem
concepts.description: When a polynomial p(x) is divided by (x − a), the remainder equals p(a); enables efficient evaluation without full division.

concepts.name: Factor Theorem
concepts.description: (x − a) is a factor of p(x) iff p(a) = 0; the remainder theorem at zero remainder.

concepts.name: Factoring Polynomials
concepts.description: Expressing a polynomial as a product of lower-degree polynomials; fundamental to solving polynomial equations and simplifying expressions.

concepts.name: Factoring out the GCF
concepts.description: Identifying the greatest common factor of all terms and factoring it out: ab + ac = a(b + c).

concepts.name: Factoring Trinomials
concepts.description: Factoring a quadratic or higher-degree trinomial into a product of lower-degree polynomials using patterns, the AC method, or the quadratic formula.

concepts.name: Special Factoring Patterns
concepts.description: Memorized factoring formulas: a²−b² = (a+b)(a−b), a³±b³ = (a±b)(a²∓ab+b²), (a±b)² = a²±2ab+b².

concepts.name: Quadratic Equation
concepts.description: A degree-2 polynomial equation ax² + bx + c = 0; solved by factoring, completing the square, or the quadratic formula; has at most 2 real roots.

concepts.name: Completing the Square
concepts.description: Rewriting ax² + bx + c by adding and subtracting (b/2a)² to form a perfect square trinomial; yields vertex form and derives the quadratic formula.

concepts.name: Quadratic Formula
concepts.description: The explicit formula x = (−b ± √(b²−4ac)) / (2a) giving all roots of ax² + bx + c = 0; derived from completing the square.

concepts.name: Discriminant
concepts.description: The expression b² − 4ac: positive → two real roots, zero → one repeated root, negative → two complex roots.

concepts.name: Polynomial Roots (Real and Complex)
concepts.description: Values x = a where p(a) = 0; a degree-n polynomial has exactly n roots (counting multiplicity) in ℂ by the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra.

concepts.name: Rational Root Theorem
concepts.description: For a polynomial with integer coefficients, any rational root p/q (in lowest terms) must have p dividing the constant term and q dividing the leading coefficient.

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
aliases: every polynomial has a complex root
concepts.description: Every non-constant polynomial with complex coefficients has at least one complex root; consequently has exactly n roots (with multiplicity) in ℂ.

concepts.name: Complex Roots of Polynomials
concepts.description: Non-real complex roots of polynomials with real coefficients come in conjugate pairs a ± bi; enables factoring over ℝ into linear and irreducible quadratic factors.

concepts.name: Rational Expressions
concepts.description: Fractions p(x)/q(x) where p and q are polynomials with q ≠ 0; simplified by factoring and canceling common factors.

concepts.name: Addition of Rational Expressions
concepts.description: Adding or subtracting rational expressions by finding a common polynomial denominator and combining numerators.

concepts.name: Multiplication of Rational Expressions
concepts.description: Multiplying rational expressions by multiplying numerators and denominators, then simplifying by canceling common factors.

concepts.name: Rational Equations
concepts.description: Equations containing rational expressions; solved by multiplying through by the LCD and checking for extraneous solutions (values that make a denominator zero).

concepts.name: Exponent Rules (Algebraic)
concepts.description: Extension of integer exponent laws to include zero, negative, and rational (fractional) exponents; enables manipulation of exponential and radical expressions.

concepts.name: Zero Exponent
concepts.description: For any nonzero base a, a⁰ = 1; follows from the quotient rule aⁿ/aⁿ = a⁰ = 1.

concepts.name: Negative Exponent
concepts.description: a⁻ⁿ = 1/aⁿ for a ≠ 0; enables writing reciprocals as powers and simplifying expressions with negative exponents.

concepts.name: Fractional Exponent
concepts.description: a^(m/n) = ⁿ√(aᵐ) = (ⁿ√a)ᵐ; bridge between radical and exponential notation, enabling uniform treatment of roots as powers.

concepts.name: Radical Expressions
concepts.description: Expressions involving n-th roots ⁿ√a; simplified by extracting perfect powers from under the radical and rationalizing denominators.

concepts.name: Simplifying Radical Expressions
concepts.description: Rewriting a radical by factoring the radicand to extract perfect powers: √(12) = 2√3.

concepts.name: Rationalizing the Denominator
concepts.description: Eliminating radicals from the denominator of a fraction by multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate or appropriate radical.

concepts.name: Radical Equations
concepts.description: Equations in which the variable appears under a radical sign; solved by isolating the radical and squaring (requiring check for extraneous solutions).

concepts.name: Exponential Function
concepts.description: A function of the form f(x) = aˣ with base a > 0, a ≠ 1; exhibits exponential growth (a > 1) or decay (a < 1) and is the inverse of the logarithm.

concepts.name: Exponential Equations
concepts.description: Equations in which the unknown appears in an exponent; solved using logarithms or by expressing both sides as powers of the same base.

concepts.name: Logarithm
concepts.description: The inverse of the exponential function: logₐ(x) is the exponent to which a must be raised to give x; defined for x > 0, a > 0, a ≠ 1.

concepts.name: Logarithm Properties
concepts.description: Key identities: log(xy) = log x + log y; log(x/y) = log x − log y; log(xᵣ) = r log x; enabling algebraic manipulation of logarithmic expressions.

concepts.name: Natural Logarithm
concepts.description: The logarithm with base e ≈ 2.71828; ln x = log_e x; arises naturally in calculus as the antiderivative of 1/x.

concepts.name: Change of Base Formula
concepts.description: logₐ(x) = log_b(x) / log_b(a) for any valid base b; allows computation using any convenient base (typically 10 or e).

concepts.name: Logarithmic Equations
concepts.description: Equations in which the unknown appears as an argument of a logarithm; solved by using logarithm properties to condense and then exponentiate.

concepts.name: Inequality
concepts.description: A mathematical statement asserting that one expression is greater than or less than another; solutions form intervals or regions rather than isolated points.

concepts.name: Polynomial Inequality
concepts.description: Inequality p(x) > 0 (or <, ≥, ≤) solved by finding roots, constructing a sign chart, and identifying intervals where the polynomial has the required sign.

concepts.name: Rational Inequality
concepts.description: Inequality of the form p(x)/q(x) > 0 (with q ≠ 0); solved using a sign chart based on zeros of p and q.

concepts.name: Binomial Theorem
concepts.description: (a+b)ⁿ = Σ C(n,k) aⁿ⁻ᵏ bᵏ; gives the general expansion of a binomial power in terms of binomial coefficients.

concepts.name: Pascal's Triangle
concepts.description: A triangular array of binomial coefficients C(n,k) where each entry is the sum of the two entries above it; encodes patterns in number theory and combinatorics.

concepts.name: Vieta's Formulas
concepts.description: Relationships between the coefficients of a polynomial and symmetric functions of its roots; e.g., for ax²+bx+c, the sum of roots is −b/a and product is c/a.

concepts.name: Point
concepts.description: A dimensionless location in space with no length, width, or height; the fundamental primitive of Euclidean geometry.

concepts.name: Line
concepts.description: A one-dimensional figure extending infinitely in both directions, determined by any two distinct points.

concepts.name: Line Segment
concepts.description: The part of a line between two endpoints; has a finite measurable length.

concepts.name: Ray
concepts.description: A part of a line with one endpoint that extends infinitely in one direction.

concepts.name: Plane
concepts.description: A two-dimensional flat surface extending infinitely; determined by any three non-collinear points.

concepts.name: Angle
concepts.description: The figure formed by two rays sharing a common endpoint (vertex); measured in degrees or radians.

concepts.name: Types of Angles
aliases: acute, obtuse, right, straight, reflex angles
concepts.description: Classification of angles: acute (0°–90°), right (90°), obtuse (90°–180°), straight (180°), reflex (180°–360°).

concepts.name: Angle Measurement
concepts.description: Angles are measured in degrees (1 full rotation = 360°) or radians (1 full rotation = 2π); a protractor measures angles physically.

concepts.name: Angle Pairs
concepts.description: Special pairs of angles: complementary (sum 90°), supplementary (sum 180°), vertical angles (equal across an intersection), and linear pairs (supplementary adjacent).

concepts.name: Parallel Lines
aliases: parallel lines cut by a transversal
concepts.description: Lines that never meet; when cut by a transversal they produce equal corresponding angles, alternate interior angles, and co-interior supplementary angles.

concepts.name: Perpendicular Lines
concepts.description: Two lines intersecting at 90°; the perpendicular from a point to a line is the shortest segment from the point to the line.

concepts.name: Triangle
concepts.description: A polygon with three sides and three angles whose sum is 180°; the simplest non-trivial polygon and the basis of trigonometry.

concepts.name: Types of Triangles
aliases: scalene, isosceles, equilateral, acute, obtuse, right triangle
concepts.description: Classification by sides: scalene (all sides different), isosceles (two sides equal), equilateral (all sides equal). By angles: acute, right, obtuse.

concepts.name: Triangle Angle Sum Theorem
concepts.description: The sum of the three interior angles of any triangle in Euclidean geometry equals 180°.

concepts.name: Right Triangle
concepts.description: A triangle with one 90° angle; the side opposite the right angle (hypotenuse) is the longest side and satisfies the Pythagorean theorem.

concepts.name: Pythagorean Theorem
concepts.description: In a right triangle with legs a, b and hypotenuse c: a² + b² = c². One of the most fundamental results in all of mathematics with hundreds of proofs.

concepts.name: Converse of the Pythagorean Theorem
concepts.description: If a² + b² = c² for sides a, b, c of a triangle, then the triangle is right-angled with hypotenuse c.

concepts.name: Congruent Triangles
concepts.description: Two triangles are congruent if they have the same shape and size; established by the congruence criteria SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, and HL (for right triangles).

concepts.name: Similar Triangles
concepts.description: Two triangles are similar if they have the same shape (proportional sides and equal angles); established by AA, SAS, or SSS similarity criteria.

concepts.name: Triangle Centers
concepts.description: Special points associated with a triangle: centroid (intersection of medians), circumcenter (circumscribed circle center), incenter (inscribed circle center), orthocenter (altitude intersection).

concepts.name: Area of a Triangle
concepts.description: Area = ½ × base × height; also Heron's formula: √(s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)) where s is the semiperimeter.

concepts.name: Polygon
concepts.description: A closed plane figure bounded by straight line segments; characterized by the number of sides and classified as regular (all sides and angles equal) or irregular.

concepts.name: Polygon Angle Sum
concepts.description: The sum of interior angles of a convex n-gon is (n−2)×180°; each interior angle of a regular n-gon is (n−2)×180°/n.

concepts.name: Quadrilateral
concepts.description: A polygon with four sides; includes parallelograms, rectangles, rhombuses, squares, trapezoids, and kites, classified by side and angle properties.

concepts.name: Parallelogram
concepts.description: A quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel; includes rectangles (90° angles), rhombuses (all sides equal), and squares (both).

concepts.name: Trapezoid
concepts.description: A quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides (the bases); area = ½(b₁ + b₂)h.

concepts.name: Regular Polygon
concepts.description: A polygon with all sides equal and all angles equal; inscribed in a circle, approaching the circle as n → ∞.

concepts.name: Area of Polygons
concepts.description: Computing areas of polygons by decomposition into triangles or by standard formulas (parallelogram: bh; trapezoid: ½(b₁+b₂)h; regular polygon: ½ap).

concepts.name: Area
concepts.description: The measure of the two-dimensional extent of a figure; for curved regions computed via integration.

concepts.name: Perimeter
concepts.description: The total length of the boundary of a two-dimensional figure; for circles it is called the circumference.

concepts.name: Circle
concepts.description: The set of all points in a plane equidistant from a fixed center point; the fundamental round figure of 2D geometry.

concepts.name: Parts of a Circle
concepts.description: Radius (center to boundary), diameter (chord through center), chord (segment joining two boundary points), arc (portion of boundary), sector (pie slice), tangent (line touching at one point).

concepts.name: Circumference of a Circle
concepts.description: The perimeter of a circle: C = 2πr = πd, where r is the radius and π ≈ 3.14159…

concepts.name: Area of a Circle
concepts.description: A = πr²; derivable via integration or as the limit of inscribed regular polygon areas.

concepts.name: Circle Theorems
concepts.description: Key theorems: inscribed angle = half central angle; tangent perpendicular to radius; equal chords equidistant from center; power of a point.

concepts.name: Equation of a Circle
concepts.description: The equation of a circle with center (h, k) and radius r is (x−h)² + (y−k)² = r²; derivable from the distance formula.

concepts.name: Geometric Proof
concepts.description: A rigorous deductive argument establishing a geometric truth from axioms, definitions, and previously proved theorems; typically presented in two-column or paragraph form.

concepts.name: Length
concepts.description: The one-dimensional measure of a line segment; extended to curves via arc length.

concepts.name: Surface Area
concepts.description: The total area of all faces of a three-dimensional solid; for curved surfaces computed via surface integrals.

concepts.name: Volume
concepts.description: The three-dimensional measure of the space enclosed by a solid; computed by formulas for standard solids or by integration.

concepts.name: Three-Dimensional Solids
concepts.description: Geometric solids: prisms (uniform cross-section), pyramids (apex), cones, cylinders, spheres; characterized by faces, edges, vertices and their counts satisfying Euler's formula V−E+F=2.

concepts.name: Platonic Solids
concepts.description: The five convex regular polyhedra (tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, icosahedron); the only regular polyhedra in 3D, classified by Euclid.

concepts.name: Coordinate Plane
concepts.description: The plane ℝ² with two perpendicular number lines (x-axis, y-axis) intersecting at the origin, enabling algebraic representation of geometric objects.

concepts.name: Cartesian Coordinates
concepts.description: The pair (x, y) locating a point in the coordinate plane by its signed distances from the y-axis (x) and x-axis (y).

concepts.name: Quadrants
concepts.description: The four regions of the coordinate plane divided by the axes: Quadrant I (x>0, y>0), II (x<0, y>0), III (x<0, y<0), IV (x>0, y<0).

concepts.name: Distance Formula
concepts.description: The distance between (x₁, y₁) and (x₂, y₂) is √((x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²); a direct application of the Pythagorean theorem in coordinates.

concepts.name: Midpoint Formula
concepts.description: The midpoint of a segment from (x₁, y₁) to (x₂, y₂) is ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2).

concepts.name: Slope
concepts.description: The slope of a line is the ratio of vertical change to horizontal change between any two points; m = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁); equal for parallel lines, negative reciprocals for perpendicular.

concepts.name: Equations of Lines
concepts.description: y = mx + b (slope-intercept), y − y₁ = m(x − x₁) (point-slope), ax + by = c (standard form); three equivalent representations of a line.

concepts.name: Conic Sections
concepts.description: Curves formed by the intersection of a double cone and a plane: circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas; unified by the general second-degree equation.

concepts.name: Parabola
concepts.description: A conic section equidistant from a focus and a directrix; graph of a quadratic function; opens up/down (y = ax²+bx+c) or left/right (x = ay²+by+c).

concepts.name: Ellipse
concepts.description: A conic section where the sum of distances from two foci is constant; equation x²/a² + y²/b² = 1; a circle is a special case with equal foci.

concepts.name: Hyperbola
concepts.description: A conic section where the difference of distances from two foci is constant; has two branches and asymptotes y = ±(b/a)x.

concepts.name: Polar Coordinates
concepts.description: An alternative 2D coordinate system using distance r from the origin and angle θ from the positive x-axis; related to Cartesian by x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ.

concepts.name: Polar Curves
concepts.description: Curves expressed in polar form r = f(θ); includes cardioids, roses, lemniscates, and Archimedean spirals.

concepts.name: Geometric Transformations
concepts.description: Functions mapping the plane to itself that preserve shape properties; isometries (translations, rotations, reflections) preserve distance; dilations scale by a factor.

concepts.name: Translation
concepts.description: A transformation that moves every point by the same vector (h, k), preserving shape, size, and orientation.

concepts.name: Rotation
aliases: rotation by θ about a point
concepts.description: A transformation that turns every point by angle θ about a fixed center; expressed by a 2×2 rotation matrix.

concepts.name: Reflection
concepts.description: A transformation that flips every point over a line of reflection, reversing orientation.

concepts.name: Dilation
concepts.description: A transformation scaling distances from a center by a constant factor k; produces a similar figure of different size.

concepts.name: Vectors in 2D
concepts.description: A quantity with both magnitude and direction in the plane, represented by an ordered pair; added head-to-tail and scaled by multiplication.

concepts.name: Vectors in 3D
concepts.description: Vectors in three-dimensional space; introduced by extending the 2D framework to triples (a, b, c) with dot and cross products.

concepts.name: Dot Product
concepts.description: The dot product a·b = Σaᵢbᵢ = |a||b|cos θ; measures the component of one vector in the direction of another; zero iff orthogonal.

concepts.name: Cross Product
concepts.description: The cross product a × b is a vector perpendicular to both a and b with magnitude |a||b|sin θ; defined only in ℝ³ (and ℝ⁷).

concepts.name: Curves in Space
concepts.description: Smooth curves in ℝⁿ studied via their parametrization; local geometry described by curvature κ and torsion τ via the Frenet-Serret apparatus.
references: do Carmo 1976 Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

concepts.name: Curvature
concepts.description: The rate at which a curve bends; κ = |dT/ds| where T is the unit tangent vector and s is arc length.

concepts.name: Frenet-Serret Formulas
concepts.description: The system T' = κN, N' = −κT + τB, B' = −τN relating the tangent T, normal N, binormal B and encoding all local geometry of a space curve.

concepts.name: Differential Geometry of Surfaces
concepts.description: The study of smooth surfaces in ℝ³ via first and second fundamental forms, Gaussian curvature K, mean curvature H, and the Gauss-Bonnet theorem.
references: Spivak 1979 A Comprehensive Introduction to Differential Geometry

concepts.name: Geometric Constructions
concepts.description: Constructions achievable using only compass and straightedge; includes bisecting angles and segments, constructing regular polygons; Galois theory classifies which are possible.

concepts.name: Angle Measure
concepts.description: Angles measured in degrees (360° per revolution) or radians (2π per revolution); π radians = 180°.

concepts.name: Degree-Radian Conversion
concepts.description: radians = degrees × π/180; degrees = radians × 180/π; fluency in both systems is essential for calculus and physics.

concepts.name: Right-Triangle Trigonometry
concepts.description: Definition of sine, cosine, and tangent as ratios of sides in a right triangle (opposite/hypotenuse, adjacent/hypotenuse, opposite/adjacent).

concepts.name: Six Trigonometric Ratios
concepts.description: The six trig ratios: sin, cos, tan and their reciprocals csc, sec, cot; fundamental to all trigonometric work.

concepts.name: Trigonometric Values at Special Angles
concepts.description: Exact values of sin, cos, tan at 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°; derived from the 30-60-90 and 45-45-90 triangle relationships.

concepts.name: Unit Circle
concepts.description: The circle of radius 1 centered at the origin; defines sin θ and cos θ as the y and x coordinates of the point at angle θ, extending trig to all real angles.

concepts.name: Reference Angles
concepts.description: The acute angle between the terminal side of an angle and the x-axis; enables computation of trig values in all four quadrants.

concepts.name: Trigonometric Functions
concepts.description: The functions sin(x), cos(x), tan(x), etc. defined for all real x via the unit circle; periodic with period 2π (or π for tan).

concepts.name: Amplitude, Period, Phase Shift
concepts.description: For y = A sin(Bx + C) + D: amplitude |A|, period 2π/|B|, phase shift −C/B, vertical shift D; enables sketching any sinusoidal curve.

concepts.name: Graphs of Trigonometric Functions
concepts.description: Sketching y = sin x, cos x, tan x and their transformations; identifying zeros, maxima, minima, and asymptotes.

concepts.name: Trigonometric Identities
concepts.description: Equations involving trig functions that hold for all valid angles; used to simplify expressions and solve equations.

concepts.name: Pythagorean Identities
concepts.description: sin²x + cos²x = 1, 1 + tan²x = sec²x, 1 + cot²x = csc²x; all derived from x² + y² = 1 on the unit circle.

concepts.name: Reciprocal Identities
concepts.description: csc x = 1/sin x, sec x = 1/cos x, cot x = 1/tan x = cos x/sin x; defining the three reciprocal trigonometric functions.

concepts.name: Sum and Difference Formulas
concepts.description: sin(A±B) = sinAcosB ± cosAsinB; cos(A±B) = cosAcosB ∓ sinAsinB; fundamental for deriving all other trig identities.

concepts.name: Double Angle Formulas
concepts.description: sin(2x) = 2sinxcosx; cos(2x) = cos²x − sin²x = 2cos²x − 1 = 1 − 2sin²x; tan(2x) = 2tanx/(1−tan²x).

concepts.name: Half Angle Formulas
concepts.description: sin(x/2) = ±√((1−cosx)/2); cos(x/2) = ±√((1+cosx)/2); derived from the double-angle formula for cos by substitution.

concepts.name: Product-to-Sum and Sum-to-Product Formulas
concepts.description: sinAcosB = ½[sin(A+B) + sin(A−B)]; enable converting products into sums (useful for integration) and vice versa.

concepts.name: Inverse Trigonometric Functions
concepts.description: The functions arcsin, arccos, arctan (restrictions of trig functions to make them invertible); their domains and ranges and the identities connecting them.

concepts.name: Trigonometric Equations
concepts.description: Equations involving trig functions; solved using identities to simplify and inverse functions to find principal solutions, then adding period multiples for the general solution.

concepts.name: Law of Sines
concepts.description: For any triangle: a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C = 2R (circumradius); enables solving non-right triangles given AAS, ASA, or SSA configurations.

concepts.name: Law of Cosines
concepts.description: c² = a² + b² − 2ab cosC; generalizes the Pythagorean theorem to arbitrary triangles; enables solving SSS and SAS configurations.

concepts.name: Polar Form of Complex Numbers
concepts.description: z = r(cosθ + i sinθ) = re^(iθ); relates the geometry of ℂ (modulus and argument) to trig functions; multiplication becomes rotation and scaling.

concepts.name: De Moivre's Theorem
concepts.description: (cos θ + i sin θ)ⁿ = cos(nθ) + i sin(nθ); enables computation of powers and n-th roots of complex numbers and derivation of multiple-angle formulas.

concepts.name: Euler's Formula
concepts.description: e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ; at θ = π: e^(iπ) + 1 = 0 (Euler's identity); unifies exponential and trigonometric functions via complex analysis.

concepts.name: Hyperbolic Functions
concepts.description: sinh x = (eˣ − e⁻ˣ)/2; cosh x = (eˣ + e⁻ˣ)/2; tanh x = sinh x/cosh x; analogues of trig functions for the unit hyperbola x²−y²=1.

concepts.name: Function
concepts.description: A rule assigning to each element of a domain set exactly one element of a codomain set; the central object of modern mathematics.

concepts.name: Domain and Range
concepts.description: The domain is the set of valid inputs; the range (image) is the set of all actual outputs; the codomain is the declared target set.

concepts.name: Function Notation
concepts.description: Writing f(x) to denote the value of f at x; evaluating f(a) means substituting x = a into the rule for f.

concepts.name: Graph of a Function
concepts.description: The graph of f is the set of all points (x, f(x)) in the coordinate plane; the vertical line test identifies graphs of functions.

concepts.name: Real-Valued Function
concepts.description: A function whose domain and codomain are subsets of ℝ; the primary objects of calculus.

concepts.name: Zero of a Function
concepts.description: A value x₀ where f(x₀) = 0; corresponds to an x-intercept of the graph; studied via the Intermediate Value Theorem.

concepts.name: Injective (One-to-One) Function
concepts.description: f is injective if f(a) = f(b) implies a = b; different inputs give different outputs; passes the horizontal line test.

concepts.name: Surjective (Onto) Function
concepts.description: f: A → B is surjective if every b ∈ B has at least one a ∈ A with f(a) = b; the range equals the codomain.

concepts.name: Bijective Function
concepts.description: A function that is both injective and surjective; establishes a perfect pairing between domain and codomain; uniquely invertible.

concepts.name: Inverse Functions
concepts.description: The inverse f⁻¹ of a bijective function f satisfies f⁻¹(f(x)) = x and f(f⁻¹(y)) = y; its graph is the reflection of f over y = x.

concepts.name: Composition of Functions
concepts.description: The composite (f∘g)(x) = f(g(x)) applies g first, then f; requires range of g ⊆ domain of f; not generally commutative.

concepts.name: Even and Odd Functions
concepts.description: f is even if f(−x) = f(x) (symmetric about y-axis); f is odd if f(−x) = −f(x) (symmetric about origin); affects integral properties.

concepts.name: Transformations of Functions
concepts.description: Systematic ways to transform a graph: vertical/horizontal shifts (f(x)±c, f(x±c)), reflections (−f(x), f(−x)), and stretches (af(x), f(bx)).

concepts.name: Periodic Function
concepts.description: A function satisfying f(x+T) = f(x) for all x and some positive T (period); trigonometric functions are the archetypal examples.

concepts.name: Linear Function
concepts.description: A function of the form f(x) = mx + b; its graph is a straight line with slope m and y-intercept b.

concepts.name: Quadratic Function
concepts.description: A degree-2 function f(x) = ax² + bx + c; its graph is a parabola; vertex at (−b/2a, f(−b/2a)); models projectile motion and optimization.

concepts.name: Vertex Form of a Quadratic
concepts.description: f(x) = a(x−h)² + k; vertex at (h, k); a > 0 means minimum, a < 0 means maximum; obtained by completing the square.

concepts.name: Polynomial Function
concepts.description: A function defined by a polynomial p(x) = aₙxⁿ + … + a₀; continuous everywhere; behavior determined by degree and leading coefficient.

concepts.name: End Behavior
concepts.description: The behavior of a polynomial as x → ±∞, determined entirely by the leading term aₙxⁿ: rises/falls depending on sign of aₙ and parity of n.

concepts.name: Real Roots of Polynomials
concepts.description: x-intercepts of the polynomial graph; multiple roots cause the curve to touch but not cross the axis; behavior near roots described by multiplicity.

concepts.name: Rational Function
concepts.description: A function p(x)/q(x) where p, q are polynomials and q ≠ 0; domain excludes zeros of q; has vertical asymptotes and possibly holes.

concepts.name: Vertical Asymptote
concepts.description: A vertical line x = a where |f(x)| → ∞; occurs where q(a) = 0 and p(a) ≠ 0 for f = p/q.

concepts.name: Horizontal Asymptote
concepts.description: A horizontal line y = L that f(x) approaches as x → ±∞; determined by comparing degrees of numerator and denominator.

concepts.name: Exponential Function
concepts.description: f(x) = aˣ (a > 0, a ≠ 1); the natural exponential eˣ is its own derivative; models growth, decay, compound interest.

concepts.name: Logarithmic Function
concepts.description: The inverse of the exponential function; f(x) = logₐ(x); domain (0, ∞); ln x is the natural logarithm; derivative is 1/x.

concepts.name: Piecewise-Defined Function
concepts.description: A function defined by different expressions on different intervals of its domain; continuity requires the pieces to agree at breakpoints.

concepts.name: Step Function
concepts.description: A piecewise-constant function; the floor ⌊x⌋ = greatest integer ≤ x; the ceiling ⌈x⌉ = smallest integer ≥ x.

concepts.name: Monotonic Function
concepts.description: A function that is either entirely increasing (f(a) < f(b) when a < b) or entirely decreasing; strictly monotonic functions are injective.

concepts.name: Operations on Functions
concepts.description: Functions can be added, subtracted, multiplied, and divided pointwise; the algebra of functions including composition as a non-commutative product.

concepts.name: Sequence
concepts.description: A function from ℕ (or a subset) to a set, listing its values as an ordered collection a₁, a₂, a₃, …

concepts.name: Arithmetic Sequence
concepts.description: A sequence where each term differs from the previous by a fixed common difference d: aₙ = a₁ + (n−1)d.

concepts.name: Geometric Sequence
concepts.description: A sequence where each term is multiplied by a fixed common ratio r: aₙ = a₁ · rⁿ⁻¹.

concepts.name: Recursive Sequences
concepts.description: Sequences defined by a recurrence relation aₙ = f(aₙ₋₁, aₙ₋₂, …) and initial conditions; e.g., Fibonacci: F₁ = F₂ = 1, Fₙ = Fₙ₋₁ + Fₙ₋₂.

concepts.name: Convergent Sequence
concepts.description: A sequence {aₙ} converges to L if for every ε > 0 there exists N such that |aₙ − L| < ε for all n > N; the ε-N definition of limit.

concepts.name: Divergent Sequence
concepts.description: A sequence that does not converge; includes sequences tending to ±∞ and oscillating sequences like (−1)ⁿ.

concepts.name: Series
concepts.description: The sum of the terms of a sequence; formally defined as the limit of partial sums; may converge or diverge.

concepts.name: Partial Sums
concepts.description: The nth partial sum Sₙ = a₁ + a₂ + … + aₙ; the series Σaₙ converges iff the sequence {Sₙ} converges.

concepts.name: Arithmetic Series
concepts.description: The sum of the first n terms of an AP: Sₙ = n(a₁ + aₙ)/2 = n(2a₁ + (n−1)d)/2.

concepts.name: Geometric Series
concepts.description: The sum of the first n terms of a GP: Sₙ = a(1−rⁿ)/(1−r); extends to an infinite series converging to a/(1−r) when |r| < 1.

concepts.name: Infinite Geometric Series
concepts.description: When |r| < 1, the infinite GP converges: S∞ = a/(1−r); when |r| ≥ 1 it diverges.

concepts.name: Convergence of Series
concepts.description: A series Σaₙ converges if its sequence of partial sums converges; multiple tests determine convergence for specific series types.

concepts.name: Divergence Test (nth Term Test)
concepts.description: If lim aₙ ≠ 0, then Σaₙ diverges; the converse is false (the harmonic series diverges despite terms → 0).

concepts.name: Comparison Test
concepts.description: If 0 ≤ aₙ ≤ bₙ and Σbₙ converges, then Σaₙ converges; if aₙ ≥ bₙ ≥ 0 and Σbₙ diverges, then Σaₙ diverges.

concepts.name: Ratio Test
concepts.description: If L = lim|aₙ₊₁/aₙ| < 1, series converges absolutely; L > 1, diverges; L = 1, inconclusive.

concepts.name: Root Test
concepts.description: If L = lim|aₙ|^(1/n) < 1, series converges absolutely; L > 1, diverges; L = 1, inconclusive.

concepts.name: Integral Test
concepts.description: If f is positive, continuous, and decreasing on [1, ∞), then Σf(n) and ∫₁^∞ f(x)dx either both converge or both diverge.

concepts.name: Alternating Series
concepts.description: Σ(−1)ⁿaₙ converges if {aₙ} is positive, decreasing, and tends to 0 (Leibniz's alternating series test).

concepts.name: Absolute and Conditional Convergence
concepts.description: Σaₙ converges absolutely if Σ|aₙ| converges (stronger); conditionally if Σaₙ converges but Σ|aₙ| diverges; Riemann rearrangement theorem.

concepts.name: Harmonic Series
concepts.description: The series Σ1/n diverges (proved by Cauchy condensation or integral test); the p-series Σ1/nᵖ converges iff p > 1.
references: Hardy 1920 A Course of Pure Mathematics

concepts.name: Telescoping Series
concepts.description: A series Σ(bₙ − bₙ₊₁) where most terms cancel; the nth partial sum equals b₁ − bₙ₊₁, enabling exact evaluation.

concepts.name: Limit of a Function
concepts.description: lim_{x→a} f(x) = L means f(x) can be made arbitrarily close to L by taking x sufficiently close (but not equal) to a; formalized by the ε-δ definition.

concepts.name: Limit Laws
concepts.description: The sum, difference, product, quotient, and power rules for limits: lim(f±g) = limf ± limg, lim(fg) = (limf)(limg), etc.

concepts.name: One-Sided Limits
concepts.description: The right-hand limit lim_{x→a⁺} f(x) and left-hand limit lim_{x→a⁻} f(x); the two-sided limit exists iff both one-sided limits exist and are equal.

concepts.name: Limits at Infinity
concepts.description: lim_{x→∞} f(x) = L means f(x) → L as x grows without bound; determines horizontal asymptotes of rational and other functions.

concepts.name: Squeeze Theorem
concepts.description: If g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ h(x) near a and lim g = lim h = L, then lim f = L; used to compute limits like lim(sinx/x) = 1.

concepts.name: Continuity
concepts.description: f is continuous at a if lim_{x→a} f(x) = f(a); on an interval if continuous at every point; the calculus definition to be sharpened in real analysis.

concepts.name: Types of Discontinuity
concepts.description: Removable (hole), jump (left/right limits exist but differ), and infinite (vertical asymptote) discontinuities; classified by one-sided limits.

concepts.name: Intermediate Value Theorem
concepts.description: If f is continuous on [a, b] and N is between f(a) and f(b), then there exists c ∈ (a, b) with f(c) = N; guarantees roots and intermediate values.

concepts.name: Slope of Tangent and Rate of Change
concepts.description: The derivative is motivated by the limit of the average rate of change (difference quotient) as the interval shrinks to zero, giving the instantaneous rate.

concepts.name: Derivative (Definition)
concepts.description: f'(x) = lim_{h→0} [f(x+h) − f(x)] / h; the instantaneous rate of change of f at x; the slope of the tangent line to the graph at x.

concepts.name: Differentiability
concepts.description: f is differentiable at a if f'(a) exists; differentiability implies continuity but not vice versa; non-differentiable at corners, cusps, and vertical tangents.

concepts.name: Basic Differentiation Rules
concepts.description: Power rule: d/dx(xⁿ) = nxⁿ⁻¹; constant multiple rule; sum/difference rule; enable differentiation of all polynomials.

concepts.name: Product Rule
concepts.description: (fg)' = f'g + fg'; enables differentiation of products of functions.

concepts.name: Quotient Rule
concepts.description: (f/g)' = (f'g − fg')/g²; enables differentiation of quotients; derivable from product rule and chain rule.

concepts.name: Chain Rule
concepts.description: The derivative of a composite function: (f∘g)'(x) = f'(g(x)) · g'(x); one of the most frequently used differentiation rules.

concepts.name: Derivative of Exponential Functions
concepts.description: d/dx(eˣ) = eˣ (eˣ is its own derivative); d/dx(aˣ) = aˣ ln a; derived via the chain rule and the natural log definition.

concepts.name: Derivative of Logarithmic Functions
concepts.description: d/dx(ln x) = 1/x; d/dx(logₐ x) = 1/(x ln a); logarithmic differentiation simplifies products and quotients raised to powers.

concepts.name: Logarithmic Differentiation
concepts.description: Taking ln of both sides before differentiating; required for variable-base-and-exponent forms like y = xˢⁱⁿˣ.

concepts.name: Derivatives of Trigonometric Functions
concepts.description: d/dx(sin x) = cos x; d/dx(cos x) = −sin x; d/dx(tan x) = sec²x; and their reciprocals; all derivable from the definition and squeeze theorem.

concepts.name: Derivatives of Inverse Trig Functions
concepts.description: d/dx(arcsin x) = 1/√(1−x²); d/dx(arctan x) = 1/(1+x²); derived via implicit differentiation of the inverse relation.

concepts.name: Derivatives of Hyperbolic Functions
concepts.description: d/dx(sinh x) = cosh x; d/dx(cosh x) = sinh x; analogous to trig but without sign changes; derivable directly from exponential definitions.

concepts.name: Implicit Differentiation
concepts.description: Differentiating both sides of an equation in x and y with respect to x, treating y as a function of x via the chain rule; gives dy/dx without solving for y.

concepts.name: Higher-Order Derivatives
concepts.description: The second derivative f'' = (f')' measures the rate of change of the slope; higher derivatives arise in Taylor series, ODEs, and curvature.

concepts.name: Related Rates
concepts.description: Using implicit differentiation with respect to time to relate the rates of change of multiple related quantities; e.g., expanding balloon radius and volume.

concepts.name: L'Hôpital's Rule
concepts.description: If lim f/g is indeterminate (0/0 or ∞/∞), then lim f/g = lim f'/g' under appropriate conditions; handles indeterminate forms like 0·∞, 1^∞, etc.

concepts.name: Mean Value Theorem
concepts.description: If f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), there exists c ∈ (a,b) with f'(c) = (f(b)−f(a))/(b−a); the average rate equals the instantaneous rate somewhere.

concepts.name: Rolle's Theorem
concepts.description: Special case of MVT: if f is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and f(a) = f(b), then there exists c ∈ (a,b) with f'(c) = 0.

concepts.name: Increasing and Decreasing Functions
concepts.description: If f' > 0 on an interval, f is increasing; if f' < 0, f is decreasing; follows from the MVT.

concepts.name: Critical Points
concepts.description: Points where f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) is undefined; candidates for local maxima, minima, and inflection points.

concepts.name: Local Extrema
concepts.description: Local max/min at critical points c; first derivative test: sign change of f'; second derivative test: f''(c) > 0 → local min, f''(c) < 0 → local max.

concepts.name: Concavity and Inflection Points
concepts.description: f is concave up where f'' > 0 (cup shape) and concave down where f'' < 0 (cap shape); inflection points where concavity changes.

concepts.name: Optimization (Calculus)
concepts.description: Finding global maxima and minima of a function on a closed interval or over all reals using critical points, endpoints, and boundary analysis.

concepts.name: Curve Sketching
concepts.description: Systematic method to sketch y = f(x) using domain, intercepts, symmetry, asymptotes, increasing/decreasing intervals, and concavity.

concepts.name: Linearization and Differentials
concepts.description: Approximating f(x) near a by its tangent line L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x−a); the differential df = f'(x)dx is the linearization of the increment.

concepts.name: Antiderivatives
concepts.description: F is an antiderivative of f if F' = f; uniquely determined up to a constant; written ∫f(x)dx = F(x) + C.

concepts.name: Riemann Sums
concepts.description: Approximations of the area under a curve by summing areas of rectangles with widths Δx and heights f(xᵢ*); the definite integral is their limit as Δx → 0.

concepts.name: Definite Integral
concepts.description: The limit of Riemann sums lim_{n→∞} Σf(xᵢ*)Δx = ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx; measures signed area between f and the x-axis from a to b.

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 1
concepts.description: If F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt and f is continuous, then F'(x) = f(x); differentiation and integration are inverse operations.

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 2
concepts.description: If F is any antiderivative of f, then ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = F(b) − F(a); enables exact evaluation of definite integrals.

concepts.name: Integration by Substitution
concepts.description: Reversing the chain rule: ∫f(g(x))g'(x)dx = ∫f(u)du where u = g(x); the most fundamental integration technique.

concepts.name: Integration by Parts
concepts.description: ∫u dv = uv − ∫v du; reverses the product rule; used for products of trig, exponential, log, and polynomial functions.

concepts.name: Trigonometric Integrals
concepts.description: Integration of products of trig functions using Pythagorean identities, double-angle formulas, and reduction formulas; includes ∫sinⁿx dx, ∫tanⁿx sec^m x dx.

concepts.name: Trigonometric Substitution
concepts.description: Substituting x = a sin θ, a tan θ, or a sec θ to eliminate square roots of the forms a²−x², a²+x², x²−a² in integrands.

concepts.name: Partial Fraction Decomposition
concepts.description: Decomposing a rational function p(x)/q(x) (deg p < deg q) into a sum of simpler fractions; enables integration of all rational functions.

concepts.name: Reduction Formulas
concepts.description: Formulas expressing ∫f^n in terms of ∫f^(n-2) or ∫f^(n-1); used for ∫sinⁿx dx, ∫cosⁿx dx, ∫xⁿeˣdx.

concepts.name: Improper Integrals
concepts.description: Definite integrals with infinite limits or integrands with singularities; defined as limits of proper integrals; convergent or divergent.

concepts.name: Area by Integration
concepts.description: Area under y = f(x) from a to b is ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx; area between curves is ∫ₐᵇ [f(x)−g(x)]dx where f ≥ g.

concepts.name: Volumes of Revolution
