concepts.description: Volume of solid formed by rotating a region: disk method V = π∫f²dx; washer method V = π∫(f²−g²)dx; shell method V = 2π∫xf(x)dx.

concepts.name: Arc Length
concepts.description: The length of a curve y = f(x) from a to b: L = ∫ₐᵇ √(1 + [f'(x)]²) dx; generalizes to parametric and polar curves.

concepts.name: Surface Area of Revolution
concepts.description: The surface area generated by rotating y = f(x) about the x-axis: S = 2π∫ₐᵇ f(x)√(1+[f'(x)]²) dx.

concepts.name: Power Series
concepts.description: A series Σcₙ(x−a)ⁿ defining a function on an interval |x−a| < R where R is the radius of convergence; the natural setting for Taylor series.

concepts.name: Radius of Convergence
concepts.description: The radius R such that the power series converges absolutely for |x−a| < R and diverges for |x−a| > R; found via the ratio or root test.

concepts.name: Taylor Series
concepts.description: Representing a smooth function as a power series about a point a: f(x) = Σf^(n)(a)(x−a)ⁿ/n!; valid on the radius of convergence.

concepts.name: Maclaurin Series
concepts.description: Taylor series centered at a = 0; key examples: eˣ = Σxⁿ/n!, sin x = Σ(−1)ⁿx^(2n+1)/(2n+1)!, cos x = Σ(−1)ⁿx^(2n)/(2n)!, 1/(1−x) = Σxⁿ.

concepts.name: Taylor Remainder and Error Bound
concepts.description: The error in approximating f by its nth Taylor polynomial: |Rₙ(x)| ≤ M|x−a|^(n+1)/(n+1)! where M bounds |f^(n+1)|; enables precision control.

concepts.name: Parametric Equations and Curves
concepts.description: Describing a curve by parametric equations x = f(t), y = g(t); enables representation of curves that are not functions, like circles and epicycloids.

concepts.name: Calculus of Parametric Curves
concepts.description: Differentiation: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt); arc length: L = ∫√((dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)²) dt; area under parametric curve.

concepts.name: Introduction to Multivariable Calculus
concepts.description: Extension of calculus to functions of two or more variables; the domain becomes a subset of ℝⁿ and the range ℝᵐ.

concepts.name: Partial Derivatives
concepts.description: The partial derivative ∂f/∂x is the derivative of f with respect to x holding all other variables constant; measures rate of change in one coordinate direction.

concepts.name: Gradient
concepts.description: ∇f = (∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y, …); the vector of partial derivatives pointing in the direction of steepest ascent; its magnitude is the maximum rate of change.

concepts.name: Directional Derivative
aliases: rate of change in direction u
concepts.description: The rate of change of f at a point in the direction of unit vector u: Dᵤf = ∇f · u; maximized when u is parallel to ∇f.

concepts.name: Multivariable Chain Rule
concepts.description: Generalizes the chain rule to functions of multiple variables via a tree of partial derivatives; fundamental for implicit differentiation in multiple variables.

concepts.name: Multivariable Extrema
concepts.description: Critical points where ∇f = 0; classified via the Hessian matrix: D > 0 and fₓₓ > 0 → local min, D > 0 and fₓₓ < 0 → local max, D < 0 → saddle point.

concepts.name: Multiple Integrals
concepts.description: Extensions of the definite integral to functions of two or three variables; computed via iterated integrals using Fubini's theorem.

concepts.name: Double Integrals
concepts.description: ∬_D f(x,y)dA computes volume under a surface over region D; evaluated by Fubini's theorem as an iterated integral; also in polar coordinates.

concepts.name: Triple Integrals
concepts.description: ∭_E f(x,y,z) dV; evaluated via iterated integrals; computable in cylindrical (r,θ,z) and spherical (ρ,φ,θ) coordinates via the Jacobian.

concepts.name: Change of Variables (Jacobian)
concepts.description: For the substitution (x,y) = T(u,v): ∬ f dA = ∬ f(T(u,v))|∂(x,y)/∂(u,v)| du dv; the Jacobian determinant is the scaling factor.

concepts.name: Line Integrals
concepts.description: Integrals along a curve C: scalar line integral ∫_C f ds measures arc-length-weighted sum; vector line integral ∫_C F·dr computes work done by force field F.

concepts.name: Surface Integrals
concepts.description: Integrals over a surface S; scalar: ∬_S f dS; vector (flux): ∬_S F·dS = ∬_S F·n dS; key for Stokes' and Gauss' theorems.

concepts.name: Vector Fields
concepts.description: An assignment of a vector to each point in a region; a field is conservative if F = ∇f for some potential f, in which case ∫_C F·dr is path-independent.

concepts.name: Curl and Divergence
concepts.description: Curl ∇×F measures rotational tendency; divergence ∇·F measures outward flux density; both are differential operators on vector fields.

concepts.name: Green's Theorem
concepts.description: Relates a line integral around a simple closed curve C to a double integral over the region D it encloses; a special case of Stokes' theorem in 2D.

concepts.name: Stokes' Theorem
concepts.description: ∬_S (∇×F)·dS = ∮_C F·dr, where C is the boundary of surface S; generalizes Green's theorem to 3D; a special case of the generalized Stokes' theorem on manifolds.

concepts.name: Divergence Theorem
concepts.description: ∭_E ∇·F dV = ∬_S F·dS, where S is the boundary of solid E; relates the total divergence inside a region to the outward flux through its boundary.

concepts.name: Fourier Series (Introduction)
concepts.description: Representing a periodic function as a sum of sines and cosines: f(x) = a₀/2 + Σ(aₙcos(nx) + bₙsin(nx)); coefficients given by orthogonality integrals.

concepts.name: Limits of Sequences (Calculus)
concepts.description: The limit of a sequence as n → ∞; computed using limit laws, l'Hôpital's rule (via continuous function), and the squeeze theorem.

concepts.name: Ordinary Differential Equation
concepts.description: An equation involving an unknown function of one variable and its derivatives. The order is the highest derivative present; the degree is the power of that highest derivative.

concepts.name: Order of a Differential Equation
concepts.description: The order of an ODE is determined by the highest derivative appearing in it. A first-order ODE involves only y′; second-order involves y″, etc.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.1

concepts.name: Linearity of Differential Equations
concepts.description: An ODE is linear if it can be written as a linear combination of y and its derivatives with coefficients depending only on the independent variable. Nonlinear ODEs include products of y and its derivatives.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.1

concepts.name: Types of Solutions
concepts.description: A general solution contains arbitrary constants equal in number to the ODE order. A particular solution fixes those constants from initial/boundary conditions. Singular solutions are not obtainable from the general solution.
references: Tenenbaum & Pollard, Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.2

concepts.name: Initial Value Problem
concepts.description: An ODE together with conditions specifying the value of the solution and its derivatives at a single point (the initial point). Determines a unique solution under appropriate smoothness conditions.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.2

concepts.name: Existence and Uniqueness Theorem
concepts.description: If f(x,y) and ∂f/∂y are continuous in a rectangle around (x₀,y₀), then the IVP y′=f(x,y), y(x₀)=y₀ has a unique solution in some interval around x₀. Proved via Picard iteration.
references: Coddington & Levinson, Theory of Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.1

concepts.name: First-Order ODE
concepts.description: An ODE involving only the first derivative y′. Solution methods include separation of variables, integrating factors, exact equations, substitution, and Bernoulli's equation technique.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.2

concepts.name: Separable Differential Equation
concepts.description: An ODE of the form dy/dx = g(x)h(y); rearranged as dy/h(y) = g(x)dx and integrated on both sides. One of the most elementary solution techniques.

concepts.name: Linear First-Order ODE
concepts.description: An ODE of the form y′ + P(x)y = Q(x). Solved by multiplying by the integrating factor μ(x)=e^{∫P dx}, making the left side an exact derivative.

concepts.name: Exact Differential Equation
concepts.description: An ODE M(x,y)dx + N(x,y)dy = 0 where ∂M/∂y = ∂N/∂x. Solution is found by identifying a potential function F such that dF=0.
references: Tenenbaum & Pollard, Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.4

concepts.name: Bernoulli Equation
concepts.description: An ODE of the form y′ + P(x)y = Q(x)yⁿ. Linearized by the substitution v = y^{1-n}, converting to a linear first-order ODE in v.

concepts.name: Homogeneous First-Order ODE
concepts.description: An ODE of the form dy/dx = F(y/x). Solved by the substitution v = y/x, transforming it into a separable equation in v and x.
references: Tenenbaum & Pollard, Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.3

concepts.name: Slope Field
concepts.description: A graphical representation of a first-order ODE by drawing short tangent-line segments at a grid of points (x,y), revealing the qualitative behavior of solutions without solving explicitly.

concepts.name: Euler's Method
concepts.description: The simplest numerical method for ODEs: step forward in x by h, updating y by y_{n+1} = y_n + h·f(x_n, y_n). Error per step O(h²); global error O(h).

concepts.name: Second-Order ODE
concepts.description: An ODE involving the second derivative y″. Of primary physical importance (Newton's second law, harmonic oscillators, beam equations). Standard form: y″ + P(x)y′ + Q(x)y = G(x).
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.3

concepts.name: Second-Order Linear ODE
concepts.description: An ODE y″ + P(x)y′ + Q(x)y = G(x). When G=0 it is homogeneous; when G≠0 it is nonhomogeneous. Solutions form a vector space (superposition principle applies for homogeneous case).

concepts.name: Homogeneous Second-Order Linear ODE
concepts.description: The equation y″ + P(x)y′ + Q(x)y = 0. Two linearly independent solutions y₁, y₂ form a fundamental set; general solution is c₁y₁ + c₂y₂. Linearity determined by the Wronskian.

concepts.name: Wronskian
concepts.description: W(y₁,y₂) = y₁y₂′ − y₁′y₂; nonzero at some point iff y₁ and y₂ are linearly independent. Abel's theorem gives W(x) = W(x₀)exp(−∫P dx).

concepts.name: Characteristic Equation
concepts.description: For constant-coefficient ODE ay″+by′+cy=0, substituting y=e^{rx} yields the characteristic equation ar²+br+c=0. The three cases (distinct real, repeated, complex roots) yield distinct solution forms.

concepts.name: Method of Undetermined Coefficients
concepts.description: A technique for finding a particular solution to ay″+by′+cy=G(x) when G(x) is a polynomial, exponential, sine, cosine, or product thereof. Assumes a trial solution of the same form as G(x) and solves for unknown coefficients.

concepts.name: Variation of Parameters
concepts.description: A general technique for finding particular solutions to y″+P(x)y′+Q(x)y=G(x) using the known fundamental set {y₁,y₂}. Sets y_p = u₁y₁+u₂y₂ and imposes conditions to determine u₁′,u₂′ via the Wronskian.

concepts.name: Harmonic Oscillator
concepts.description: The ODE my″+by′+ky=F(t) models spring-mass-damper systems. Cases: undamped (b=0), underdamped (b²<4mk), critically damped (b²=4mk), overdamped (b²>4mk). Each case determines the transient behavior.

concepts.name: Resonance
concepts.description: When the forcing frequency matches the natural frequency of an undamped oscillator, the amplitude grows without bound (pure resonance). In damped systems, resonance occurs at a slightly different frequency (practical resonance).

concepts.name: Higher-Order Linear ODE
concepts.description: An nth-order linear ODE has n linearly independent solutions forming an n-dimensional solution space. Constant-coefficient case solved by characteristic polynomial of degree n; methods generalize from second-order case.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.4

concepts.name: Laplace Transform
concepts.description: The integral transform ℒ{f}(s) = ∫₀^∞ e^{−st}f(t)dt converting differential equations into algebraic equations in s. Transforms derivatives into polynomial multiplication in s.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.6

concepts.name: Laplace Transform Properties
concepts.description: Key properties: linearity (ℒ{af+bg}=aℒ{f}+bℒ{g}), first shifting theorem (ℒ{e^{at}f}(s)=F(s−a)), second shifting theorem (Heaviside step), derivative rule (ℒ{f′}=sF(s)−f(0)), convolution.

concepts.name: Inverse Laplace Transform
concepts.description: The process of recovering f(t) from its Laplace transform F(s). Standard technique: use partial fraction decomposition to match to known transform pairs from tables.

concepts.name: Solving ODEs with Laplace Transform
concepts.description: Workflow: take Laplace transform of ODE to get algebraic equation in Y(s), solve for Y(s), take inverse Laplace to obtain y(t). Initial conditions are automatically incorporated in the transform step.

concepts.name: Convolution Theorem (Laplace)
concepts.description: ℒ{f*g}(s) = F(s)·G(s), where (f*g)(t)=∫₀^t f(τ)g(t−τ)dτ is the convolution. Allows recovery of the inverse Laplace of a product without full partial-fraction decomposition.

concepts.name: Systems of ODEs
concepts.description: A set of ODEs involving multiple dependent variables: x′=Ax+b (linear) or x′=f(x) (nonlinear). First-order systems can represent nth-order scalar ODEs via state-vector reduction.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.7

concepts.name: Matrix Method for Linear Systems
concepts.description: For x′=Ax, solutions are of the form x=e^{λt}v where (λ,v) are eigenvalue-eigenvector pairs of A. Three cases: distinct real, repeated, complex conjugate eigenvalues — each producing a distinct fundamental matrix.

concepts.name: Phase Plane Analysis
concepts.description: For 2D autonomous systems x′=f(x,y), y′=g(x,y), trajectories in the xy-plane (phase plane) reveal global solution behavior. Equilibria classified by eigenvalue structure: nodes, spirals, saddles, centers.
references: Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, Ch.5–6

concepts.name: Stability Analysis
concepts.description: Classification of equilibria of ODEs: stable (Lyapunov), asymptotically stable, or unstable. Linearization near equilibria using the Jacobian determines local stability; Lyapunov functions give global results.
references: Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, Ch.6; Perko, Differential Equations and Dynamical Systems

concepts.name: Series Solution of ODEs
concepts.description: Assumes y=∑aₙxⁿ and substitutes into the ODE to determine the recurrence relation for coefficients aₙ. Valid when x=0 is an ordinary point of the ODE.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.5

concepts.name: Frobenius Method
concepts.description: For ODEs with a regular singular point at x=0, seeks solutions of the form y=xʳ∑aₙxⁿ. The indicial equation determines r. Three subcases based on root difference: distinct non-integer, integer, or equal roots.

concepts.name: Bessel's Equation
concepts.description: x²y″+xy′+(x²−ν²)y=0; solutions are Bessel functions Jᵥ(x) and Yᵥ(x). Arise in problems with cylindrical symmetry; Jᵥ regular at origin, Yᵥ singular.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, §5.7; Watson, Bessel Functions

concepts.name: Legendre's Equation
concepts.description: (1−x²)y″−2xy′+n(n+1)y=0; polynomial solutions are Legendre polynomials Pₙ(x). Arise in problems with spherical symmetry. Form an orthogonal basis on [−1,1].

concepts.name: Sturm-Liouville Theory
concepts.description: The boundary value problem (p(x)y′)′+q(x)y+λw(x)y=0 with boundary conditions. Has countably many eigenvalues λₙ and orthogonal eigenfunctions φₙ forming a complete set in L²([a,b],w).
references: Birkhoff & Rota, Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.10

concepts.name: Eigenfunction Expansion
concepts.description: Expanding a function f in terms of eigenfunctions φₙ of a Sturm-Liouville problem: f(x)=∑cₙφₙ(x) with cₙ=⟨f,φₙ⟩/⟨φₙ,φₙ⟩. Generalizes Fourier series to arbitrary weighted inner products.
references: Birkhoff & Rota, Ordinary Differential Equations, Ch.10

concepts.name: Boundary Value Problem
concepts.description: An ODE with conditions specified at two or more distinct points. Unlike IVPs, existence and uniqueness are not guaranteed; a BVP may have no solution, a unique solution, or infinitely many solutions.

concepts.name: Fourier Series
concepts.description: Represents a periodic function as f(x)=a₀/2+∑(aₙcos(nπx/L)+bₙsin(nπx/L)), with coefficients computed by orthogonality integrals. Cornerstone of classical and modern analysis.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.10; Körner, Fourier Analysis

concepts.name: Convergence of Fourier Series
concepts.description: Dirichlet's theorem: if f is piecewise smooth, the Fourier series converges to f(x) at continuity points and to [f(x⁺)+f(x⁻)]/2 at jumps. Near jump discontinuities, overshoot is ~9% (Gibbs phenomenon).

concepts.name: Fourier Sine and Cosine Series
concepts.description: On [0,L], a function can be extended as an odd function (yielding a pure sine series) or an even function (yielding a pure cosine series), matching different boundary conditions in PDE applications.

concepts.name: Fourier Transform
concepts.description: The integral transform f̂(ω)=∫_{-∞}^∞ f(t)e^{−iωt}dt for non-periodic functions. Converts differentiation to multiplication by iω; used for solving ODEs/PDEs on the whole real line.
references: Boyce & DiPrima, Elementary Differential Equations, Ch.10; Folland, Fourier Analysis

concepts.name: Partial Differential Equation
concepts.description: An equation involving partial derivatives of an unknown function of several variables. Classified (for linear second-order) as elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic based on the discriminant of the principal part.
references: Evans, Partial Differential Equations; Strauss, Partial Differential Equations

concepts.name: Classification of Second-Order PDEs
concepts.description: For Au_{xx}+Bu_{xy}+Cu_{yy}+lower=0, the discriminant B²−4AC determines: elliptic (<0), parabolic (=0), hyperbolic (>0). Each class has distinct mathematical structure and physical interpretation.

concepts.name: Separation of Variables (PDE)
concepts.description: Assumes u(x,t)=X(x)T(t) (or analogous in other variables), splitting the PDE into ODEs for each factor, linked by a separation constant. Yields eigenvalue problems for X, then Fourier series expansions.

concepts.name: Heat Equation
concepts.description: u_t = k·u_{xx} (parabolic). Models heat diffusion and Brownian motion. Solved on finite intervals by separation of variables + Fourier series; on the full line by Fourier transform giving Gaussian kernel solution.
references: Strauss, Partial Differential Equations, Ch.1; Evans, PDE, Ch.2

concepts.name: Wave Equation
concepts.description: u_{tt} = c²u_{xx} (hyperbolic). Models string vibrations, acoustics, and light. D'Alembert's solution u=f(x+ct)+g(x−ct) on the infinite line; Fourier series on finite intervals.

concepts.name: Laplace's Equation
concepts.description: ∇²u = u_{xx}+u_{yy} = 0 (elliptic). Solutions are harmonic functions, satisfying the mean value property and maximum principle. Solved on disks/rectangles by separation; on complex domains by conformal mapping.
references: Evans, PDE, Ch.2; Strauss, PDE, Ch.6

concepts.name: Harmonic Functions
concepts.description: Functions satisfying ∇²u=0. Key properties: mean value property (value at a point equals average over any surrounding sphere/circle), maximum/minimum principle (extrema only on boundary), uniqueness of Dirichlet problem.

concepts.name: Poisson's Equation
concepts.description: ∇²u = f; the nonhomogeneous version of Laplace's equation. Arises in electrostatics (Poisson's equation for potential), Newtonian gravity. Solved by Green's functions when f is a source term.

concepts.name: Green's Function
concepts.description: The Green's function G(x,ξ) is the response to a unit point source: LG=δ(x−ξ). Allows expressing solutions to Lu=f as u(x)=∫G(x,ξ)f(ξ)dξ. Encodes both operator and boundary conditions.
references: Evans, PDE, §2.4; Stakgold & Holst, Green's Functions and Boundary Value Problems

concepts.name: Nonlinear ODE
concepts.description: An ODE in which the unknown function or its derivatives appear nonlinearly. General analytic solutions rarely exist; qualitative theory (phase plane, Lyapunov, bifurcation) and numerical methods are primary tools.

concepts.name: Bifurcation Theory
concepts.description: A bifurcation is a qualitative change in the solution structure of a system as a parameter crosses a critical value. Classic types: saddle-node, pitchfork, transcritical, Hopf. Each has a canonical normal form.
references: Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, Ch.3; Guckenheimer & Holmes, Nonlinear Oscillations

concepts.name: Chaotic Dynamics
concepts.description: Deterministic systems exhibiting sensitive dependence on initial conditions: nearby trajectories diverge exponentially (positive Lyapunov exponent). Lorenz attractor is the canonical example. Not the same as randomness.
references: Strogatz, Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos, Ch.9–12

concepts.name: Vector
concepts.description: An ordered tuple of numbers (a vector in ℝⁿ) or an abstract element of a vector space. In ℝⁿ, vectors represent magnitudes with direction; algebraically they are the fundamental objects of linear algebra.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.1; Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.1

concepts.name: Vector Addition
concepts.description: Component-wise addition: (a₁,…,aₙ)+(b₁,…,bₙ)=(a₁+b₁,…,aₙ+bₙ). Geometrically, the parallelogram law. Satisfies commutativity, associativity, identity (zero vector), and inverse (negation).

concepts.name: Scalar Multiplication
concepts.description: Multiplying a vector by a scalar c: c(a₁,…,aₙ)=(ca₁,…,caₙ). Stretches or compresses the vector; negation reverses direction. Together with addition gives the vector space structure.

concepts.name: Dot Product
concepts.description: a·b = ∑aᵢbᵢ = |a||b|cos θ. Algebraically: sum of products of corresponding components. Geometrically: |a||b|cos(angle). Zero iff vectors are orthogonal.

concepts.name: Vector Norm
concepts.description: |v| = √(v·v) = √(∑vᵢ²). More generally, the p-norm: |v|_p = (∑|vᵢ|^p)^{1/p}. Satisfies positivity, homogeneity, and triangle inequality (defines a metric).

concepts.name: Unit Vector
concepts.description: A vector of norm 1. Obtained by normalizing: v̂ = v/|v|. Standard basis vectors e₁,…,eₙ are the canonical unit vectors in ℝⁿ.

concepts.name: Orthogonality
concepts.description: Two vectors are orthogonal iff their dot product is zero. Orthogonality generalizes perpendicularity to arbitrary inner product spaces. Orthogonal vectors are linearly independent.

concepts.name: Cross Product
concepts.description: Defined in ℝ³: a×b = (a₂b₃−a₃b₂, a₃b₁−a₁b₃, a₁b₂−a₂b₁). Result is perpendicular to both a and b; magnitude is |a||b|sin θ (area of parallelogram). Anti-commutative.

concepts.name: Matrix
concepts.description: A rectangular array of numbers with m rows and n columns. Encodes linear transformations (m×n matrix ↔ linear map ℝⁿ→ℝᵐ), system coefficients, data, and more.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.2

concepts.name: Matrix Addition
concepts.description: Component-wise addition of matrices of the same dimensions. Satisfies all vector space axioms — matrices of the same size form a vector space.

concepts.name: Matrix Multiplication
concepts.description: (AB)_{ij} = ∑_k A_{ik}B_{kj}; requires inner dimensions to match. Non-commutative in general. Associative; distributes over addition. Encodes composition of linear transformations.

concepts.name: Matrix Transpose
concepts.description: (Aᵀ)_{ij} = A_{ji}: rows become columns and vice versa. Key properties: (AB)ᵀ=BᵀAᵀ, (Aᵀ)ᵀ=A. Symmetric matrices satisfy A=Aᵀ.

concepts.name: Symmetric Matrix
concepts.description: A = Aᵀ. Symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors (Spectral Theorem). Ubiquitous in physics, statistics (covariance matrices), and optimization (Hessians).

concepts.name: System of Linear Equations
concepts.description: A set of m linear equations in n unknowns, written Ax=b. Three outcomes: unique solution (consistent, full column rank), no solution (inconsistent), or infinitely many solutions (dependent). Structure is determined by rank of A and [A|b].
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.2

concepts.name: Augmented Matrix
concepts.description: The matrix [A|b] formed by appending the right-hand side column b to the coefficient matrix A. Row operations on [A|b] preserve the solution set and are used to row-reduce to echelon form.

concepts.name: Row Reduction
concepts.description: Three elementary row operations (swap, scale, add multiple of one row to another) transform Ax=b without changing its solution set. Systematic application yields row echelon or reduced row echelon form.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, §2.2; Lay, Linear Algebra, §1.2

concepts.name: Row Echelon Form
concepts.description: REF: zeros below each pivot; RREF: additionally, zeros above each pivot and each pivot equals 1. RREF is unique. Pivot columns correspond to basic variables; non-pivot columns to free variables.
references: Lay, Linear Algebra and Its Applications, §1.2

concepts.name: Rank
concepts.description: The number of pivots in REF; equals the dimension of the column space (column rank) and row space (row rank). Column rank = row rank always. Rank determines existence and uniqueness of solutions to Ax=b.

concepts.name: Matrix Inverse
concepts.description: A square matrix A is invertible iff det(A)≠0, iff rank(A)=n, iff columns are linearly independent. The inverse A⁻¹ satisfies AA⁻¹=A⁻¹A=I. Computed via [A|I] → [I|A⁻¹] by row reduction.

concepts.name: Determinant
concepts.description: A scalar function det: Mₙ(F) → F satisfying: multilinearity in rows, alternating (swap changes sign), det(I)=1. det(A)≠0 iff A invertible. Geometric meaning: signed volume of parallelepiped formed by rows.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.5; Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.10

concepts.name: Cofactor Expansion
concepts.description: det(A) = ∑_j a_{ij}C_{ij} along any row or column, where C_{ij}=(−1)^{i+j}M_{ij} is the cofactor and M_{ij} is the (i,j) minor. Efficient for small matrices; O(n!) complexity — not used for large n.

concepts.name: Properties of Determinants
concepts.description: Key properties: det(AB)=det(A)det(B), det(Aᵀ)=det(A), det(cA)=cⁿdet(A), det(A⁻¹)=1/det(A). Row operations: swap changes sign, scaling multiplies det by scalar, adding row multiples leaves det unchanged.

concepts.name: Cramer's Rule
concepts.description: For Ax=b with det(A)≠0, xᵢ = det(Aᵢ)/det(A), where Aᵢ replaces column i with b. Theoretically important (explicit formula), computationally impractical for large n.

concepts.name: LU Factorization
concepts.description: Decomposes A=LU (or PA=LU with pivoting), where L is lower triangular and U is upper triangular. Enables efficient solution of multiple systems with the same A via forward/back substitution.

concepts.name: Vector Space
concepts.description: A set V with vector addition and scalar multiplication satisfying 8 axioms (closure, commutativity, associativity, identity, inverses, scalar distributivity, vector distributivity, unit scalar). Examples: ℝⁿ, polynomials, function spaces.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.1

concepts.name: Subspace
concepts.description: A subset W of a vector space V that is itself a vector space under the inherited operations. Equivalent condition: W is closed under addition and scalar multiplication and contains the zero vector.

concepts.name: Span
concepts.description: span(S) = the set of all linear combinations of vectors in S. The smallest subspace containing S. A set spans V iff every vector in V can be expressed as a linear combination of vectors in S.

concepts.name: Linear Independence
concepts.description: Vectors v₁,…,vₖ are linearly independent iff c₁v₁+⋯+cₖvₖ=0 implies all cᵢ=0. Equivalently, no vector in the set is a linear combination of the others. Key property for basis construction.

concepts.name: Basis
concepts.description: A set of vectors that is both linearly independent and spans V. Every vector in V is uniquely expressible as a linear combination of basis vectors. The standard basis of ℝⁿ is {e₁,…,eₙ}.

concepts.name: Dimension
concepts.description: The number of vectors in any basis of V; all bases of V have the same cardinality. dim(ℝⁿ)=n. For subspaces: dim(U)+dim(U⊥)=dim(V).

concepts.name: Coordinates
concepts.description: Given an ordered basis β={b₁,…,bₙ}, the coordinate vector [v]_β = (c₁,…,cₙ) where v=∑cᵢbᵢ. Coordinates depend on the choice of basis. Change of basis converts between coordinate representations.

concepts.name: Change of Basis
concepts.description: If β and γ are bases, the change-of-basis matrix P_{β→γ} satisfies [v]_γ = P[v]_β. Matrix representations of a linear map in two bases are related by similarity: B = P⁻¹AP.

concepts.name: Null Space
concepts.description: N(A) = {x : Ax=0}; a subspace of ℝⁿ. Dimension is the nullity of A; its vectors are the solutions to the homogeneous system. Found by row-reducing A and parameterizing free variables.

concepts.name: Column Space
concepts.description: C(A) = {Ax : x∈ℝⁿ}; the span of the columns of A; a subspace of ℝᵐ. Ax=b is consistent iff b∈C(A). dim(C(A)) = rank(A).

concepts.name: Rank-Nullity Theorem
concepts.description: For a linear map T:V→W (or m×n matrix A): rank(T) + nullity(T) = dim(V) (= n for matrices). Constrains the sizes of image and kernel.

concepts.name: Linear Map
concepts.description: A map T:V→W satisfying T(u+v)=T(u)+T(v) and T(cv)=cT(v). Completely determined by its action on a basis. Every linear map between finite-dimensional spaces is represented by a matrix in given bases.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.3

concepts.name: Kernel and Image of Linear Map
concepts.description: ker(T) = {v : T(v)=0} is a subspace of V; im(T) = {T(v) : v∈V} is a subspace of W. T is injective iff ker(T)={0}; T is surjective iff im(T)=W.

concepts.name: Matrix Representation of Linear Map
concepts.description: Given ordered bases β of V and γ of W, the matrix [T]_{γβ} has columns [T(bⱼ)]_γ. Matrix multiplication then corresponds to composition of linear maps: [T∘S]=[T][S].

concepts.name: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
concepts.description: Av=λv for a nonzero vector v; λ is an eigenvalue, v is an eigenvector. Eigenvectors are fixed directions under the transformation; eigenvalues give the scaling factors.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.6; Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.5

concepts.name: Characteristic Polynomial
concepts.description: p(λ) = det(A−λI); eigenvalues are its roots. Degree n polynomial for n×n matrix. Cayley-Hamilton theorem states A satisfies its own characteristic polynomial: p(A)=0.

concepts.name: Eigenspace
concepts.description: E_λ = ker(A−λI) = {v : Av=λv}; a subspace of ℝⁿ. Its dimension is the geometric multiplicity of λ. Algebraic multiplicity ≥ geometric multiplicity; equality holds for diagonalizable matrices.

concepts.name: Diagonalization
concepts.description: A is diagonalizable iff it has n linearly independent eigenvectors. Then A=PDP⁻¹ where D=diag(λ₁,…,λₙ) and P has eigenvectors as columns. Powers: Aᵏ=PDᵏP⁻¹.

concepts.name: Matrix Exponential
concepts.description: e^A = ∑_{k=0}^∞ Aᵏ/k!. For diagonalizable A=PDP⁻¹: e^A = Pe^DP⁻¹. Used to express solutions to x′=Ax as x(t)=e^{At}x(0). Satisfies d/dt e^{At} = Ae^{At}.

concepts.name: Jordan Normal Form
concepts.description: Every matrix over ℂ is similar to a block-diagonal matrix with Jordan blocks Jₖ(λ) on the diagonal. Generalizes diagonalization to non-diagonalizable matrices. Jordan blocks have λ on the diagonal and 1s on the superdiagonal.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.8; Horn & Johnson, Matrix Analysis

concepts.name: Spectral Theorem
concepts.description: Every real symmetric (or complex Hermitian) matrix is orthogonally (unitarily) diagonalizable: A=QΛQᵀ with Q orthogonal and Λ real diagonal. Eigenvalues are real; eigenvectors from different eigenspaces are orthogonal.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.7

concepts.name: Positive Definite Matrix
concepts.description: A symmetric matrix A is positive definite iff vᵀAv>0 for all nonzero v, iff all eigenvalues are positive, iff all leading principal minors are positive (Sylvester's criterion). PSD: ≥0 instead of >0.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, §6.5; Horn & Johnson, Matrix Analysis

concepts.name: Cholesky Decomposition
concepts.description: A positive definite matrix A = LLᵀ where L is lower triangular with positive diagonal. More efficient than LU for symmetric positive definite systems (half the work). Widely used in numerical linear algebra.

concepts.name: Inner Product
concepts.description: A generalization of the dot product: a positive-definite symmetric bilinear form (real) or sesquilinear form (complex) ⟨·,·⟩:V×V→F. Induces a norm |v|=√⟨v,v⟩.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.6

concepts.name: Inner Product Space
concepts.description: A vector space equipped with an inner product. Cauchy-Schwarz inequality: |⟨u,v⟩| ≤ |u||v|. The norm makes it a normed space; if complete, it becomes a Hilbert space.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.6

concepts.name: Orthogonal and Orthonormal Basis
concepts.description: A basis where all vectors are mutually orthogonal (orthogonal basis) or additionally unit vectors (orthonormal basis). In an ONB, coordinates of v are simply ⟨v,eᵢ⟩. Expansion: v=∑⟨v,eᵢ⟩eᵢ.

concepts.name: Gram-Schmidt Process
concepts.description: Iteratively converts a linearly independent set into an orthonormal basis: subtract projections onto previous vectors, then normalize. Foundation of QR factorization.

concepts.name: Orthogonal Projection
concepts.description: The projection of v onto subspace W is the closest point in W to v, with v−proj_W(v) ⊥ W. For one vector: proj_u(v) = (v·u/u·u)u. For a subspace with ONB {e₁,…,eₖ}: proj = ∑⟨v,eᵢ⟩eᵢ.

concepts.name: Least Squares
concepts.description: When Ax=b has no solution, the least squares solution minimizes |Ax−b|². Found by the normal equations AᵀAx̂=Aᵀb. Solution: x̂=(AᵀA)⁻¹Aᵀb when AᵀA is invertible.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, Ch.4

concepts.name: QR Factorization
concepts.description: A = QR where Q is orthogonal (Qᵀ=Q⁻¹) and R is upper triangular. Computed by Gram-Schmidt or Householder reflections. Used for least squares, eigenvalue computation (QR algorithm).
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, §4.4; Golub & Van Loan, Matrix Computations

concepts.name: Singular Value Decomposition
concepts.description: Any m×n matrix A = UΣVᵀ where U (m×m) and V (n×n) are orthogonal and Σ (m×n) is diagonal with non-negative entries (singular values). Most powerful matrix decomposition; foundation of numerical linear algebra and data science.
references: Strang, Introduction to Linear Algebra, §7.1; Golub & Van Loan, Matrix Computations, Ch.8

concepts.name: Singular Values
concepts.description: The singular values σᵢ = √(eigenvalues of AᵀA) = √(eigenvalues of AAᵀ). They generalize eigenvalues to non-square matrices; σ₁ = |A| (operator 2-norm). Encode the 'strength' of each mode of the transformation.

concepts.name: Moore-Penrose Pseudoinverse
concepts.description: A⁺ = VΣ⁺Uᵀ where Σ⁺ inverts each nonzero singular value and leaves zero singular values as zero. Gives the minimum-norm least squares solution x̂=A⁺b for any system Ax=b.

concepts.name: Tensor
concepts.description: A multilinear map T: V×⋯×V×V*×⋯×V*→F of type (r,s), where r contravariant and s covariant indices. Includes scalars (0,0), vectors (1,0), dual vectors (0,1), and matrices (1,1). Tensor product ⊗ constructs higher-rank tensors.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Appendix; Misner, Thorne & Wheeler, Gravitation, Ch.2 (tensors)

concepts.name: Dual Space
concepts.description: V* = Hom(V,F), the space of all linear functionals on V. dim(V*)=dim(V). Dual basis {f¹,…,fⁿ} satisfies fⁱ(eⱼ)=δⁱⱼ. Key in differential geometry (1-forms) and functional analysis.
references: Axler, Linear Algebra Done Right, Ch.3

concepts.name: Distance in Vector Spaces
concepts.description: d(u,v) = |u−v|. Euclidean distance in ℝⁿ. The norm induces a metric satisfying non-negativity, symmetry, and triangle inequality.

concepts.name: Angle Between Vectors
concepts.description: cos θ = (a·b)/(|a||b|). θ=π/2 iff a⊥b. Cauchy-Schwarz inequality guarantees this value is in [−1,1], ensuring θ is well-defined.

concepts.name: Sample Space
concepts.description: The set Ω of all possible outcomes of a random experiment. May be finite, countably infinite, or uncountable. Each point ω∈Ω is an elementary outcome.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.2; Billingsley, Probability and Measure

concepts.name: Event
concepts.description: A subset A⊆Ω to which a probability can be assigned. For finite/countable Ω, every subset is an event. For uncountable Ω, events form a σ-algebra (collection closed under complements and countable unions).
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.2

concepts.name: Probability Measure
concepts.description: A function P: events → [0,1] satisfying the probability axioms. A probability space is a triple (Ω, ℱ, P). P is a special case of a measure with P(Ω)=1.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.2

concepts.name: Axioms of Probability
concepts.description: Kolmogorov's three axioms: (1) P(A)≥0 for all events A, (2) P(Ω)=1, (3) P(A∪B)=P(A)+P(B) for mutually exclusive A,B. All probability results follow from these three axioms.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.2; Feller, Introduction to Probability Theory, Vol.1

concepts.name: Classical Probability
concepts.description: When outcomes are equally likely: P(A) = |A|/|Ω|. Requires counting favorable vs total outcomes. Foundation of historical probability theory; applies to dice, cards, urns.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.2

concepts.name: Combinatorial Probability
concepts.description: Computing P(A) by counting: using permutations, combinations, and the multiplication/addition principles. Common setups: sampling with/without replacement, ordered/unordered selection.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.2–3

concepts.name: Conditional Probability
concepts.description: P(A|B) = P(A∩B)/P(B) for P(B)>0. The probability of A given that B has occurred. Updates the sample space from Ω to B. Foundation of Bayesian reasoning.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.3

concepts.name: Law of Total Probability
concepts.description: If {B₁,…,Bₙ} partitions Ω, then P(A) = ∑P(A|Bᵢ)P(Bᵢ). Breaks a complex probability into conditional probabilities over a partition of the sample space.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §3.3

concepts.name: Bayes' Theorem
concepts.description: P(Bᵢ|A) = P(A|Bᵢ)P(Bᵢ)/∑P(A|Bⱼ)P(Bⱼ). Updates prior probability P(Bᵢ) to posterior P(Bᵢ|A) given evidence A. Foundational to Bayesian statistics.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §3.4

concepts.name: Independence
concepts.description: Events A and B are independent iff P(A∩B)=P(A)P(B), equivalently P(A|B)=P(A). Mutual independence of n events: every subset product formula holds. Pairwise independence does not imply mutual independence.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §3.5

concepts.name: Bayesian Inference
concepts.description: A framework treating parameters as random variables with prior distributions. After observing data, apply Bayes' theorem to obtain the posterior. Posterior ∝ likelihood × prior. Foundation of Bayesian statistics and machine learning.
references: Gelman et al., Bayesian Data Analysis

concepts.name: Random Variable
concepts.description: A measurable function X:Ω→ℝ assigning a real number to each outcome. Discrete RV has countable range; continuous RV has uncountable range. Encodes probabilistic information in numerical form.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.4

concepts.name: Discrete Random Variable
concepts.description: A random variable with countably many possible values. Characterized by a probability mass function (PMF) p(x)=P(X=x), where ∑p(x)=1.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.4

concepts.name: Continuous Random Variable
concepts.description: A random variable whose CDF F(x) is differentiable, with density f(x)=F′(x). P(a≤X≤b)=∫_a^b f(x)dx. Individual values have probability zero; probability is computed over intervals.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.5

concepts.name: Probability Mass Function
concepts.description: p(x) = P(X=x) for each value x in the range of a discrete RV. Satisfies p(x)≥0 and ∑p(x)=1. Completely characterizes the distribution of a discrete RV.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.4

concepts.name: Probability Density Function
concepts.description: f(x) ≥ 0 with ∫_{-∞}^∞ f(x)dx = 1. P(a≤X≤b) = ∫_a^b f(x)dx. Not a probability itself — f(x) can exceed 1. Completely characterizes the distribution of a continuous RV.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.5

concepts.name: Cumulative Distribution Function
concepts.description: F(x) = P(X≤x). Right-continuous, non-decreasing, F(−∞)=0, F(∞)=1. Unified description for discrete and continuous RVs. Connects PMF/PDF via differentiation and summation.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.4–5

concepts.name: Quantile
concepts.description: The p-quantile (or 100p-th percentile) is the value xₚ such that F(xₚ)=p. The 0.5-quantile is the median. Quartiles Q1, Q2, Q3 correspond to p=0.25, 0.5, 0.75.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.5

concepts.name: Probability Distribution
concepts.description: The complete probabilistic description of a random variable, given by its CDF (or PMF/PDF). Named distributions arise as models for specific types of random phenomena.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.4–5

concepts.name: Discrete Distributions
concepts.description: Key named discrete distributions: Bernoulli (single trial, success prob p), Binomial Bin(n,p) (n independent Bernoulli trials), Poisson Poi(λ) (rare events), Geometric (trials until first success), Negative Binomial, Hypergeometric (sampling without replacement).
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.4

concepts.name: Continuous Distributions
concepts.description: Key named continuous distributions: Uniform U(a,b), Exponential Exp(λ) (memoryless waiting times), Normal N(μ,σ²) (ubiquitous via CLT), Gamma Γ(α,β), Beta B(α,β) (probabilities as RVs), Chi-squared χ²(k), t-distribution (means inference), F-distribution (variance ratios).
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.5

concepts.name: Normal Distribution
concepts.description: f(x) = (1/σ√(2π)) exp(−(x−μ)²/(2σ²)). Symmetric, bell-shaped. Determined by mean μ and variance σ². Appears as the limit distribution in the CLT. Standard normal Z=N(0,1).
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §5.4

concepts.name: Standard Normal Distribution
concepts.description: Z=N(0,1) with pdf φ(z)=(1/√(2π))e^{−z²/2} and CDF Φ(z). Standardization: X∼N(μ,σ²) → Z=(X−μ)/σ. Probabilities computed via z-table or numerical approximation.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §5.4

concepts.name: Expected Value
concepts.description: E[X] = ∑x·P(X=x) (discrete) or ∫x·f(x)dx (continuous). The center of mass of the distribution. Fundamental measure of the average value of a random variable.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.7

concepts.name: Linearity of Expectation
concepts.description: E[aX+bY] = aE[X]+bE[Y] for any RVs X,Y (even dependent) and constants a,b. Powerful tool for computing expectations without knowing the joint distribution. Extends to finite sums.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §7.2

concepts.name: Law of the Unconscious Statistician
concepts.description: E[g(X)] = ∑g(x)P(X=x) (discrete) or ∫g(x)f(x)dx (continuous), computed without first finding the distribution of g(X). Streamlines computation of expected values of transformed RVs.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §7.2

concepts.name: Variance
concepts.description: Var(X) = E[(X−μ)²] = E[X²]−(E[X])². Measures spread/dispersion. Key identities: Var(aX+b) = a²Var(X); Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y)+2Cov(X,Y).
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §7.4

concepts.name: Standard Deviation
concepts.description: σ = √Var(X). Same units as X. The most common measure of spread. ~68% of data within 1σ of mean for normal distributions (68-95-99.7 rule).
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §7.4

concepts.name: Moments
concepts.description: The kth moment of X is E[Xᵏ]; the kth central moment is E[(X−μ)ᵏ]. Skewness = E[(X−μ)³]/σ³; kurtosis = E[(X−μ)⁴]/σ⁴ − 3. Moments characterize distributions.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §7.7

concepts.name: Moment Generating Function
concepts.description: M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}] when finite in a neighborhood of 0. k-th derivative at 0 gives E[Xᵏ]. Uniquely determines the distribution. Sum of independent RVs: M_{X+Y} = M_X·M_Y.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §7.7

concepts.name: Characteristic Function
concepts.description: φ_X(t) = E[e^{itX}]. Always exists (unlike MGF). Uniquely determines the distribution. Inversion formula: f(x) = (1/2π)∫φ_X(t)e^{−itx}dt. Used in proofs of CLT.

concepts.name: Covariance
concepts.description: Cov(X,Y) = E[(X−μ_X)(Y−μ_Y)] = E[XY]−E[X]E[Y]. Positive: X,Y tend to be above/below means together; negative: opposite. Zero for independent RVs (but not converse).
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §7.4

concepts.name: Correlation
concepts.description: ρ(X,Y) = Cov(X,Y)/(σ_X σ_Y) ∈ [−1,1]. Dimensionless measure of linear dependence. |ρ|=1 iff Y=aX+b linearly. ρ=0 means uncorrelated (not necessarily independent).
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §7.4

concepts.name: Joint Distribution
concepts.description: The joint distribution of (X,Y) is given by F(x,y)=P(X≤x, Y≤y). For discrete: joint PMF p(x,y)=P(X=x,Y=y). For continuous: joint PDF f(x,y) with P∈A=∬_A f dxdy.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, Ch.6

concepts.name: Marginal Distribution
concepts.description: The distribution of one RV obtained from the joint distribution by summing/integrating out the other: f_X(x) = ∫f(x,y)dy. X and Y are independent iff f(x,y) = f_X(x)f_Y(y).
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §6.1

concepts.name: Conditional Distribution
concepts.description: f_{X|Y}(x|y) = f(x,y)/f_Y(y). The distribution of X given Y=y. Conditional expectation E[X|Y=y] = ∫x f_{X|Y}(x|y)dx generalizes conditional probability.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §6.3

concepts.name: Conditional Expectation
concepts.description: E[X|Y] is a random variable, function of Y. Tower property (law of total expectation): E[X] = E[E[X|Y]]. Law of total variance: Var(X)=E[Var(X|Y)]+Var(E[X|Y]).
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §7.5; Williams, Probability with Martingales

concepts.name: Chebyshev's Inequality
concepts.description: P(|X−μ|≥kσ) ≤ 1/k². Provides a distribution-free bound on the probability of deviating from the mean. Weaker than distribution-specific bounds but universally applicable.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §8.2

concepts.name: Markov's Inequality
concepts.description: For non-negative X: P(X≥a) ≤ E[X]/a. The most basic probabilistic inequality; implies Chebyshev by applying to (X−μ)².
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §8.1

concepts.name: Law of Large Numbers
concepts.description: WLLN: X̄ₙ →^P μ (convergence in probability). SLLN: X̄ₙ →^{a.s.} μ (almost sure convergence). For iid RVs with E[X]=μ. Justifies using sample means to estimate population means.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §8.2; Billingsley, Probability and Measure, Ch.6

concepts.name: Central Limit Theorem
concepts.description: For iid RVs Xᵢ with mean μ and variance σ²: √n(X̄ₙ−μ)/σ →^d N(0,1). The sum of many independent RVs is approximately normal regardless of their individual distributions. Foundation of statistical inference.
references: Ross, A First Course in Probability, §8.3; Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples

concepts.name: Modes of Convergence
concepts.description: Four modes: (1) almost sure (a.s.): P(Xₙ→X)=1; (2) in probability: P(|Xₙ−X|>ε)→0; (3) in Lp: E[|Xₙ−X|^p]→0; (4) in distribution: Fₙ(x)→F(x) at continuity points. Hierarchy: a.s. ⟹ in prob ⟹ in distribution.

concepts.name: Probability Generating Function
concepts.description: G_X(z) = E[z^X] = ∑P(X=k)z^k for non-negative integer-valued RVs. Derivatives at 0 give factorial moments. Product of PGFs corresponds to sum of independent RVs.
references: Feller, Introduction to Probability Theory, Vol.1, Ch.11

concepts.name: Markov Chain
concepts.description: A sequence of RVs {Xₙ} where P(Xₙ₊₁=j|X₀,…,Xₙ) = P(Xₙ₊₁=j|Xₙ) (Markov property). Future depends only on present state, not history. Characterized by state space and transition matrix.
references: Ross, Introduction to Probability Models, Ch.4; Norris, Markov Chains

concepts.name: Transition Matrix
concepts.description: P = [pᵢⱼ] where pᵢⱼ = P(Xₙ₊₁=j|Xₙ=i). Row-stochastic: each row sums to 1, all entries ≥0. n-step transition probability: P^n. Key tool for computing multi-step probabilities.

concepts.name: Stationary Distribution
concepts.description: A distribution π such that πP=π. If an irreducible, positive recurrent chain exists, π is unique. At stationarity, the chain's distribution does not change. Computed by solving πP=π, ∑πᵢ=1.

concepts.name: Ergodic Theorem (Markov Chains)
concepts.description: An irreducible, aperiodic Markov chain converges to its unique stationary distribution: Pⁿ_{ij} → πⱼ. Time averages converge to ensemble averages under π (ergodic theorem). Foundation of MCMC methods.

concepts.name: Martingale
concepts.description: A sequence {Mₙ} is a martingale if E[Mₙ₊₁|M₀,…,Mₙ]=Mₙ (fairness condition). Submartingale: ≥; supermartingale: ≤. Optional stopping theorem and martingale convergence are key results.
references: Williams, Probability with Martingales; Durrett, Probability: Theory and Examples, Ch.5

concepts.name: Poisson Process
concepts.description: A counting process N(t) with N(0)=0, independent increments, and N(t)−N(s)∼Poi(λ(t−s)). Interarrival times are iid Exp(λ). Models rare-event streams: phone calls, radioactive decays, web requests.
references: Ross, Introduction to Probability Models, Ch.5

concepts.name: Population and Sample
concepts.description: A population is the complete set of units of interest; a sample is a subset drawn from it. Statistical inference uses sample data to draw conclusions about the population. Sampling must be representative to be valid.
references: DeGroot & Schervish, Probability and Statistics, Ch.1; Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics

concepts.name: Descriptive Statistics
concepts.description: Methods for summarizing and displaying data without inference. Includes numerical summaries (mean, median, standard deviation) and graphical displays (histograms, boxplots, scatterplots).
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, Ch.1

concepts.name: Measures of Center
concepts.description: Mean (arithmetic average): x̄ = (∑xᵢ)/n. Median: middle value when sorted. Mode: most frequent value. Trimmed mean: removes extreme values. Mean is sensitive to outliers; median is robust.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, §1.2

concepts.name: Measures of Spread
concepts.description: Range = max − min. IQR = Q3 − Q1 (robust). Sample variance s² = ∑(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) (Bessel's correction for unbiasedness). Sample SD s = √s². Five-number summary: min, Q1, median, Q3, max.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, §1.2

concepts.name: Percentile
concepts.description: The p-th percentile is the value below which p% of the data fall. Q1=25th percentile, Q2=median=50th, Q3=75th. IQR=Q3−Q1.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, §1.2

concepts.name: Data Visualization
concepts.description: Graphical data summaries: histogram (frequency distribution), boxplot (five-number summary with outlier detection), scatterplot (bivariate relationship), bar chart (categorical data), stemplot, Q-Q plot (normality check).
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, Ch.1

concepts.name: Sampling Methods
concepts.description: SRS (simple random sample): each unit equally likely to be selected. Stratified: population divided into strata, SRS within each. Cluster: sample entire clusters. Systematic, multistage. Selection bias if method is non-random.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, Ch.3

concepts.name: Sampling Distribution
concepts.description: The probability distribution of a statistic (e.g., X̄, S², p̂) over all possible samples of size n. For X̄: E[X̄]=μ, SD(X̄)=σ/√n (standard error). By CLT, X̄ is approximately normal for large n.
references: DeGroot & Schervish, Probability and Statistics, Ch.6

concepts.name: Standard Error
concepts.description: SE(X̄) = σ/√n (known σ) or s/√n (estimated from sample). Measures variability of the sample statistic. Decreases as n increases. SE is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, §3.3

concepts.name: Estimator
concepts.description: A rule (function of sample data) for estimating a population parameter. An estimate is the specific value from a given sample. Properties: unbiasedness (E[θ̂]=θ), consistency, efficiency, sufficiency.
references: DeGroot & Schervish, Probability and Statistics, Ch.7

concepts.name: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
concepts.description: Bias = E[θ̂]−θ. Variance = E[(θ̂−E[θ̂])²]. MSE = Bias² + Variance. Unbiased estimators (bias=0) may have high variance; biased estimators can have lower MSE. Core concept in statistical learning.

concepts.name: Consistency of Estimators
concepts.description: θ̂ₙ is consistent iff θ̂ₙ →^P θ as n→∞. Sufficient condition: bias→0 and variance→0. By LLN, sample mean is consistent for population mean.

concepts.name: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
concepts.description: Chooses θ̂ = argmax L(θ|data) where L(θ) = ∏f(xᵢ|θ) is the likelihood. Maximized by setting the score (∂log L/∂θ) to zero. MLEs are consistent, asymptotically normal, and asymptotically efficient.
references: DeGroot & Schervish, Probability and Statistics, Ch.7

concepts.name: Method of Moments
concepts.description: Sets theoretical moments E[Xᵏ] equal to sample moments (∑xᵢᵏ)/n and solves for parameters. Simple to compute; less efficient than MLE but useful when likelihood is intractable.

concepts.name: Confidence Interval
concepts.description: An interval [L,U] such that P(L≤θ≤U)=1−α (confidence level). The 95% CI for μ with known σ: x̄ ± 1.96σ/√n. Interpretation: in repeated sampling, 95% of such intervals contain θ.
references: DeGroot & Schervish, Probability and Statistics, Ch.8; Moore & McCabe, IPS, Ch.6

concepts.name: Confidence Interval for a Mean
concepts.description: Known σ: x̄ ± z_{α/2}·σ/√n (z-interval). Unknown σ, estimated by s: x̄ ± t_{α/2,n-1}·s/√n (t-interval using t-distribution with n−1 df). Width decreases with larger n.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, §7.1

concepts.name: Confidence Interval for a Proportion
concepts.description: p̂ ± z_{α/2}√(p̂(1−p̂)/n). Valid when np̂≥10 and n(1−p̂)≥10 (large sample condition). Uses normal approximation to binomial distribution.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, §8.1

concepts.name: Hypothesis Testing
concepts.description: Formal procedure to decide between H₀ (null) and H₁ (alternative). Steps: state hypotheses, collect data, compute test statistic, find p-value or compare to critical region, make decision. Foundation of frequentist inference.
references: DeGroot & Schervish, Probability and Statistics, Ch.9; Moore & McCabe, IPS, Ch.6

concepts.name: Test Statistic
concepts.description: A function of the data measuring evidence against H₀. Under H₀, the test statistic has a known distribution (z, t, χ², F). The observed value is compared to the null distribution to compute a p-value.

concepts.name: p-value
concepts.description: P(test statistic ≥ observed | H₀). Small p-value is evidence against H₀; reject when p<α (significance level). The p-value is NOT P(H₀ is true) — that is a Bayesian quantity.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, §6.2

concepts.name: Type I and Type II Errors
concepts.description: Type I error: reject H₀ when true (false positive); rate α (significance level). Type II error: fail to reject H₀ when false (false negative); rate β. Tradeoff: decreasing α increases β for fixed n.

concepts.name: Power of a Test
concepts.description: Power = P(reject H₀ | H₁ true) = 1−β. Increases with sample size, effect size, and α. Power analysis used for sample size determination before collecting data.

concepts.name: z-Test
concepts.description: Tests H₀: μ=μ₀ with known σ. Test statistic Z=(X̄−μ₀)/(σ/√n)~N(0,1) under H₀. Compare to z_{α/2} for two-tailed, z_α for one-tailed. Also used for proportion tests.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, §6.2

concepts.name: t-Test
concepts.description: Tests H₀: μ=μ₀ with unknown σ. Test statistic T=(X̄−μ₀)/(S/√n)~t(n−1). Two-sample t for comparing two means (pooled or Welch). Paired t-test for matched pairs. Robust to normality violation for large n.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, §7.1

concepts.name: Chi-Squared Test
concepts.description: Goodness-of-fit: tests if observed frequencies match expected. Independence: tests if two categorical variables are independent in a contingency table. Statistic: χ²=∑(O−E)²/E~χ²(df) under H₀.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, Ch.9

concepts.name: Analysis of Variance
concepts.description: Tests equality of means across k≥2 groups. Partitions total variability: SS_Total = SS_Between + SS_Within. Test statistic F = MS_Between/MS_Within ~ F(k−1, N−k) under H₀. Requires equal variances and approximate normality.
references: DeGroot & Schervish, Probability and Statistics, Ch.11

concepts.name: Two-Way ANOVA
concepts.description: Extends one-way ANOVA to two categorical factors and their interaction. Tests main effects of each factor and the interaction effect. Allows for blocked designs to reduce residual variance.
references: Montgomery, Design and Analysis of Experiments, Ch.5

concepts.name: Sample Correlation
concepts.description: r = ∑(xᵢ−x̄)(yᵢ−ȳ) / √[∑(xᵢ−x̄)²∑(yᵢ−ȳ)²] ∈ [−1,1]. Measures strength and direction of linear association. r²=coefficient of determination. Testing H₀:ρ=0 uses t-statistic with n−2 df.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, Ch.2

concepts.name: Simple Linear Regression
concepts.description: Models Y = β₀ + β₁X + ε, ε~N(0,σ²). OLS estimates: β̂₁ = Sxy/Sxx, β̂₀ = ȳ − β̂₁x̄. Inference on β₁: t-statistic, CI. R² = SS_Reg/SS_Total measures fit. Residual diagnostics check assumptions.
references: DeGroot & Schervish, Probability and Statistics, Ch.11; Faraway, Linear Models with R

concepts.name: Multiple Linear Regression
concepts.description: y = Xβ + ε with β̂ = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy (OLS). Adjusted R², F-test for overall significance, t-tests per coefficient. Multicollinearity, influential points, residual diagnostics. Model selection: AIC, BIC, stepwise.
references: Faraway, Linear Models with R; Weisberg, Applied Linear Regression

concepts.name: Covariance Matrix
concepts.description: Σ_{ij} = Cov(Xᵢ,Xⱼ). Symmetric positive semidefinite matrix. Diagonal: Σ_{ii} = Var(Xᵢ). Generalization of variance to multivariate random vectors. Key in multivariate normal distribution.
references: Anderson, Introduction to Multivariate Statistical Analysis

concepts.name: Normal Distribution (Statistics)
concepts.description: The N(μ,σ²) distribution underpins most parametric tests. Assessed by Q-Q plots, Shapiro-Wilk test. 68-95-99.7 rule. Sample mean from any distribution is approximately normal for large n (CLT).
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, §1.3

concepts.name: Normal Approximation
concepts.description: Bin(n,p) ≈ N(np, np(1−p)) for large n. Poisson(λ) ≈ N(λ,λ) for large λ. Continuity correction: P(X≤k)=P(X≤k+0.5) improves approximation for discrete distributions.
references: Moore & McCabe, Introduction to the Practice of Statistics, §5.2

concepts.name: Nonparametric Tests
concepts.description: Tests that make no parametric distributional assumptions. Sign test, Wilcoxon signed-rank test (paired data), Mann-Whitney U test (two samples), Kruskal-Wallis (k samples). Based on ranks rather than exact values.

concepts.name: Bayesian Statistics
concepts.description: Treats parameters as random with prior π(θ). After data x, posterior π(θ|x) ∝ L(x|θ)π(θ). Inference based on posterior: point estimates (MAP, posterior mean), credible intervals. Contrasts with frequentist CI/p-value paradigm.
references: Gelman et al., Bayesian Data Analysis; DeGroot & Schervish, Probability and Statistics, Ch.7

concepts.name: Conjugate Prior
concepts.description: A prior family closed under Bayesian updating: when likelihood is from the conjugate family, the posterior has the same distributional form as the prior with updated parameters. E.g., Beta prior + Binomial likelihood → Beta posterior.
references: Gelman et al., Bayesian Data Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.name: Credible Interval
concepts.description: A 95% credible interval [a,b] satisfies P(θ∈[a,b]|data)=0.95. Unlike frequentist CI, this is a direct probability statement about the parameter. Highest Posterior Density (HPD) intervals are narrowest credible intervals.
references: Gelman et al., Bayesian Data Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.name: Experimental Design
concepts.description: Principles: randomization (assign treatments randomly), replication (sufficient sample size), blocking (group similar units to reduce noise). Key designs: CRD, RCBD, factorial. Controls for confounding; enables causal inference.
references: Montgomery, Design and Analysis of Experiments

concepts.name: Sufficient Statistic
concepts.description: T(X) is sufficient for θ if the conditional distribution of X|T(X)=t doesn't depend on θ. Factorization criterion: f(x|θ) = g(T(x)|θ)·h(x). A sufficient statistic captures all information about θ in the sample.

concepts.name: Rao-Blackwell Theorem
concepts.description: If θ̂ is an unbiased estimator and T is sufficient for θ, then θ̃ = E[θ̂|T] is also unbiased and has MSE no greater than θ̂. Conditioning on a sufficient statistic always improves or maintains an estimator.

concepts.name: Counting Principles
concepts.description: Multiplication principle: if task A can be done in m ways and task B in n ways independently, then A and B together can be done in m·n ways. Addition principle: m+n ways if tasks are mutually exclusive.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, Ch.6; Brualdi, Introductory Combinatorics

concepts.name: Permutations
concepts.description: P(n,r) = n!/(n−r)! ordered arrangements of r from n distinct objects. Circular permutations: (n−1)!. Permutations with repetition: nʳ. Permutations with identical objects: n!/(n₁!n₂!⋯).
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §6.3

concepts.name: Combinations
concepts.description: C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n−r)!); the number of unordered selections of r from n. Pascal's identity: C(n,r)=C(n−1,r−1)+C(n−1,r). Combinatorial identity: ∑C(n,k)=2ⁿ.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §6.4

concepts.name: Binomial Theorem
concepts.description: (x+y)ⁿ = ∑_{k=0}^n C(n,k) xᵏ y^{n-k}. Coefficients are binomial coefficients. Multinomial theorem generalizes to (x₁+⋯+xₘ)ⁿ. Applications: probability, approximation.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §6.4

concepts.name: Combinatorics
concepts.description: The study of counting, arrangement, and combination of objects. Techniques: direct counting, bijections, recursion, generating functions. Applied to probability, algorithms, and coding theory.

concepts.name: Stars and Bars
concepts.description: The number of ways to place n identical balls into k distinct bins is C(n+k−1, k−1). Models solutions to x₁+x₂+⋯+xₖ=n in non-negative integers. With at-least-one constraint: C(n−1, k−1).
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §6.5

concepts.name: Pigeonhole Principle
concepts.description: If n+1 objects are placed into n containers, at least one contains ≥2 objects. Generalized: at least one container has ≥⌈N/k⌉ objects. Used to prove existence of coincidences.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §6.2

concepts.name: Inclusion-Exclusion Principle
concepts.description: |A₁∪⋯∪Aₙ| = ∑|Aᵢ| − ∑|Aᵢ∩Aⱼ| + ⋯ (alternating). Applied to count objects with at least one property by inclusion/exclusion. Used to derive Euler's totient, derangement formula.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §8.5

concepts.name: Derangements
concepts.description: D(n) = n!∑_{k=0}^n (−1)ᵏ/k! ≈ n!/e. The number of permutations with no fixed point. Derived via inclusion-exclusion. D(n)/n! → 1/e as n→∞; probability a random permutation is a derangement.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §8.5

concepts.name: Recurrence Relation
concepts.description: An equation defining a sequence aₙ in terms of earlier terms. Example: Fibonacci: Fₙ=Fₙ₋₁+Fₙ₋₂. Can often be solved in closed form (characteristic equation) or via generating functions.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, Ch.8

concepts.name: Linear Recurrence
concepts.description: aₙ = c₁aₙ₋₁+⋯+cₖaₙ₋ₖ. Characteristic polynomial r^k−c₁r^{k-1}−⋯=0; distinct roots rᵢ → aₙ=∑Aᵢrᵢⁿ. Repeated roots: polynomial multipliers. Nonhomogeneous case: particular + homogeneous solution.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §8.2

concepts.name: Divide-and-Conquer Recurrence
concepts.description: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). Master Theorem gives asymptotic solution in three cases depending on comparison of f(n) to n^{log_b a}. Applies to analysis of recursive algorithms (merge sort, binary search).
references: Cormen et al., Introduction to Algorithms, Ch.4

concepts.name: Generating Functions
concepts.description: A power series A(x)=∑aₙxⁿ encoding a sequence {aₙ}. Allows algebraic manipulation to find closed forms, count combinations, solve recurrences. Exponential GF: B(x)=∑aₙxⁿ/n! for labeled structures.

concepts.name: Ordinary Generating Function
concepts.description: A(x) = ∑_{n≥0} aₙxⁿ for unlabeled combinatorial structures. Product of OGFs corresponds to convolution of sequences. Partial fractions decompose rational GFs into partial sums, yielding linear recurrence solutions.

concepts.name: Exponential Generating Function
concepts.description: B(x) = ∑_{n≥0} bₙxⁿ/n! for labeled structures. Product of EGFs corresponds to labeled union. eˣ is the EGF for permutations; e^{eˣ−1} for set partitions. Essential for counting labeled objects.
references: Wilf, generatingfunctionology, Ch.2; Stanley, Enumerative Combinatorics, Vol.2

concepts.name: Graph
concepts.description: A graph G=(V,E) consists of vertices V and edges E⊆V×V. Undirected: edges are unordered pairs; directed: ordered pairs. Degree of vertex: number of incident edges. Handshaking lemma: ∑deg = 2|E|.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, Ch.10; Diestel, Graph Theory

concepts.name: Types of Graphs
concepts.description: Simple graph (no loops/multi-edges), multigraph (multiple edges allowed). Complete graph Kₙ: every pair connected. Bipartite: vertices split into two sets with edges only between sets. Regular: all vertices same degree.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §10.2

concepts.name: Graph Representation
concepts.description: Adjacency matrix: A_{ij}=1 iff (i,j)∈E. Adjacency list: list of neighbors for each vertex. Incidence matrix: rows=vertices, columns=edges. Matrix representation enables algebraic graph theory.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §10.3

concepts.name: Graph Connectivity
concepts.description: A path is a sequence of distinct vertices connected by edges. A cycle is a closed path. A graph is connected if there is a path between every pair of vertices. Strongly connected (directed): path from every vertex to every other.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §10.4

concepts.name: Euler and Hamiltonian Paths
concepts.description: Eulerian circuit: traverses every edge exactly once; exists iff connected with all even-degree vertices (Euler's theorem). Hamiltonian cycle: visits every vertex exactly once; NP-complete to determine existence.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §10.5

concepts.name: Trees
concepts.description: A tree is a connected acyclic graph. Properties: n vertices → n−1 edges; unique path between any two vertices. Rooted tree: one vertex designated root, gives parent-child hierarchy. Binary tree: each node has at most 2 children.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §11.1

concepts.name: Spanning Tree
concepts.description: A spanning tree of G uses all vertices and n−1 edges with no cycles. For weighted graphs, the minimum spanning tree (MST) minimizes total edge weight. Kruskal's algorithm: greedy by edge weight; Prim's: greedy from a starting vertex.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §11.5; Cormen et al., Introduction to Algorithms, Ch.23

concepts.name: Graph Coloring
concepts.description: Assign colors to vertices so no adjacent vertices share a color. The chromatic number χ(G) is the minimum number of colors needed. Four Color Theorem: χ ≤ 4 for planar graphs. Greedy algorithms give upper bounds.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §10.8

concepts.name: Planar Graph
concepts.description: A graph that can be drawn in the plane without edge crossings. Euler's formula: V−E+F=2 (V vertices, E edges, F faces). Kuratowski's theorem: planar iff no K₅ or K₃,₃ subdivision. At most 3V−6 edges.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §10.7

concepts.name: Propositional Logic
concepts.description: The study of logical connectives (∧, ∨, ¬, →, ↔) and their truth-functional properties. Normal forms: DNF, CNF. SAT (satisfiability) problem: determine if a formula is satisfiable. Foundation of digital circuit design.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, Ch.1

concepts.name: Boolean Circuits and Logic Gates
concepts.description: Electronic implementation of Boolean functions using AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR, XOR gates. Any Boolean function has a circuit representation. Minimization via Karnaugh maps or Quine-McCluskey algorithm.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, §12.3

concepts.name: Predicate Logic and Proof Methods
concepts.description: First-order logic with quantifiers ∀ and ∃. Proof techniques: direct, contrapositive, contradiction, induction, counterexample. Proof by induction especially critical in discrete settings.
references: Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, Ch.1–5

concepts.name: Asymptotic Notation
concepts.description: f=O(g): f grows at most as fast as g. f=Ω(g): f grows at least as fast. f=Θ(g): f grows at the same rate. Used to classify algorithmic efficiency independent of constants and lower-order terms.
references: Cormen et al., Introduction to Algorithms, Ch.3; Rosen, Discrete Mathematics, §3.2

concepts.name: Algorithm Complexity
concepts.description: Worst/average/best case time and space complexity of algorithms. Common complexities: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), O(2ⁿ). Loop analysis, recursion tree method.
references: Cormen et al., Introduction to Algorithms, Ch.2–4

concepts.name: Complexity Classes
concepts.description: P: solvable in polynomial time. NP: verifiable in polynomial time. NP-complete: in NP and every NP problem reduces to it. P=NP? is the most famous open problem in computer science. Examples: SAT, 3-coloring, TSP.
references: Sipser, Introduction to the Theory of Computation, Ch.7–8

concepts.name: Catalan Numbers
concepts.description: Cₙ = C(2n,n)/(n+1). Count parenthesizations, triangulations of convex polygon, binary trees, Dyck paths. Recurrence: Cₙ=∑_{k=0}^{n-1}CₖCₙ₋₁₋ₖ. GF: C(x)=(1−√(1−4x))/(2x).
references: Stanley, Enumerative Combinatorics, Vol.2, Appendix; Koshy, Catalan Numbers with Applications

concepts.name: Stirling Numbers
aliases: Stirling numbers of the first kind
Stirling numbers of the second kind
concepts.description: S(n,k) [second kind]: number of partitions of [n] into k non-empty subsets. s(n,k) [first kind (unsigned)]: number of permutations of [n] with k cycles. Connections to Bell numbers and rising/falling factorials.

concepts.name: Algebraic Structure
concepts.description: A set together with one or more binary operations satisfying axioms. The same set with different operations yields different structures. Abstract algebra studies these structures independently of the nature of elements.
references: Dummit & Foote, Abstract Algebra, Ch.1; Herstein, Topics in Algebra

concepts.name: Binary Operation
concepts.description: A function *: S×S→S assigning a unique element to each ordered pair. Closure: the result always stays in S. Associativity, commutativity, identity, and inverses are additional properties an operation may have.

concepts.name: Group
concepts.description: A set G with binary operation · satisfying: closure, associativity, identity element e (ge=eg=g), and inverses (∀g, ∃g⁻¹: gg⁻¹=e). Abelian (commutative) group: additionally gh=hg. Examples: (ℤ,+), (GL_n(ℝ),·).
references: Dummit & Foote, Abstract Algebra, Ch.1; Herstein, Topics in Algebra, Ch.2

concepts.name: Group Operation Examples
concepts.description: Concrete groups: (ℤₙ,+) cyclic; (ℤₙ*,·) units mod n; symmetric group Sₙ (permutations); dihedral group D_n (symmetries of n-gon); GL_n (invertible matrices). Cayley table fully describes a finite group.

concepts.name: Subgroup
concepts.description: H≤G iff H is non-empty, closed under the operation, and closed under inverses (subgroup criterion). Equivalently: ∀a,b∈H, ab⁻¹∈H. Trivial subgroups: {e} and G itself.

concepts.name: Cyclic Group
concepts.description: G = ⟨g⟩ = {gⁿ : n∈ℤ}; generated by a single element g. Finite cyclic of order n: ℤₙ≅⟨g⟩. Every subgroup of a cyclic group is cyclic. The cyclic groups are the simplest non-trivial groups.

concepts.name: Order of Group and Elements
concepts.description: |G| = number of elements in G. ord(g) = smallest positive n with gⁿ=e. In finite group: ord(g) divides |G| (Lagrange). Order 1 only for identity; cyclic subgroup ⟨g⟩ has order ord(g).

concepts.name: Coset
concepts.description: Left coset of H in G: aH = {ah : h∈H}. Cosets partition G; all cosets have the same size |H|. aH=bH iff a⁻¹b∈H. Index [G:H] = |G|/|H| = number of distinct cosets.

concepts.name: Lagrange's Theorem
concepts.description: If G is finite and H≤G, then |H| divides |G|. Equivalently, [G:H]·|H|=|G|. Corollary: ord(g) divides |G|; groups of prime order are cyclic.

concepts.name: Normal Subgroup
concepts.description: N◁G iff gNg⁻¹=N for all g∈G, equivalently gN=Ng (left and right cosets coincide). Kernels of homomorphisms are always normal. Normal subgroups are exactly those for which the quotient group is well-defined.

concepts.name: Quotient Group
concepts.description: G/N = {gN : g∈G} with operation (aN)(bN)=abN. Well-defined iff N◁G. |G/N|=[G:N]=|G|/|N| (finite case). The quotient 'collapses' N to the identity.

concepts.name: Group Homomorphism
concepts.description: A map φ:G→H with φ(ab)=φ(a)φ(b). Preserves group structure. ker(φ) = {g∈G : φ(g)=eH} is a normal subgroup of G. im(φ) is a subgroup of H.

concepts.name: Group Isomorphism
concepts.description: A bijective homomorphism φ:G→H. Isomorphic groups are structurally identical. Automorphism: isomorphism G→G. Classify groups up to isomorphism: cyclic groups ℤₙ; abelian groups as products of cyclic groups.

concepts.name: First Isomorphism Theorem
concepts.description: If φ:G→H is a homomorphism, then G/ker(φ) ≅ im(φ). The quotient by the kernel is isomorphic to the image. Generalizes to rings and modules. Proved by showing the induced map Ḡ→im(φ) is an isomorphism.

concepts.name: Second and Third Isomorphism Theorems
concepts.description: Second (Diamond): If H≤G and N◁G, then HN/N ≅ H/(H∩N). Third: If N◁H◁G, then (G/N)/(H/N) ≅ G/H. Together with the first theorem, they describe the subgroup lattice structure under homomorphisms.

concepts.name: Group Action
concepts.description: A group action of G on set X: a map G×X→X satisfying identity and compatibility. Orbit of x: Gx={gx:g∈G}. Stabilizer of x: Gₓ={g:gx=x}. Orbit-Stabilizer theorem: |Gx|·|Gₓ|=|G|.

concepts.name: Burnside's Lemma
concepts.description: Number of distinct orbits = (1/|G|)∑_{g∈G}|X^g| where X^g is the set fixed by g. Counts distinct colorings/patterns under symmetry. Key tool in combinatorial enumeration (Polya counting).

concepts.name: Sylow Theorems
concepts.description: For |G|=pᵐ·k (p prime, p∤k): (1) Sylow p-subgroups of order pᵐ exist; (2) they are all conjugate; (3) their number nₚ satisfies nₚ|k and nₚ≡1(mod p). Used to classify groups of given order.

concepts.name: Symmetric Group
concepts.description: Sₙ = all bijections on {1,…,n}; |Sₙ|=n!. Every finite group embeds in Sₙ (Cayley's theorem). Cycle notation; cycle structure determines conjugacy class. Transpositions generate Sₙ.

concepts.name: Alternating Group
concepts.description: Aₙ = even permutations (product of even number of transpositions); |Aₙ|=n!/2. A normal subgroup of Sₙ. For n≥5, Aₙ is simple (no proper normal subgroups). Simplicity of A₅ implies quintic is not solvable by radicals.

concepts.name: Ring
concepts.description: A set R with two operations (+,·) where (R,+) is an abelian group, · is associative, and · distributes over +. Commutative ring: ab=ba. Examples: ℤ, ℤₙ, polynomial rings, matrices. Unity 1 if present makes a unital ring.

concepts.name: Ideal
concepts.description: A subset I⊆R such that (I,+)≤(R,+) and for all r∈R, i∈I: ri∈I and ir∈I. Quotient ring R/I is well-defined iff I is an ideal. Principal ideal ⟨a⟩={ra:r∈R} generated by one element.

concepts.name: Quotient Ring
concepts.description: R/I = {r+I : r∈R} with (r+I)+(s+I)=(r+s)+I and (r+I)(s+I)=rs+I. Analogue of quotient groups. First isomorphism theorem for rings: if φ:R→S is a ring homomorphism, R/ker(φ)≅im(φ).

concepts.name: Prime and Maximal Ideals
