concepts.description: Prime ideal P: ab∈P ⟹ a∈P or b∈P. R/P is an integral domain. Maximal ideal M: no ideal strictly between M and R. R/M is a field. In commutative rings: every maximal ideal is prime.

concepts.name: Ring Homomorphism
concepts.description: A map φ:R→S with φ(a+b)=φ(a)+φ(b) and φ(ab)=φ(a)φ(b). Preserves ring structure. ker(φ) is an ideal; im(φ) is a subring. Ring isomorphism: bijective ring homomorphism.

concepts.name: Polynomial Ring
concepts.description: R[x]: polynomials with coefficients in ring R under standard addition and multiplication. F[x] for field F is a Euclidean domain with polynomial division algorithm. Irreducible polynomials generate maximal ideals.

concepts.name: Euclidean Domain
concepts.description: An integral domain R with a Euclidean norm N:R\{0}→ℕ₀ allowing division with remainder. Examples: ℤ (norm = absolute value), F[x] (norm = degree). In EDs, the Euclidean algorithm computes GCDs.

concepts.name: Principal Ideal Domain
concepts.description: An integral domain where every ideal is principal (generated by one element). Every ED is a PID but not conversely. In a PID, every prime ideal is maximal and irreducibles equal primes. Structure theorem for modules over PIDs generalizes Jordan form.

concepts.name: Unique Factorization Domain
concepts.description: An integral domain where every non-zero, non-unit element factors uniquely (up to order and units) into irreducibles. Every PID is a UFD. ℤ[x] is a UFD (Gauss's lemma) but not a PID.

concepts.name: Field
concepts.description: A commutative ring where every non-zero element has a multiplicative inverse. Examples: ℚ, ℝ, ℂ, ℤₚ (p prime). The characteristic of a field is 0 or a prime. Fields are the coefficient domains of vector spaces.

concepts.name: Field Extension
concepts.description: K is a field extension of F (K/F) if K is a field containing F as subfield. Degree [K:F] = dim_F(K). Tower law: [K:F]=[K:E][E:F]. Extensions are simple (K=F(α)), algebraic, or transcendental.

concepts.name: Algebraic Extension
concepts.description: α is algebraic over F if it satisfies some polynomial with F-coefficients. Minimal polynomial mα(x): the monic irreducible polynomial of least degree satisfied by α. F(α)≅F[x]/(mα(x)); [F(α):F]=deg(mα).

concepts.name: Finite Field
concepts.description: Finite fields exist iff |F|=pⁿ (p prime, n≥1), denoted 𝔽_{pⁿ} or GF(pⁿ). Unique up to isomorphism. Multiplicative group is cyclic. Used in coding theory, cryptography, and combinatorics.

concepts.name: Galois Theory
concepts.description: Studies field extensions via their automorphism groups. Fundamental theorem: a bijective correspondence between subfields of a Galois extension K/F and subgroups of Gal(K/F). Resolves classical impossibility problems.
references: Dummit & Foote, Abstract Algebra, Ch.14; Stewart, Galois Theory

concepts.name: Galois Group
concepts.description: Gal(K/F) = {σ:K→K automorphism fixing F pointwise}. Group under composition. |Gal(K/F)| = [K:F] for Galois extensions. For 𝔽_{pⁿ}/𝔽_p: Gal ≅ ℤₙ (Frobenius generates it).

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Galois Theory
concepts.description: For a Galois extension K/F: there is a bijection between intermediate fields E (F⊆E⊆K) and subgroups H of Gal(K/F). Correspondence: E↔Gal(K/E); normal subgroups correspond to Galois subextensions.

concepts.name: Group Inverse
concepts.description: For every element g in a group G, there exists a unique g⁻¹ such that gg⁻¹=g⁻¹g=e. Uniqueness proved from the axioms: if ga=e and gb=e then a=b.

concepts.name: Completeness of ℝ
concepts.description: Every non-empty subset of ℝ bounded above has a supremum (least upper bound). Equivalently: every Cauchy sequence in ℝ converges. This is what distinguishes ℝ from ℚ and is the basis of all convergence arguments.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.1; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.1

concepts.name: Supremum and Infimum
concepts.description: sup(S) = least upper bound of S; inf(S) = greatest lower bound. For bounded sets: sup/inf always exist in ℝ (completeness). sup is attained iff it is a maximum; similarly for inf.

concepts.name: Archimedean Property
concepts.description: For any real x, there exists a natural number n with n>x. Equivalently: the naturals are unbounded in ℝ. Corollary: ℚ is dense in ℝ (between any two reals there is a rational).

concepts.name: Convergence of Sequences
concepts.description: Rigorous definition: aₙ→L iff ∀ε>0, ∃N: n>N ⟹ |aₙ−L|<ε. Sequence properties: convergent ⟹ bounded; limit laws; Bolzano-Weierstrass (every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.3; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.name: Cauchy Sequence
concepts.description: A sequence where terms become arbitrarily close: ∀ε>0, ∃N: m,n>N ⟹ |aₘ−aₙ|<ε. In ℝ: Cauchy ⟺ convergent (completeness). Foundation for defining completeness in metric spaces.

concepts.name: Series (Rigorous)
concepts.description: Rigorous treatment: ∑aₙ converges iff partial sums form a Cauchy sequence. Absolute convergence (∑|aₙ|<∞) implies convergence and rearrangement invariance. Conditional convergence is rearrangement-sensitive (Riemann rearrangement theorem).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.3; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.name: Absolute Convergence
concepts.description: ∑aₙ absolutely converges iff ∑|aₙ| converges. Implies convergence of ∑aₙ. Absolutely convergent series may be rearranged to any order without changing the sum. Conditionally convergent series can be rearranged to any value.

concepts.name: Metric Space
concepts.description: A set X with a metric d:X×X→ℝ≥0 satisfying: d(x,y)=0⟺x=y, symmetry, triangle inequality. Examples: ℝⁿ (Euclidean), C([a,b]) (sup metric), discrete metric. Generalizes real analysis to abstract spaces.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.2; Munkres, Topology, Ch.2

concepts.name: Open and Closed Sets
concepts.description: Open ball: B(x,r)={y:d(x,y)<r}. Open set: every point is interior (surrounded by an open ball inside the set). Closed set: complement is open, equivalently contains all its limit points. Closure: smallest closed set containing S.

concepts.name: Completeness of Metric Spaces
concepts.description: A metric space is complete iff every Cauchy sequence converges. ℝⁿ is complete; ℚ is not. Completion: every metric space embeds densely into a complete space. The Baire category theorem holds in complete metric spaces.

concepts.name: Compactness
concepts.description: A set K is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover. Heine-Borel (in ℝⁿ): compact ⟺ closed and bounded. Sequential compactness: every sequence has a convergent subsequence (equivalent in metric spaces).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §2.31; Munkres, Topology, Ch.3

concepts.name: Connectedness
concepts.description: A set E is connected if it cannot be written as the union of two non-empty separated sets. Intervals in ℝ are the only connected subsets. Preserved under continuous maps. Intermediate Value Theorem: consequence of connectedness.

concepts.name: Continuity (ε-δ)
concepts.description: f is continuous at a iff ∀ε>0, ∃δ>0: |x−a|<δ ⟹ |f(x)−f(a)|<ε. Equivalently: sequences xₙ→a ⟹ f(xₙ)→f(a). In metric spaces: preimages of open sets are open.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.4; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.4

concepts.name: Uniform Continuity
concepts.description: f is uniformly continuous on E iff ∀ε>0, ∃δ>0: |x−y|<δ ⟹ |f(x)−f(y)|<ε (δ depends only on ε, not on x). Every continuous function on a compact set is uniformly continuous (Heine-Cantor theorem).

concepts.name: Lipschitz Continuity
concepts.description: |f(x)−f(y)| ≤ L|x−y| for some constant L (Lipschitz constant). Lipschitz ⟹ uniformly continuous ⟹ continuous. Important in ODE existence-uniqueness (Picard-Lindelöf) and optimization.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §4; Royden, Real Analysis, Ch.5

concepts.name: Extreme Value Theorem
concepts.description: A continuous function on a compact (closed, bounded in ℝⁿ) set attains its maximum and minimum. Proof: image of compact set under continuous map is compact; compact sets in ℝ have a max and min.

concepts.name: Intermediate Value Theorem (Rigorous)
concepts.description: If f:[a,b]→ℝ is continuous and f(a)<c<f(b), then ∃x∈(a,b) with f(x)=c. Proof: connected image of connected set is connected; connected subsets of ℝ are intervals.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §4.23; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, §4.5

concepts.name: Differentiability (Rigorous)
concepts.description: f′(a) = lim_{h→0} [f(a+h)−f(a)]/h. Differentiable ⟹ continuous; converse fails (|x| at 0). In ℝⁿ: total derivative Df(a) is a linear map; differentiable implies all partials exist but not conversely.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.5

concepts.name: Mean Value Theorem (Rigorous)
concepts.description: If f:[a,b]→ℝ is continuous, differentiable on (a,b), then ∃c∈(a,b) with f′(c)=(f(b)−f(a))/(b−a). Proved via Rolle's theorem. Fundamental tool: f′=0 on interval ⟹ f constant.

concepts.name: Taylor's Theorem (Rigorous)
concepts.description: f(x) = ∑_{k=0}^n f^{(k)}(a)/k! (x−a)^k + R_n(x) where the Lagrange remainder R_n(x) = f^{(n+1)}(c)/(n+1)! (x−a)^{n+1} for some c between a and x. Gives quantitative error bounds for polynomial approximations.

concepts.name: Riemann Integral (Rigorous)
concepts.description: Partition P of [a,b]; upper/lower Darboux sums U(f,P) and L(f,P). f is Riemann integrable iff inf U(f,P) = sup L(f,P). Riemann integrable functions are bounded; continuous functions are integrable.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.6; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.7

concepts.name: Riemann Integrability
concepts.description: f is Riemann integrable on [a,b] iff f is bounded and its set of discontinuities has Lebesgue measure zero. Continuous functions and monotone functions are integrable. A function with infinitely many discontinuities can still be integrable.

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Rigorous)
concepts.description: Part 1: If f integrable and F(x)=∫_a^x f, then F is Lipschitz; if f continuous at x₀ then F′(x₀)=f(x₀). Part 2: If F is an antiderivative of f, then ∫_a^b f = F(b)−F(a). Proof uses the MVT and Riemann sum properties.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §6.20; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.7

concepts.name: Uniform Convergence
concepts.description: fₙ→f uniformly on E iff ∀ε>0, ∃N: n>N ⟹ sup_{x∈E}|fₙ(x)−f(x)|<ε. Uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous; integrates termwise; differentiates termwise if derivatives converge uniformly.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.7; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.6

concepts.name: Pointwise Convergence
concepts.description: fₙ→f pointwise on E iff ∀x∈E, ∀ε>0, ∃N(x,ε): n>N ⟹ |fₙ(x)−f(x)|<ε. Weaker than uniform convergence; the limit of continuous functions need not be continuous. Dini's theorem: monotone pointwise limit on compact set is uniform.

concepts.name: Weierstrass Approximation Theorem
concepts.description: Every continuous function on [a,b] can be uniformly approximated by polynomials. Proved by Bernstein polynomials. Stone-Weierstrass generalizes to compact Hausdorff spaces with algebras separating points.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §7.26; Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis, Ch.5

concepts.name: Banach Fixed-Point Theorem
concepts.description: A contraction mapping T on a complete metric space (|d(Tx,Ty)| ≤ k·d(x,y), k<1) has a unique fixed point, attained in the limit of iterated applications T^n(x₀). Used to prove ODE existence-uniqueness (Picard iteration).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §9.23 (implicit function); Coddington & Levinson, Ch.1

concepts.name: Baire Category Theorem
concepts.description: A complete metric space cannot be written as a countable union of nowhere dense sets. Equivalently: a countable intersection of dense open sets is dense. Key consequence: functions with everywhere-defined derivative cannot be continuous but nowhere differentiable (wait — no: Baire implies such functions exist and form a residual set).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §5.6; Royden, Real Analysis, Ch.7

concepts.name: Implicit Function Theorem
concepts.description: If F:ℝⁿ×ℝᵐ→ℝᵐ is C¹ and F(a,b)=0 with ∂F/∂y invertible at (a,b), then locally y is a C¹ function of x with y(a)=b. Used to solve equations implicitly near known solutions.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §9.28; Munkres, Analysis on Manifolds

concepts.name: Inverse Function Theorem
concepts.description: If f:ℝⁿ→ℝⁿ is C¹ and Df(a) is invertible, then f is locally invertible near a with C¹ inverse satisfying D(f⁻¹)(f(a)) = [Df(a)]⁻¹. Proved using the Banach fixed-point theorem.

concepts.name: Complex Numbers (Analysis)
concepts.description: ℂ = {x+iy : x,y∈ℝ}. |z| = √(x²+y²), arg(z). Polar form: z=re^{iθ}. Operations: addition, multiplication, conjugation z̄=x−iy, |z|²=zz̄. The Riemann sphere adds ∞ by one-point compactification.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, Ch.1; Conway, Functions of One Complex Variable, Ch.1

concepts.name: Complex-Valued Function
concepts.description: f(z) = u(x,y) + iv(x,y) where u = Re(f), v = Im(f) are real-valued functions of two real variables. Limits and continuity defined using |z−z₀|→0 analogously to the real case.

concepts.name: Cauchy-Riemann Equations
concepts.description: f=u+iv is complex differentiable at z iff the CR equations hold: ∂u/∂x=∂v/∂y and ∂u/∂y=−∂v/∂x. If u,v have continuous partials satisfying CR, then f is holomorphic. Critical necessary and sufficient conditions for complex differentiability.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §2.2; Conway, Functions of One Complex Variable, §II.2

concepts.name: Analytic (Holomorphic) Functions
concepts.description: f is holomorphic on open U if f′(z) exists at every z∈U. Analytic: can be represented by a power series locally. In ℂ: holomorphic ⟺ analytic (unlike in ℝ). Entire function: holomorphic on all of ℂ.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, Ch.2; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.1

concepts.name: Harmonic Functions (Complex Analysis)
concepts.description: u is harmonic iff Δu = u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0. The real and imaginary parts of a holomorphic function are harmonic. Every harmonic u has a harmonic conjugate v (locally) such that f=u+iv is holomorphic.

concepts.name: Power Series in ℂ
concepts.description: ∑aₙ(z−z₀)ⁿ converges absolutely in |z−z₀|<R (radius of convergence, R from Hadamard formula). Holomorphic functions are locally equal to their Taylor series. Ratio/root tests work in ℂ as in ℝ.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §5.1; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.name: Complex Line Integral
concepts.description: ∫_C f(z)dz = ∫_a^b f(γ(t))γ′(t)dt for a piecewise C¹ curve γ:[a,b]→ℂ. Satisfies linearity and reversal of path. Estimation lemma: |∫_C f dz| ≤ max|f|·length(C).
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, Ch.4; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.1

concepts.name: Cauchy's Theorem
concepts.description: If f is holomorphic on a simply connected domain D, then ∮_C f(z)dz = 0 for every closed curve C in D. Equivalently, the integral is path-independent. Proved via Green's theorem (with Goursat's refinement removing differentiability assumption on f′).
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §4.2; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.name: Cauchy-Goursat Theorem
concepts.description: Goursat's key insight: Cauchy's theorem holds without assuming f′ is continuous (only that f′ exists). The proof triangulates the region and uses a delicate estimation argument. This makes the theorem apply to all holomorphic functions.

concepts.name: Cauchy Integral Formula
concepts.description: If f is holomorphic on and inside a simple closed curve C, then f(z₀) = (1/2πi)∮_C f(z)/(z−z₀)dz for z₀ inside C. Recovers function values from boundary values. Foundation of all deeper complex analysis.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §4.2; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.name: Derivatives of Holomorphic Functions
concepts.description: Every holomorphic function has derivatives of all orders: f^{(n)}(z₀) = (n!/2πi) ∮_C f(z)/(z−z₀)^{n+1} dz. Cauchy's inequality: |f^{(n)}(z₀)| ≤ n!M/Rⁿ for |f|≤M on |z−z₀|=R.

concepts.name: Morera's Theorem
concepts.description: Converse of Cauchy's theorem: if f is continuous on a domain D and ∮_T f dz=0 for every triangle T⊂D, then f is holomorphic on D. Used to prove holomorphicity without computing derivatives directly.

concepts.name: Liouville's Theorem
concepts.description: Every bounded entire function is constant. Proof: Cauchy's inequality gives f′(z)=0 everywhere for bounded entire f. Sharp contrast with real analysis where bounded non-constant entire functions exist (sin, cos — but not entire on ℝ without analogy).
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §4.3; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra (Complex Analysis)
concepts.description: Every non-constant polynomial with complex coefficients has at least one complex root. Proof via Liouville: if p(z)≠0 everywhere, 1/p is bounded entire, hence constant. Iterating: p has exactly n roots (counted with multiplicity).

concepts.name: Identity Theorem
concepts.description: If f,g are holomorphic on a connected domain D and f=g on a set with a limit point in D, then f≡g on D. Consequence: a holomorphic function is completely determined by its values on any set with a limit point.

concepts.name: Analytic Continuation
concepts.description: Extending a holomorphic function beyond its original domain of definition by matching power series on overlapping regions. The continuation is unique (identity theorem). Used to define log z, z^α, and the Riemann zeta function on all of ℂ.

concepts.name: Singularities
concepts.description: Isolated singularity z₀: f holomorphic in 0<|z−z₀|<r. Types: removable (f bounded near z₀), pole (|f|→∞), essential (neither). Riemann's theorem: removable singularity iff bounded near z₀.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §5.1; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.3

concepts.name: Poles and Meromorphic Functions
concepts.description: f has a pole of order n at z₀ iff (z−z₀)ⁿf(z) is holomorphic and non-zero at z₀. Meromorphic function: holomorphic except at isolated poles. Meromorphic on ℂ with singularity at ∞ is a rational function.

concepts.name: Essential Singularity
concepts.description: At an essential singularity, the function takes values arbitrarily close to any complex number (Casorati-Weierstrass). Great Picard: near an essential singularity, f takes all complex values with at most one exception infinitely often. Example: e^{1/z} at z=0.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §5.3; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.3

concepts.name: Laurent Series
concepts.description: In the annulus r<|z−z₀|<R: f(z) = ∑_{n=−∞}^∞ aₙ(z−z₀)ⁿ. Principal part: terms with n<0. aₙ = (1/2πi)∮_C f(z)/(z−z₀)^{n+1}dz. Classifies singularities: finite/zero/infinite principal part ↔ removable/pole/essential.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §5.1; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.3

concepts.name: Residue
concepts.description: Res(f,z₀) = a₋₁ in the Laurent expansion. For a simple pole: Res(f,z₀) = lim_{z→z₀}(z−z₀)f(z). For order-n pole: Res(f,z₀) = (1/(n−1)!) lim_{z→z₀} d^{n-1}/dz^{n-1}[(z−z₀)ⁿf(z)].
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §5.2; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.3

concepts.name: Residue Theorem
concepts.description: ∮_C f(z)dz = 2πi ∑ Res(f,zₖ) (sum over poles inside C). Generalizes Cauchy's theorem (no poles) and integral formula (simple pole at z₀). The most powerful computational tool in complex analysis.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §5.2; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.3

concepts.name: Evaluating Real Integrals via Residues
concepts.description: Techniques for computing real integrals ∫_{-∞}^∞ f(x)dx by extending to a closed contour in ℂ and applying the residue theorem. Jordan's lemma handles exponential factors. Enables evaluation of Gaussian, sinc, and other integrals.
references: Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.3; Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §5.3

concepts.name: Maximum Modulus Principle
concepts.description: If f is holomorphic on a bounded domain D and continuous on D̄, then |f| attains its maximum on the boundary ∂D. Non-constant holomorphic function has no interior maximum. Equivalent to: |f| is subharmonic.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §6.1; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.3

concepts.name: Conformal Mapping
concepts.description: A holomorphic bijection is conformal (angle-preserving) wherever f′≠0. The Jacobian of a conformal map is a rotation-dilation matrix. Used to map complicated domains to simpler ones (disc, upper half-plane) for PDE applications.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, Ch.6; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.8

concepts.name: Möbius Transformation
concepts.description: f(z) = (az+b)/(cz+d) with ad−bc≠0. Maps circles and lines to circles and lines. Three parameters determine a Möbius transformation (three-point normalization). Forms the group PGL(2,ℂ). Unique map sending three prescribed points to three others.

concepts.name: Riemann Mapping Theorem
concepts.description: Every simply connected proper open subset of ℂ is biholomorphic to the unit disc. The map is unique up to a Möbius transformation of the disc. Proved using normal families and Montel's theorem.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §6.1; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.8

concepts.name: Argument Principle
concepts.description: (1/2πi)∮_C f′(z)/f(z)dz = Z−P, where Z and P are the numbers of zeros and poles inside C (counted with multiplicity). The integral counts the winding number of f around 0.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, §5.2; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.3

concepts.name: Rouché's Theorem
concepts.description: If |f(z)−g(z)|<|g(z)| on a simple closed contour C, then f and g have the same number of zeros inside C. Used to count zeros of polynomials and prove FTA. Easy to apply when the dominant term is clear.

concepts.name: Riemann Surface
concepts.description: A one-dimensional complex manifold; the natural domain for multivalued functions like log z and √z. Riemann surface of log z is an infinite-sheeted helical cover of ℂ\ {0}. Compact Riemann surfaces classified by genus.
references: Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, Ch.8; Miranda, Algebraic Curves and Riemann Surfaces

concepts.name: Riemann Zeta Function
concepts.description: ζ(s) = ∑n^{-s} for Re(s)>1; analytically continued to ℂ with a single simple pole at s=1. Functional equation: ξ(s)=ξ(1−s). Nontrivial zeros: all known have Re(s)=1/2 (Riemann Hypothesis). Encodes distribution of primes via Euler product.
references: Davenport, Multiplicative Number Theory; Stein & Shakarchi, Complex Analysis, Ch.6

concepts.name: Topological Space
concepts.description: A set X with a collection τ of subsets (open sets) satisfying: ∅,X∈τ; arbitrary unions of open sets are open; finite intersections of open sets are open. Generalizes the notion of 'neighborhood' beyond metric spaces.
references: Munkres, Topology, Ch.2; Armstrong, Basic Topology, Ch.1

concepts.name: Open and Closed Sets (Topology)
concepts.description: Open sets: elements of τ. Closed sets: complements of open sets. Interior int(A): largest open set in A. Closure cl(A): smallest closed set containing A. Boundary ∂A = cl(A)∩cl(Aᶜ).

concepts.name: Interior, Closure, and Boundary
concepts.description: int(A) = largest open subset of A. cl(A) = smallest closed set containing A. ∂A = cl(A)\int(A). Dense: cl(A)=X. Nowhere dense: int(cl(A))=∅. These operations characterize the topology.

concepts.name: Basis for a Topology
concepts.description: A collection ℬ of open sets such that every open set is a union of elements of ℬ. Example: open balls in a metric space form a basis. The order topology on ℝ uses open intervals as a basis. Second-countable: has a countable basis.

concepts.name: Continuity (Topology)
concepts.description: f:X→Y is continuous iff f⁻¹(V) is open in X for every open V in Y. Equivalent (for metric spaces) to the ε-δ definition. Composition of continuous maps is continuous. Continuity is a topological property.

concepts.name: Homeomorphism
concepts.description: A continuous bijection f:X→Y with continuous inverse. Homeomorphic spaces are topologically identical. Topological properties (compactness, connectedness, genus) are homeomorphism invariants. Classic examples: torus ≇ sphere; coffee cup ≅ donut.
references: Munkres, Topology, §2.4; Armstrong, Basic Topology, Ch.3

concepts.name: Compactness (Topology)
concepts.description: A space X is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover. Key properties: closed subsets of compact spaces are compact; continuous image of compact space is compact; compact Hausdorff spaces are normal. Extends Heine-Borel to abstract settings.

concepts.name: Tychonoff's Theorem
concepts.description: An arbitrary product of compact spaces is compact (in the product topology). Equivalent to the Axiom of Choice. Used in functional analysis (Banach-Alaoglu theorem). Most powerful compactness result.

concepts.name: Connectedness (Topology)
concepts.description: X is connected iff it cannot be written as a union of two disjoint non-empty open sets. Path-connected: any two points connected by a path. Path-connected ⟹ connected; converse fails. Components: maximal connected subsets.

concepts.name: Separation Axioms
concepts.description: Hierarchy: T₀ (topologically distinguishable), T₁ (points closed), T₂=Hausdorff (distinct points have disjoint neighborhoods), T₃=regular, T₄=normal (disjoint closed sets have disjoint open neighborhoods). Urysohn's lemma holds in normal spaces.

concepts.name: Quotient Space
concepts.description: Given f:X→Y, the quotient topology on Y is the finest topology making f continuous. Examples: circle S¹ = [0,1] with endpoints identified; torus = square with edges identified. Constructs new spaces from old by gluing.

concepts.name: Product Topology
concepts.description: Product topology on X×Y: subbasis of sets π₁⁻¹(U) and π₂⁻¹(V). Basis: U×V for open U⊆X, V⊆Y. Projections are continuous. Universal property: f:(Z,τ)→X×Y continuous iff each component is continuous.

concepts.name: Homotopy
concepts.description: Two continuous maps f,g:X→Y are homotopic if there exists a continuous H:X×[0,1]→Y with H(x,0)=f(x) and H(x,1)=g(x). Homotopy is an equivalence relation. Fundamental in algebraic topology and classifying spaces.
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, §1.0; Munkres, Topology, Ch.9

concepts.name: Homotopy Equivalence
concepts.description: X and Y are homotopy equivalent if ∃f:X→Y, g:Y→X with g∘f≃id_X and f∘g≃id_Y. Weaker than homeomorphism. S¹ and ℝ²\{0} are homotopy equivalent. Homotopy equivalences induce isomorphisms on homotopy and homology groups.

concepts.name: Fundamental Group
concepts.description: π₁(X,x₀) = homotopy classes of loops based at x₀, with concatenation. π₁(S¹)=ℤ; π₁(Sⁿ)=0 for n≥2; π₁(torus)=ℤ². The fundamental group classifies the 'holes' in 1D that loops can detect. Simply connected: π₁={e}.
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, Ch.1; Munkres, Topology, Ch.9

concepts.name: Seifert-van Kampen Theorem
concepts.description: If X=U∪V with U,V open and U∩V path-connected, then π₁(X) is the amalgamated free product π₁(U)*_{π₁(U∩V)}π₁(V). Allows computing π₁ of a space from the π₁ of pieces. Computes π₁ of surfaces and CW complexes.

concepts.name: Covering Space
concepts.description: p:Ẽ→X is a covering map if every x∈X has an open neighborhood U such that p⁻¹(U) is a disjoint union of homeomorphic copies. Universal cover: simply connected covering space. Galois correspondence: subgroups of π₁(X) ↔ covering spaces.
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, §1.3; Munkres, Topology, Ch.9

concepts.name: Simplicial Complex
concepts.description: A simplicial complex: a collection of simplices (points, edges, triangles, tetrahedra, …) closed under faces. Provides a combinatorial way to study topological spaces. Every compact manifold admits a triangulation.
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, §2.1; Munkres, Elements of Algebraic Topology

concepts.name: Homology
concepts.description: Assigns abelian groups Hₙ(X) (n-th homology groups) to topological spaces. H₀: connected components; H₁: loops not bounding discs; H₂: 2D holes. Functorial and homotopy invariant. Computable via simplicial/CW chain complexes.
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, Ch.2; Munkres, Elements of Algebraic Topology

concepts.name: Euler Characteristic
concepts.description: χ(X) = ∑(-1)ⁿ rank(Hₙ(X)) = V−E+F for polyhedra. Topological invariant: homeomorphic spaces have same χ. Sphere: χ=2; torus: χ=0; genus-g surface: χ=2−2g. Euler-Poincaré formula generalizes V−E+F=2.

concepts.name: Cohomology
concepts.description: Dual to homology: H^n(X) = Hom(Hₙ(X),ℤ) (universal coefficients). Has cup product structure making H*(X) a ring. De Rham cohomology: closed/exact differential forms. De Rham's theorem: de Rham ≅ singular (with ℝ coefficients).
references: Hatcher, Algebraic Topology, Ch.3; Bott & Tu, Differential Forms in Algebraic Topology

concepts.name: Topological Manifold
concepts.description: A Hausdorff, second-countable topological space where every point has a neighborhood homeomorphic to ℝⁿ. Examples: curves (1-manifolds), surfaces (2-manifolds), ℝⁿ, spheres Sⁿ. Classification of compact surfaces: connected sum of tori or projective planes.
references: Munkres, Topology, Ch.9; Lee, Introduction to Topological Manifolds

concepts.name: Smooth Manifold
concepts.description: A manifold with a compatible atlas of C∞ coordinate charts. Admits smooth functions, vector fields, differential forms, and tensors. Sard's theorem: set of critical values has measure zero. Foundation of differential topology and differential geometry.
references: Lee, Introduction to Smooth Manifolds; Guillemin & Pollack, Differential Topology

concepts.name: σ-Algebra
concepts.description: A collection ℱ of subsets of X closed under: complement, countable union, countable intersection. (X,ℱ) is a measurable space. Borel σ-algebra ℬ(ℝ): generated by open sets. σ-algebras capture the notion of 'measurable' sets.
references: Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis, Ch.1; Royden, Real Analysis, Ch.1

concepts.name: Measure
concepts.description: A function μ:ℱ→[0,∞] satisfying: μ(∅)=0 and countable additivity (μ(⋃Eₙ)=∑μ(Eₙ) for pairwise disjoint Eₙ). (X,ℱ,μ) is a measure space. A probability measure is a measure with μ(X)=1.

concepts.name: Lebesgue Measure
concepts.description: Constructed via outer measure m*(E)=inf{∑|Iₙ| : E⊆⋃Iₙ} and Carathéodory's criterion. Extends length to all measurable subsets of ℝ. Translation invariant; m([a,b])=b−a. Not all sets are measurable (Vitali set).
references: Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis, Ch.1; Royden, Real Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.name: Measure Zero
concepts.description: E has measure zero iff μ(E)=0. Countable sets have measure zero; uncountable sets can (Cantor set). A property holds almost everywhere (a.e.) if the set where it fails has measure zero. The Cantor set: closed, uncountable, measure zero, nowhere dense.

concepts.name: Measurable Function
concepts.description: f:(X,ℱ)→(Y,𝒢) is measurable iff f⁻¹(E)∈ℱ for all E∈𝒢. For Borel: preimage of every open set is measurable. Every continuous function on ℝ is Borel measurable. Measurability needed for Lebesgue integration.

concepts.name: Simple Function
concepts.description: φ = ∑aᵢ1_{Eᵢ} (finite linear combination of indicator functions). ∫φ dμ = ∑aᵢμ(Eᵢ). Every non-negative measurable function is the limit of an increasing sequence of simple functions (monotone approximation).

concepts.name: Lebesgue Integral
concepts.description: ∫f dμ = sup{∫φ dμ : φ simple, 0≤φ≤f} for non-negative f; extend by f=f⁺−f⁻. Generalizes Riemann integral: every Riemann integrable function is Lebesgue integrable with same value. Integrates more functions; supports limit theorems.
references: Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis, Ch.1; Royden, Real Analysis, Ch.4

concepts.name: Convergence Theorems
concepts.description: MCT: if 0≤fₙ↑f a.e., then ∫fₙ dμ → ∫f dμ. Fatou's Lemma: ∫liminf fₙ ≤ liminf ∫fₙ. DCT: if |fₙ|≤g with ∫g<∞ and fₙ→f a.e., then ∫fₙ→∫f. These allow interchange of limits and integrals under appropriate conditions.

concepts.name: Lᵖ Spaces
concepts.description: Lᵖ = {f measurable : ∫|f|ᵖ dμ <∞} with norm |f|_p=(∫|f|ᵖ)^{1/p}. For p=∞: ess-sup. Hölder's inequality: |∫fg|≤|f|_p|g|_q for 1/p+1/q=1. Minkowski: triangle inequality. Lᵖ is complete (Riesz-Fischer).
references: Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis, Ch.3; Royden, Real Analysis, Ch.7

concepts.name: L² Space
concepts.description: L²(μ) with inner product ⟨f,g⟩=∫fḡ dμ is a Hilbert space (complete inner product space). Orthonormal bases and Fourier series are central. Parseval's theorem: ∑|⟨f,eₙ⟩|² = |f|².
references: Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis, Ch.4; Stein & Shakarchi, Real Analysis, Ch.4

concepts.name: Product Measure and Fubini's Theorem
concepts.description: Product σ-algebra: ℱ₁⊗ℱ₂ generated by rectangles A×B. Product measure: μ₁⊗μ₂(A×B)=μ₁(A)μ₂(B). Fubini: if ∫∫|f|<∞ then iterated integrals equal the double integral. Tonelli: for non-negative f, order of integration may be switched freely.
references: Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis, Ch.8

concepts.name: Radon-Nikodym Theorem
concepts.description: If ν≪μ (ν absolutely continuous w.r.t. μ), then ∃ measurable f≥0 (Radon-Nikodym derivative dν/dμ) with ν(E)=∫_E f dμ. Generalizes density functions. Key tool in probability (conditional expectation) and functional analysis.

concepts.name: Abstract Measure Spaces
concepts.description: σ-finite: X = ⋃Xₙ with μ(Xₙ)<∞. Complete measure: if μ(A)=0 and B⊆A then B measurable. Carathéodory extension theorem: extend a premeasure from a ring to a σ-algebra. Lebesgue measure constructed this way from length of intervals.
references: Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis, Ch.1; Halmos, Measure Theory

concepts.name: Normed Space
concepts.description: A vector space X with norm |·|:X→[0,∞) satisfying: |x|=0⟺x=0, |αx|=|α||x|, |x+y|≤|x|+|y|. The norm induces a metric d(x,y)=|x−y|. Examples: (ℝⁿ,|·|_p), C([a,b]) with sup-norm.
references: Rudin, Functional Analysis, Ch.1; Conway, A Course in Functional Analysis

concepts.name: Completeness
concepts.description: A normed space is complete iff every Cauchy sequence converges. Not all normed spaces are complete (e.g., C([0,1]) with L¹ norm is not; its completion is L¹). Completeness is essential for fixed-point theorems and spectral theory.

concepts.name: Banach Space
concepts.description: A complete normed vector space. Examples: ℝⁿ (any norm), Lᵖ(μ) for 1≤p≤∞, C(K) with sup norm, ℓᵖ sequence spaces. The Riesz-Fischer theorem: Lᵖ is complete. Most functional analysis theorems require completeness.
references: Rudin, Functional Analysis, Ch.1; Conway, A Course in Functional Analysis, Ch.1

concepts.name: Hilbert Space
concepts.description: A complete inner product space. Projection theorem: every closed convex subset has a unique nearest point. Riesz representation: every continuous linear functional is ⟨·,y⟩ for some y. Orthonormal bases exist (Gram-Schmidt / Zorn).
references: Rudin, Functional Analysis, Ch.4; Conway, A Course in Functional Analysis, Ch.1

concepts.name: Bounded Linear Operator
concepts.description: T:X→Y linear is bounded iff |T| = sup_{|x|≤1}|Tx|<∞, equivalently continuous everywhere. B(X,Y) = space of bounded operators; Banach space under operator norm. T bounded iff maps bounded sets to bounded sets.

concepts.name: Dual Space
concepts.description: X* = B(X,𝕂) = bounded linear functionals on X; a Banach space under |f|=sup_{|x|≤1}|f(x)|. Canonical examples: (Lᵖ)* ≅ Lq (1/p+1/q=1), (C(K))* = regular Borel measures (Riesz). Reflexive spaces X ≅ X**.

concepts.name: Hahn-Banach Theorem
concepts.description: A bounded linear functional on a subspace can be extended to the whole space with the same norm. Geometric form: disjoint convex sets can be separated by a hyperplane. Implies: X* separates points; dual determines the space.
references: Rudin, Functional Analysis, §3.2; Conway, A Course in Functional Analysis, Ch.IV

concepts.name: Open Mapping Theorem
concepts.description: A surjective bounded linear operator between Banach spaces is open (images of open sets are open). Consequence: a bijective bounded linear operator has a bounded inverse. Proved using the Baire category theorem.
references: Rudin, Functional Analysis, §2.11; Conway, A Course in Functional Analysis, §III.5

concepts.name: Closed Graph Theorem
concepts.description: A linear map T:X→Y between Banach spaces with closed graph (xₙ→x, Txₙ→y ⟹ y=Tx) is bounded. Makes it easier to verify boundedness by checking the graph condition rather than the norm estimate.

concepts.name: Uniform Boundedness Principle
concepts.description: If {Tα} is a family of bounded linear operators from Banach X to normed Y with sup_α|Tαx|<∞ for each x, then sup_α|Tα|<∞ (uniformly bounded in operator norm). Proved via Baire category. Prevents pointwise-convergent sequences of operators from 'blowing up'.

concepts.name: Riesz Representation Theorem
concepts.description: Every bounded linear functional on a Hilbert space H is of the form f(x)=⟨x,y⟩ for a unique y∈H with |f|=|y|. This establishes a (conjugate-linear) isometric isomorphism H≅H*. Hilbert spaces are self-dual.

concepts.name: Spectral Theory
concepts.description: Spectrum σ(T) = {λ : T−λI not invertible}. Resolvent ρ(T) = ℂ\σ(T). For self-adjoint operators: σ(T)⊆ℝ. Spectral theorem: every normal operator on Hilbert space diagonalizes via a spectral measure. Generalizes matrix diagonalization to infinite dimensions.
references: Rudin, Functional Analysis, Ch.11–12; Conway, A Course in Functional Analysis, Ch.IX

concepts.name: Compact Operators
concepts.description: T is compact if it maps bounded sets to precompact sets. Compact operators have discrete spectrum (only 0 is possible accumulation point); non-zero eigenvalues have finite multiplicity. Fredholm alternative: Tx=λx has a theory analogous to finite-dimensional systems.
references: Rudin, Functional Analysis, Ch.4; Conway, A Course in Functional Analysis, Ch.VII

concepts.name: Fourier Transform (Functional Analysis)
concepts.description: The Fourier transform extends from L¹∩L² to a unitary operator on L²(ℝ) (Plancherel theorem): |f̂|_2 = |f|_2. Makes L²(ℝ) self-dual. Foundation of spectral theory for differential operators and signal processing.
references: Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis, Ch.9; Stein & Shakarchi, Real Analysis, Ch.5

concepts.name: Distributions
concepts.description: Continuous linear functionals on the space 𝒟 of test functions (C∞ with compact support). Dirac δ: δ(φ)=φ(0). Every locally integrable function defines a distribution. Differentiation always possible: ⟨T′,φ⟩=−⟨T,φ′⟩. Foundation of modern PDE theory.
references: Rudin, Functional Analysis, Ch.6; Hörmander, The Analysis of Linear Partial Differential Operators, Ch.1

concepts.name: Special Functions
concepts.description: Γ(z) = ∫₀^∞ t^{z-1}e^{-t}dt (meromorphic extension of factorial). B(x,y) = Γ(x)Γ(y)/Γ(x+y). Orthogonal polynomials (Hermite, Laguerre, Legendre, Chebyshev) arise as eigenfunctions of Sturm-Liouville problems and form ONBs in weighted L² spaces.
references: Whittaker & Watson, A Course of Modern Analysis; Lebedev, Special Functions and Their Applications

concepts.name: Dense Subspaces and Approximation
concepts.description: A subspace S⊆X is dense iff cl(S)=X: every point in X can be approximated arbitrarily closely by elements of S. Examples: polynomials dense in C([0,1]) (Weierstrass); C∞ dense in Lᵖ; trigonometric polynomials dense in L² (Fourier).
references: Rudin, Functional Analysis, §1; Royden, Real Analysis, Ch.7

concepts.name: Convolution
concepts.description: (f*g)(x) = ∫f(x−y)g(y)dy. Young's inequality: |f*g|_r ≤ |f|_p|g|_q (1/r=1/p+1/q−1). Fourier transform converts convolution to multiplication: ℱ(f*g)=ℱ(f)·ℱ(g). Used in smoothing, differential equations, and signal processing.

concepts.name: Floating-Point Arithmetic
concepts.description: Floating-point numbers: ±m × 2^e with mantissa m and exponent e. Machine epsilon ε: smallest number with 1+ε≠1. IEEE 754 standard: 32-bit (single), 64-bit (double). Catastrophic cancellation, overflow, underflow.
references: Higham, Accuracy and Stability of Numerical Algorithms, Ch.2; Trefethen & Bau, Numerical Linear Algebra, Lecture 13

concepts.name: Error Analysis
concepts.description: Absolute error: |x̂−x|. Relative error: |x̂−x|/|x|. Sources: round-off (finite precision), truncation (approximation of infinite process). Forward/backward error analysis; condition number κ(A) measures problem sensitivity.
references: Burden & Faires, Numerical Analysis, Ch.1; Higham, Accuracy and Stability, Ch.1

concepts.name: Root-Finding Methods
concepts.description: Bisection: O(log(1/ε)) iterations, guaranteed. Newton's method: quadratic convergence near a root (but requires f′). Secant: superlinear, no derivatives. Brent's method combines bisection + secant for robustness.
references: Burden & Faires, Numerical Analysis, Ch.2; Trefethen & Bau, Numerical Linear Algebra

concepts.name: Newton's Method
concepts.description: xₙ₊₁ = xₙ − f(xₙ)/f′(xₙ). Quadratic convergence near simple roots: error at step n+1 is O((error at step n)²). Fails or converges slowly near multiple roots or saddle points. Generalizes to systems: xₙ₊₁ = xₙ − [J(xₙ)]⁻¹F(xₙ).

concepts.name: Polynomial Interpolation
concepts.description: Given n+1 data points, the unique interpolating polynomial of degree ≤n. Lagrange form: L(x)=∑yᵢ∏_{j≠i}(x−xⱼ)/(xᵢ−xⱼ). Newton form: nested multiplication. Runge's phenomenon: high-degree polynomials oscillate at edges.

concepts.name: Spline Interpolation
concepts.description: Piecewise polynomial curves with continuity conditions across breakpoints. Cubic splines: C²; natural splines add boundary conditions. B-splines form a basis for spline spaces and are numerically stable. Avoid Runge's phenomenon.
references: Burden & Faires, Numerical Analysis, §3.4; de Boor, A Practical Guide to Splines

concepts.name: Numerical Differentiation
concepts.description: Forward: f′(x) ≈ (f(x+h)−f(x))/h, error O(h). Centered: (f(x+h)−f(x−h))/(2h), error O(h²). Richardson extrapolation improves accuracy. Trade-off: small h reduces truncation error but increases round-off.

concepts.name: Numerical Integration
concepts.description: Trapezoidal rule: O(h²) per panel. Simpson's rule: O(h⁴). Composite rules for [a,b]. Gaussian quadrature: n-point rule exact for degree ≤2n−1 polynomials; uses Legendre polynomial nodes and weights. Adaptive methods control error automatically.

concepts.name: LU Factorization (Numerical)
concepts.description: Partial pivoting ensures numerical stability: PA=LU. Operation count O(n³/3). Condition number κ(A)=|A||A⁻¹| governs error amplification: relative error ≤ κ(A) × relative residual.
references: Golub & Van Loan, Matrix Computations, Ch.3; Trefethen & Bau, Numerical Linear Algebra, Lecture 20

concepts.name: Cholesky Factorization (Numerical)
concepts.description: For symmetric positive definite A: A=LLᵀ, computed in O(n³/6) flops — half the cost of LU. Backward stable. Test for positive definiteness: Cholesky fails iff A is not positive definite.

concepts.name: Iterative Methods for Linear Systems
concepts.description: Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel: simple splitting methods; converge for strictly diagonally dominant A. Conjugate gradient: optimal for SPD systems, O(√κ) iterations. GMRES for general A. Preconditioning reduces condition number.
references: Golub & Van Loan, Matrix Computations, Ch.11; Saad, Iterative Methods for Sparse Linear Systems

concepts.name: QR Algorithm
concepts.description: Iteratively computes eigenvalues: A₀=A, A_{k+1}=RₖQₖ (QR decomposition of Aₖ). Converges to quasi-upper triangular form with eigenvalues on diagonal. With shifts, converges cubically for symmetric A. Foundation of LAPACK's dgeev.
references: Golub & Van Loan, Matrix Computations, Ch.8; Trefethen & Bau, Numerical Linear Algebra, Lecture 28

concepts.name: SVD (Numerical)
concepts.description: Computed via bidiagonalization (Golub-Reinsch): first reduce A to bidiagonal B by Householder reflectors, then apply QR-like iteration. Backward stable; gives singular values to machine precision. Cost O(mn²) for m≥n.
references: Golub & Van Loan, Matrix Computations, Ch.8.6

concepts.name: Euler's Method (Numerical ODE)
concepts.description: yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + hf(tₙ,yₙ). Global error O(h); local truncation error O(h²). Simple but often too inaccurate; stiff equations require implicit Euler (yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + hf(tₙ₊₁,yₙ₊₁)) for stability.

concepts.name: Runge-Kutta Methods
concepts.description: RK4: classic fourth-order method; evaluates f at four points per step; global error O(h⁴). Embedded pairs (Dormand-Prince, Fehlberg) estimate error and control step size adaptively. Butcher tableau encodes all RK methods.
references: Burden & Faires, Numerical Analysis, §5.2; Hairer, Nørsett & Wanner, Solving ODEs I

concepts.name: Stiff ODEs and Implicit Methods
concepts.description: Stiff ODEs have widely separated eigenvalues; explicit methods need tiny steps. Implicit Euler (backward Euler), trapezoidal rule, and BDF methods are A-stable or stiffly stable. Require solving nonlinear equations at each step.
references: Hairer & Wanner, Solving ODEs II: Stiff and Differential-Algebraic Problems

concepts.name: Unconstrained Optimization
concepts.description: Find min f(x) over x∈ℝⁿ. Necessary condition: ∇f(x*)=0. Sufficient: ∇²f(x*) positive definite (second-order condition). Local vs global optima. Functions with many local minima require global optimization.
references: Nocedal & Wright, Numerical Optimization, Ch.1; Boyd & Vandenberghe, Convex Optimization, Ch.9

concepts.name: Convex Function
concepts.description: f is convex iff f(λx+(1−λ)y) ≤ λf(x)+(1−λ)f(y). Equivalent: ∇²f ⪰ 0. Jensen's inequality: f(E[X]) ≤ E[f(X)]. Every local minimum of a convex function is global. Key property underlying convex optimization.

concepts.name: Convex Set
concepts.description: A set C is convex iff for any x,y∈C and λ∈[0,1]: λx+(1−λ)y∈C. Convex hull: smallest convex set containing S. Examples: halfspaces, polyhedra, balls, simplices. Intersection of convex sets is convex.

concepts.name: Convex Optimization
concepts.description: Minimize a convex function f over a convex set C: every local minimum is global. Standard form: minimize f₀(x) subject to fᵢ(x)≤0 (convex) and hᵢ(x)=0 (affine). Efficiently solvable in polynomial time (interior-point methods).

concepts.name: Linear Programming
concepts.description: Minimize cᵀx subject to Ax≤b, x≥0. Optimal at a vertex of the feasible polytope. Simplex algorithm: walks vertices; exponential worst case, polynomial in practice. Interior-point (Karmarkar): polynomial time. LP dual: maximize bᵀy, Aᵀy=c, y≥0.
references: Bertsimas & Tsitsiklis, Introduction to Linear Optimization

concepts.name: Quadratic Programming
concepts.description: Minimize ½xᵀQx+cᵀx subject to Ax≤b. Convex if Q⪰0. Active-set and interior-point methods solve QPs. SVM training is a QP. Generalizes LP; basis of sequential quadratic programming (SQP) for nonlinear programs.

concepts.name: Semidefinite Programming
concepts.description: Minimize C•X subject to Aᵢ•X=bᵢ, X⪰0 (positive semidefinite). Generalizes LP and QP. Interior-point methods solve SDPs in polynomial time. Used in control theory, combinatorial optimization relaxations, and matrix completion.
references: Vandenberghe & Boyd, Semidefinite Programming, SIAM Review 1996

concepts.name: Duality Theory
concepts.description: Lagrange dual: g(λ,ν) = inf_x L(x,λ,ν) where L is the Lagrangian. Weak duality: d*≤p* always. Strong duality (d*=p*): holds for convex problems under Slater's constraint qualification (strictly feasible point). Dual certifies optimality.

concepts.name: KKT Conditions
concepts.description: Necessary conditions for optimality in constrained optimization: stationarity (∇L=0), primal feasibility, dual feasibility (λᵢ≥0), complementary slackness (λᵢfᵢ(x*)=0). Sufficient for convex programs. Generalize Lagrange multipliers to inequality constraints.
references: Boyd & Vandenberghe, Convex Optimization, Ch.5; Nocedal & Wright, Numerical Optimization, Ch.12

concepts.name: Lagrange Multipliers
concepts.description: For minimize f(x) subject to g(x)=0: at a local optimum, ∇f = λ∇g (∇f parallel to ∇g). The system ∇f=λ∇g, g(x)=0 has n+1 equations for n+1 unknowns (x,λ). Generalizes to multiple constraints.

concepts.name: Gradient Descent
concepts.description: xₖ₊₁ = xₖ − αₖ∇f(xₖ). Converges for convex f with Lipschitz gradient: rate O(1/k). Strongly convex: linear (geometric) convergence. Step size by line search or fixed rule. Most widely used optimization algorithm in machine learning.
references: Nocedal & Wright, Numerical Optimization, Ch.3; Bubeck, Convex Optimization: Algorithms and Complexity

concepts.name: Stochastic Gradient Descent
concepts.description: xₖ₊₁ = xₖ − αₖ ∇fᵢₖ(xₖ) using a random sample index iₖ. Computationally cheap per iteration for large datasets. Converges with diminishing step sizes. Mini-batch SGD: average gradient over a random batch. Foundation of neural network training.
references: Bottou, Curtis & Nocedal, Optimization Methods for Large-Scale Machine Learning, SIAM Review 2018

concepts.name: Newton's Method for Optimization
concepts.description: xₖ₊₁ = xₖ − [∇²f(xₖ)]⁻¹∇f(xₖ). Quadratic convergence near a strong minimizer. Requires Hessian (expensive); quasi-Newton (BFGS) approximates Hessian from gradients. L-BFGS: limited memory variant for large-scale problems.

concepts.name: Principal Component Analysis
concepts.description: Find the k principal directions (eigenvectors of covariance matrix ΣX) maximizing variance. Equivalent to truncated SVD of the centered data matrix. Reduces dimension while preserving maximum variance. Applications in statistics, ML, and image compression.
references: Jolliffe, Principal Component Analysis; Bishop, PRML, Ch.12

concepts.name: Integer Programming
concepts.description: LP with integer (or binary) constraints on variables. NP-hard in general. Solution methods: branch and bound, branch and cut, LP relaxation + cutting planes. Applications: scheduling, network design, combinatorial optimization.
references: Wolsey, Integer Programming; Bertsimas & Tsitsiklis, Introduction to Linear Optimization, Ch.11

concepts.name: Dynamic Programming
concepts.description: Optimization method exploiting optimal substructure: optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems. Bellman equation: V*(s) = max_a [r(s,a) + γV*(s′)]. Applications: shortest paths, knapsack, sequence alignment, reinforcement learning.
references: Bellman, Dynamic Programming; Cormen et al., Introduction to Algorithms, Ch.15

concepts.name: Graph
concepts.description: A graph G=(V,E) with vertex set V and edge set E⊆V×V. Basic parameters: n=|V| (order), m=|E| (size). Degree deg(v): edges incident to v. Handshaking: ∑deg(v)=2m. Extends to directed, weighted, and multigraphs.
references: Diestel, Graph Theory, Ch.1; Bondy & Murty, Graph Theory

concepts.name: Graph Invariants
concepts.description: Numerical properties preserved under isomorphism: order n, size m, degree sequence, diameter, girth (length of shortest cycle), chromatic number χ, clique number ω, independence number α, edge connectivity λ.

concepts.name: Graph Operations
concepts.description: Subgraph H⊆G: H=(V′,E′) with V′⊆V, E′⊆E. Induced subgraph G[S]: all edges between S⊆V. Complement: G̅ has same vertices, opposite edges. Union G∪H, intersection. Contraction: merge endpoints of an edge.

concepts.name: Connectivity
concepts.description: Vertex connectivity κ(G): min vertices to remove to disconnect. Edge connectivity λ(G): min edges. κ≤λ≤δ (min degree). Menger's theorem: κ = max internally disjoint paths. Whitney's theorem unifies these via max-flow min-cut.

concepts.name: Tree
concepts.description: A connected acyclic graph; n−1 edges. Equivalent characterizations: connected and acyclic; acyclic with n−1 edges; any two vertices have a unique path. Cayley's formula: the number of labeled trees on n vertices is n^{n-2}.
references: Diestel, Graph Theory, Ch.1; Bondy & Murty, Graph Theory, Ch.4

concepts.name: Minimum Spanning Tree
concepts.description: Spanning tree of minimum total weight. Kruskal: sort edges, add greedily avoiding cycles (uses Union-Find data structure), O(m log m). Prim: grow from a vertex, always adding the minimum edge leaving the current tree, O(m log n) with heaps.
references: Cormen et al., Introduction to Algorithms, Ch.23; Diestel, Graph Theory, Ch.1

concepts.name: Shortest Path Algorithms
concepts.description: Dijkstra (non-negative weights): greedy, O((V+E)log V). Bellman-Ford (any weights, no negative cycles): O(VE), detects negative cycles. Floyd-Warshall (all-pairs): O(V³). A*: heuristic-guided Dijkstra. SPFA: practical improvement of Bellman-Ford.
references: Cormen et al., Introduction to Algorithms, Ch.24–25

concepts.name: Maximum Flow
concepts.description: Find maximum flow from source s to sink t in a capacitated network. Ford-Fulkerson: repeatedly augment along s-t paths; O(Ef) or O(E|f*|). Edmonds-Karp: BFS augmentation, O(VE²). Max-flow = Min-cut (max-flow min-cut theorem). Basis of many combinatorial algorithms.
references: Cormen et al., Introduction to Algorithms, Ch.26; Diestel, Graph Theory, Ch.8

concepts.name: Matching
concepts.description: A matching M is a set of pairwise non-adjacent edges. Perfect matching: every vertex matched. Hall's theorem (bipartite): perfect matching exists iff |N(S)|≥|S| for all S. Augmenting paths algorithm; Edmonds' blossom algorithm for general graphs.
references: Diestel, Graph Theory, Ch.2; Bondy & Murty, Graph Theory, Ch.8

concepts.name: Eulerian Circuit
concepts.description: Euler's theorem: a connected graph has an Eulerian circuit iff every vertex has even degree; has an Eulerian path iff exactly two vertices have odd degree. Hierholzer's algorithm constructs the circuit in O(E). Applies to the Königsberg bridges problem.

concepts.name: Hamiltonian Cycle
concepts.description: A cycle visiting every vertex exactly once. NP-complete to determine existence (unlike Eulerian circuits). Dirac's theorem: exists if every vertex has degree ≥n/2. Traveling Salesman Problem: find minimum-weight Hamiltonian cycle (NP-hard). Approximation algorithms for metric TSP.
references: Diestel, Graph Theory, §10; Garey & Johnson, Computers and Intractability

concepts.name: Graph Coloring
concepts.description: Chromatic number χ(G). Brooks' theorem: χ(G)≤Δ (max degree) unless G is complete or an odd cycle. Four Color Theorem: χ≤4 for planar graphs (proved by computer in 1976). Chromatic polynomial: counts proper colorings with k colors.
references: Diestel, Graph Theory, Ch.5; Bondy & Murty, Graph Theory, Ch.14

concepts.name: Algebraic Graph Theory
concepts.description: Laplacian L=D−A (D=degree matrix). Eigenvalues of L: λ₁=0 (multiplicity = connected components). Cheeger inequality relates λ₂ to edge expansion. Graph Fourier transform. Eigenvalues of A determine walks, spectral clustering.
references: Cvetković, Doob & Sachs, Spectra of Graphs; Godsil & Royle, Algebraic Graph Theory

concepts.name: Ramsey Theory
concepts.description: Ramsey number R(s,t): smallest n such that any 2-coloring of Kₙ's edges contains a red Kₛ or blue Kₜ. R(3,3)=6. Bounds: 2^{s/2}≤R(s,s)≤C(2s−2,s−1). Party problem: R(3,3)=6 means in any 6 people, 3 know each other or 3 are mutual strangers.
references: Graham, Rothschild & Spencer, Ramsey Theory; Diestel, Graph Theory, Ch.9

concepts.name: Extremal Graph Theory
concepts.description: Turán's theorem: the maximum edges in an n-vertex Kᵣ₊₁-free graph is ex(n,Kᵣ₊₁) = (1−1/r)n²/2, achieved by the Turán graph T(n,r). Szemerédi Regularity Lemma: every dense graph can be decomposed into quasi-random bipartite parts.
references: Bollobás, Extremal Graph Theory; Diestel, Graph Theory, Ch.7

concepts.name: Random Graphs
concepts.description: G(n,p): each edge included independently with probability p. Threshold for property P: sharp threshold p* such that G(n,p) has P a.a.s. for p>>p* and lacks P a.a.s. for p<<p*. Connectivity threshold: p=log(n)/n.
references: Bollobás, Random Graphs; Janson, Łuczak & Ruciński, Random Graphs

concepts.name: Category
concepts.description: A category C consists of: a class of objects Ob(C), for each pair (A,B) a set Hom(A,B) of morphisms, composition ∘ (associative), and identity morphisms 1_A. Examples: Set (sets + functions), Grp (groups + homomorphisms), Top (spaces + continuous maps), Vect_k (vector spaces + linear maps).
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, Ch.1; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, Ch.1

concepts.name: Types of Morphisms
concepts.description: Monomorphism (mono): gf=hf ⟹ g=h (left cancellable; generalizes injective). Epimorphism (epi): fg=fh ⟹ g=h (right cancellable; generalizes surjective). Isomorphism: morphism with a two-sided inverse. Endomorphism: f:A→A. Automorphism: isomorphism A→A.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §I.5

concepts.name: Functor
concepts.description: A structure-preserving map between categories: F sends objects to objects and morphisms to morphisms, preserving composition and identities. Contravariant functor: reverses arrows. Examples: forgetful functors, free functors, homology, π₁.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §I.6; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, §1.3

concepts.name: Natural Transformation
concepts.description: A natural transformation η:F⟹G between functors F,G:C→D assigns to each object X a morphism η_X:F(X)→G(X) such that for all f:X→Y, η_Y∘F(f)=G(f)∘η_X (naturality). The 'right notion of morphism' between functors.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §I.4; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, §1.4

concepts.name: Functor Category
concepts.description: The functor category [C,D] (also written D^C) has functors F:C→D as objects and natural transformations as morphisms. Composition is pointwise. Allows treating functors as objects in a category — foundation of higher category theory.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §II.6

concepts.name: Yoneda Lemma
concepts.description: Nat(Hom(A,−), F) ≅ F(A) naturally in A and F. An object A is completely determined by the functor Hom(A,−) it represents. The Yoneda embedding C→[Cᵒᵖ,Set] is fully faithful — the deepest elementary result of category theory.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §III.2; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, Ch.2

concepts.name: Representable Functor
concepts.description: F:C→Set is representable iff F≅Hom(A,−) for some object A (the representing object). The universal element is the element u∈F(A) corresponding to id_A. Representability captures universal properties.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §III.2

concepts.name: Limits and Colimits
concepts.description: A limit of a diagram D:J→C is a universal cone over D. Colimit: universal cocone. Products and coproducts are limits/colimits of discrete diagrams. Equalizers, pullbacks, pushouts are special limits. Functors that preserve limits are continuous.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, Ch.V; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, Ch.3

concepts.name: Equalizer and Coequalizer
concepts.description: Equalizer of f,g:A→B: an object E with morphism e:E→A such that f∘e=g∘e, universal with this property. In Set: E = {a∈A : f(a)=g(a)}. Coequalizer: colimit; in Set: quotient by the equivalence relation generated by {(f(a),g(a))}.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §V.2

concepts.name: Pullback and Pushout
concepts.description: Pullback of f:A→C, g:B→C: the limit of the diagram A→C←B. In Set: {(a,b)∈A×B : f(a)=g(b)}. Pushout: colimit of A←C→B. Pullbacks compute fiber products; pushouts glue spaces. Essential in topology, algebraic geometry, type theory.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, §II.3

concepts.name: Adjunction
concepts.description: F⊣G (F left adjoint to G) iff Hom_D(FA,B)≅Hom_C(A,GB) naturally. Unit η:1_C⟹GF, counit ε:FG⟹1_D. Adjoints are ubiquitous: free/forgetful, product/exponential, direct/inverse image, colimit/constant. 'Adjoint functors arise everywhere' (Mac Lane).
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, Ch.IV; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, Ch.4

concepts.name: Monad
concepts.description: A monad on C: a functor T:C→C with unit η:1⟹T and multiplication μ:T²⟹T satisfying associativity and unit laws. Every adjunction F⊣G determines a monad GF. Algebras over a monad generalize many algebraic structures. Monads appear in functional programming (Haskell Maybe, IO, List).
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, Ch.VI; Riehl, Category Theory in Context, Ch.5

concepts.name: Tensor Product (Categorical)
concepts.description: A monoidal category (C,⊗,I) has a tensor product ⊗:C×C→C and unit I satisfying associativity and unit laws up to natural isomorphism. Examples: (Set,×,{*}), (Ab,⊗_ℤ,ℤ), (Vect,⊗,k). Symmetric monoidal: A⊗B≅B⊗A.
references: Mac Lane, Categories for the Working Mathematician, Ch.VII

concepts.name: Topos
concepts.description: An elementary topos is a category with finite limits, exponentials (function objects), and a subobject classifier Ω. Generalizes set-like mathematics; internal logic is intuitionistic. Grothendieck toposes: sheaves on a site. Bridges logic, geometry, and algebra.
references: Mac Lane & Moerdijk, Sheaves in Geometry and Logic; Goldblatt, Topoi: the Categorial Analysis of Logic

concepts.name: Higher Category Theory
concepts.description: 2-category: categories as objects, functors as morphisms, natural transformations as 2-morphisms. ∞-categories (quasicategories, Segal spaces): homotopy-coherent structures. Homotopy Type Theory (HoTT): types are spaces, proofs are paths. Active research frontier.
references: Lurie, Higher Topos Theory; Riehl, Verity, Elements of ∞-Category Theory; Univalent Foundations, HoTT Book
